Asha was not in the corridor.

I got up quietly. Bare feet on cold stone. I moved to the spiral staircase that led up to the bedroom level and listened. The staircase amplified sound, funneling it downward like a speaking tube. Through it, I could hear the storm—wind and rain and the occasional crack of a branch or a loosened piece of debris. But beneath the storm sounds, something else.

Footsteps.

Soft, careful footsteps on the heated glass floor of the bedroom corridor, one level above me.

Asha had gone upstairs. Into the part of the house that Tom had declared unsafe. Into the glass rooms with their transparent walls and their exposure to the compromised northeast window. Into the dark.

I should have woken someone. Tom, at least—Tom, who was solid and capable and trained for emergencies. Or Elliot, who was my ally, my co-conspirator, the person who knew my secret and had agreed to keep it.

I didn't. I went alone.

The staircase was cold—the heated floors didn't extend to the stairs—and I climbed quickly, my hand on the steel railing, my feet finding each step by memory and touch. The bedroom level was dark. The emergency lighting up here had failed—storm-related, probably, a circuit breaker tripped by the wind or the water. The only light was the faint, diffused glow of the storm itself, filtering through the rain-streaked glass walls in shifting patterns of gray and silver.

"Asha?" My voice was a whisper. It traveled down the corridor and came back to me, distorted by glass and distance, sounding like someone else's voice.

No answer.

I moved down the corridor. Past my room—the frosted glass door was open, the room beyond dark and empty. Past Asha's room—also open, also empty. Past Juliet's room, Marcus's room, Elliot's room. All open. All empty. The evacuation had left them like abandoned cells, the mattresses gone, the personal belongings scattered.

At the end of the corridor, the door to the main staircase—the one leading up to the living area on the top level. It was open.

I climbed.

The main living area was a different world at night. By day, it was all light and openness—the glass walls admitting the sky and the sea, the space feeling infinite. By night, in the storm, with the emergency lighting dead, it was a cavern. The glass walls were opaque with rain, streaming sheets of water that turned the exterior into an abstract wash of darkness. The fireplace was cold, its embers dead. The furniture was shadows.

And in the center of the room, standing on the glass floor that looked down through the bedroom level and into the foundation level below—standing on the transparent surface through which, if the lights were on, you could see all three levels of the house at once, like looking into the stacked chambers of a heart—

Was Asha.

She was barefoot. She was wearing the oversized T-shirt and leggings she'd slept in. Her dark hair was loose, falling past her shoulders, and her arms were at her sides, and she was standing perfectly still, looking down through the glass floor at something I couldn't see.

"Asha."

She didn't move. Didn't turn. Didn't acknowledge my voice.

I crossed the room carefully. The glass floor was cold under my feet—the heating system had failed with the emergency lighting, or had been turned off. Each step was a small act of faith: trusting the glass, trusting the steel frame, trusting that the transparent surface beneath me was solid enough to hold my weight despite every visual cue suggesting otherwise.

I reached Asha and looked down.

Through the glass floor, through the transparent ceiling of the bedroom level, I could see the corridor below. The frosted glass of the bedroom doors. The faint glow of the emergency lights on the foundation level, one story further down.

And on the floor of the bedroom corridor, directly beneath our feet, a shape.

A human shape.

Lying on the glass floor of the corridor. Motionless. One arm extended, the other tucked beneath the body. Blonde hair spread across the transparent surface like spilled light.

Juliet.

"Asha," I said again, and this time I touched her arm.

She flinched. Turned. Her eyes were wide, pupils blown, the dark irises almost entirely consumed by black. She looked at me as if she didn't recognize me—as if I were a stranger, or a ghost, or a fragment of a dream that had followed her into waking.

"She's not moving," Asha whispered.

I looked down again. Juliet was on her back. Her eyes were closed. Her cream-colored sweater was dark across the midsection—a spreading stain that, in the dim light, could have been water or wine or shadow.

Or blood.

"Stay here," I told Asha. "Don't move."

I ran.

Down the main staircase, through the living area, down to the bedroom corridor. The emergency lights on this level were working—dim, amber, barely adequate—and in their glow, I could see what I couldn't see from above.

Juliet was lying in the middle of the corridor. On her back. Her eyes were closed. Her right arm was extended, fingers curled around something small and dark—a phone. Not her phone—Iris had confiscated those. A different phone. A burner, maybe, or a satellite phone, something she'd brought secretly, something she'd hidden.

The stain on her sweater was blood.

Not a lot. Not the dramatic, spreading pool of violence that movies depict. A contained stain, roughly the size of a dinner plate, centered over her left side, just below her ribs. The blood was dark—almost black in the amber light—and it wasn't spreading anymore. It had stopped.

Which meant either the bleeding had been controlled.

Or her heart had stopped pumping.

I dropped to my knees beside her. Pressed two fingers against her neck. The skin was warm. The pulse was there—faint, thready, too fast. Alive.

"Juliet." I shook her shoulder gently. "Juliet, can you hear me?"

Her eyelids fluttered. Her lips moved. No sound.

I pulled up her sweater. The wound was small—a puncture, not a slash. Clean edges, minimal tearing. A blade, thin and sharp. A knife wound. Deliberately placed—just below the ribs, angled upward, targeting the spleen or the kidney. A wound designed to cause internal bleeding. A wound designed to kill slowly.

"Help!" I shouted. My voice echoed through the glass corridors, bouncing off every transparent surface, multiplying. "I need help up here! Now!"

Footsteps on the stairs. Tom first—always Tom first, the responder, the man who ran toward danger. Then Elliot. Then Marcus. Then Iris, barefoot, her silver hair wild, her dark eyes taking in the scene with a single, sweeping glance.

"She's been stabbed," I said. "Left side, below the ribs. She's losing blood."

Tom was beside me in an instant, his hands moving to Juliet's wound with the practiced efficiency of a man who had treated injuries in the field. "I need towels. Clean towels. And pressure. Something I can use for a compress."

Elliot was already moving. Marcus stood in the corridor, his face the color of ash, his hands hanging at his sides. Not frozen—calculating. Even now, even in the face of blood and crisis, Marcus Cole was calculating.

Iris knelt beside Tom. "How bad?"

"Can't tell without seeing the depth. If it's hit the spleen, she needs surgery. If it's just muscle and tissue, she needs stitches and antibiotics. Either way, she needs a hospital."

"The dock is destroyed. The radio—"

"Get the radio. Call the Coast Guard. Tell them we have a stabbing victim on Calder Island. Tell them we need a helicopter evacuation." Tom's voice was steel. The voice of command, of authority, of a man who had spent thirty years making life-and-death decisions in seconds. "Now, Iris."

Iris went.

I stayed with Juliet. Held her hand—the one that wasn't clutching the phone—and watched Tom apply pressure to the wound, his big hands steady, his jaw set, his blue eyes focused with an intensity that burned through everything else. In this moment, Tom Rafferty was not a man haunted by guilt or secrets or the ghosts of people he hadn't saved. He was a rescuer. Pure. Simple. Doing the one thing he knew how to do better than anything else.

Juliet's eyes opened.

They found mine. And in them, I saw something I didn't expect.

Not pain. Not fear.

Recognition.

She knew something. She had found something—upstairs, in the dark, in the part of the house she'd gone to while the rest of us slept. She had found something that someone didn't want her to find. And she had been stabbed for it.

Her lips moved. I leaned close. Her breath was warm and irregular against my ear.

One word. Barely a whisper. More shape than sound.

"Bram."

Then her eyes closed, and her hand went limp in mine, and the phone slipped from her fingers and clattered to the glass floor, its screen cracked, its secrets locked behind a password I didn't know.

TOM — NOW

The human body contains approximately five liters of blood.

Tom knew this the way he knew the flash point of diesel fuel or the load-bearing capacity of a two-by-six stud—not as abstract knowledge but as operational fact, the kind of information that meant the difference between saving a life and watching one end. Five liters. Ten and a half pints. Roughly eight percent of total body weight. Lose fifteen percent and you get dizzy. Lose thirty percent and you go into shock. Lose forty percent and you die.

Juliet Marsh was losing blood.

Not catastrophically—the wound was small, the blade thin, and whatever had punctured her had missed the major vessels. Tom could tell by the color: dark red, almost maroon, venous rather than arterial. Arterial blood was bright, urgent, pulsing with the heart's rhythm. Venous blood was slower, steadier, more patient. You could survive venous bleeding if someone applied pressure and kept applying it and didn't panic and didn't stop.

Tom did not panic. Tom did not stop.

He knelt on the glass floor of the bedroom corridor with his hands pressed against Juliet's left side, a folded towel beneath his palms serving as a compress. The blood had soaked through the first towel in ninety seconds. Elliot had brought a second. Tom replaced it without lifting the pressure—a technique he'd learned in his second year on the job, during a warehouse fire that had dropped a beam on a twenty-year-old apprentice electrician and opened his femoral artery like a zipper.

That man had lived. Tom had saved him.

Claire Davenport had not lived. Tom had not saved her.

He pushed the thought away. Filed it in the locked room in his mind where he kept the failures, the losses, the faces of the people he'd been too late for or too far from or too weak to reach. That room was crowded. It had been crowded for years. But the door still held, and as long as it held, he could function.

"Talk to me, Juliet," he said. His voice was steady—command voice, the tone that cut through chaos and panic and the animal noise of crisis. "Open your eyes. Stay with me."

Her eyelids fluttered. Her lips were pale—too pale, the color retreating, the body pulling blood inward to protect the vital organs. Compensatory shock. Stage one. He had minutes before stage two, when the blood pressure would drop and the heart rate would spike and the body's defenses would start to fail.

"Nora," Tom said without looking up. "The phone she was holding. Is there a signal?"

He was standing in the corridor with his back against the wall, his arms crossed, his face the rigid mask of a man holding himself together through sheer force of will. "She named him. No answer.

A longer silence. Footsteps on stone. A door opening, then closing. Then Iris's voice again, louder this time, with an edge Tom hadn't heard before—the edge of someone whose carefully constructed plan has just veered into territory she hadn't mapped.

"He's gone."

Tom looked at Nora. Nora looked at Marcus. Marcus looked at the dark corridor stretching behind them, the glass walls streaming with rain, the house full of shadows and exits and hiding places.

"The boat—"

Nora moved into position. Her hands replaced his on the compress—smaller hands, but steady, and she applied pressure with a firmness that suggested she'd done this before or had an instinct for it.

Tom opened the suture kit. Sterile needle, sterile thread, forceps, scissors. His hands remembered the motions—he'd sutured in the field twice, once on a fellow firefighter whose arm had been torn open by a collapsing roof joist, once on himself, a gash on his thigh from a piece of shrapnel during a gas explosion. Neither time had been elegant. Both times, the person had survived.

"This is going to hurt her," Tom said. "Even with the lidocaine. If she wakes up during the stitching, I need someone to hold her still."

"I'll do it," Marcus said. He stepped forward, uncrossing his arms, rolling up his sleeves. His movements were precise, controlled—the movements of a man channeling his fear into utility. Whatever else Marcus Cole was—whatever he'd done, whatever he was hiding—he was not a man who fell apart in a crisis. He was a man who reorganized himself around the crisis and kept moving.

Tom loaded the syringe with lidocaine. "Nora, ease the compress back. Slowly. I need to see the wound."

Nora peeled the towel away. The wound was visible now—a slit, roughly two centimeters long, just below the left costal margin. Clean edges. Minimal tissue damage around the entry point. A thin blade, very sharp, inserted with precision.

Not a frenzied attack. Not a crime of passion. This was surgical. Deliberate. The work of someone who knew where to put a knife to cause maximum internal damage with minimum external evidence.

Tom injected the lidocaine around the wound perimeter. Juliet didn't react—she was deep in unconsciousness now, her body's defenses pulling her down into the dark where pain couldn't reach.

He applied the hemostatic powder to the wound bed. Waited thirty seconds for it to activate. Then, with the steadiness that thirty years of emergency work had burned into his hands, he began to suture.

The needle pierced skin. The thread pulled through. One stitch. Two. Three.

Behind him, Elliot stood at the rain-streaked window, looking out into the storm. Looking for Bram.

"He could be anywhere on the island," Elliot said. "The tree cover would hide him. There are outbuildings—I saw a shed near the dock path, some kind of storage structure on the north side."

"We're not going after him," Tom said. "Not in this weather. Not in the dark."

"He stabbed Juliet."

"Maybe. Or maybe Juliet saw him and assumed. Or maybe someone else did it and Bram ran because he knew he'd be blamed." Tom tied off the fourth stitch. "Right now, my priority is keeping her alive. Everything else comes later."

"And if he comes back? If he comes back with the knife?"

Tom looked up from the wound. Met Elliot's eyes. "Then we deal with it."

"How?"

"The way you deal with any threat. You see it coming, and you don't back down."

He turned back to Juliet. Stitch five. Stitch six. The wound was closing, the edges meeting neatly, the bleeding stopped. The hemostatic powder was doing its job. The sutures were holding.

But the internal damage—the damage he couldn't see, couldn't assess, couldn't reach—was the variable. If the blade had penetrated the peritoneum, if there was a laceration to the spleen or the kidney, Juliet could be bleeding into her abdominal cavity right now. Slowly. Quietly. The compress and sutures holding the outside closed while the inside filled with blood.

He'd know in the next few hours. If her vitals held—pulse, blood pressure, consciousness—she'd make it to the helicopter. If they didn't—

Tom tied off the final stitch. Seven sutures, evenly spaced, each one a small bridge between the edges of the wound. He applied a clean gauze dressing, taped it down, and sat back on his heels.

His hands were covered in blood. Juliet's blood. He looked at them in the amber light—the deep red staining the lines of his palms, the creases of his knuckles—and thought about Claire Davenport's twin sister, whose blood he'd never touched because he'd never reached her, because he'd chosen his daughter instead.

You chose, Juliet had said. And Claire Davenport died.

Yes. He had chosen. And now, here, on this island, in this storm, he was choosing again. Choosing to save the person in front of him instead of chasing the person who'd hurt her. Choosing the immediate over the important. Choosing life over justice.

He hoped it would be enough this time.
