Asha wanted to go home. And Lena—Iris—whoever she was—was trying to maintain control of a situation that had long since escaped her control, her voice rising in pitch, her gestures becoming sharper, the composed therapist dissolving into the desperate, grieving mother she had always been beneath the mask.

I left them to it.

The bedroom corridor was dark—the emergency lighting still dead, the only illumination the faint ambient glow of the storm filtering through the rain-washed glass. I moved by memory and touch, my bare feet on the cold glass floor, my fingertips trailing along the frosted wall panels.

Juliet's room. Third door on the right.

The frosted glass door was open. Inside, the room was as she'd left it—mattress gone, taken to the foundation level, but everything else in place. Her suitcase, open on the desk, clothes folded with geometric precision. Her toiletry bag, zipped, in the bathroom. A pair of leather ankle boots by the wall, positioned side by side, heels aligned.

I searched quickly and thoroughly. I'd been searching rooms since I was fourteen—foster homes, group homes, the apartments of people who took me in and the apartments of people I took things from. I knew where people hid things. I knew the false bottoms of suitcases, the insides of shoes, the taped undersides of desk drawers, the pages of books with rectangles cut from the centers.

Juliet hadn't been that creative.

The document was in the inside pocket of her suitcase—a zippered compartment that was obvious enough to suggest she hadn't expected anyone to search her belongings, or that she'd hidden it in haste, without time for finesse.

It was a printout. Two pages, stapled at the corner, slightly crumpled. A bank statement—not Juliet's. The account holder's name was at the top of the first page, printed in the small, precise font of institutional finance.

ALDRIC, ANDREI L.

ACCOUNT #: 7734-9921-0084

INSTITUTION: FIRST MARITIME BANK OF MAINE

Andrei Aldric. Lena's dead husband. Maisie's father.

The statement covered a three-month period: August through October 2018. The months leading up to the ferry disaster. And in those three months, the account showed a pattern of transactions that made my stomach tighten.

August 14: Wire transfer received, $15,000. Originator: HARGROVE HOLDINGS LLC.

August 29: Wire transfer received, $15,000. Originator: HARGROVE HOLDINGS LLC.

September 12: Wire transfer received, $15,000. Originator: HARGROVE HOLDINGS LLC.

October 1: Wire transfer received, $15,000. Originator: HARGROVE HOLDINGS LLC.

$60,000 total. Four payments. From the parent company of Blackwater Marine Transit to the personal bank account of Lena Aldric's dead husband.

But Andrei had died in 2014. Four years before these transfers. Which meant someone else had been accessing his account. Using his name, his identity, his banking credentials—the way I used Sarah's.

Lena.

Lena had been receiving money from Hargrove Holdings. $60,000 over three months. Through her dead husband's account. In the months before the ferry she claimed had been sabotaged. The ferry that killed her daughter.

I stared at the bank statement in the dim light, and the pieces rearranged themselves in my mind—not gently, not gradually, but with the violent, disorienting speed of a kaleidoscope being shaken. The pattern I'd been seeing—Lena as the grieving mother, the relentless investigator, the woman driven by love and loss to uncover the truth—shattered and reformed into something different. Something worse.

What if Lena wasn't investigating the sabotage?

What if Lena was the saboteur?

The thought was obscene. A mother, killing her own daughter. But the money was real. The connection to Hargrove was real. And Lena's behavior—the elaborate deception, the isolation, the systematic dismantling of her guests' defenses—took on a different meaning if seen not as investigation but as cover. Not as a search for truth but as a search for the one person who could connect her to the crime.

But why bring us here? Why expose herself to the risk of discovery?

Unless she was looking for something specific. A piece of evidence. A witness. Someone who had seen something on the ferry that could link her to the sabotage—and she needed to find out what they knew before she could act.

Not therapy. Not justice.

Cleanup.

I folded the bank statement and put it in my pocket. Then I searched the rest of Juliet's belongings.

In the toe of her left boot, rolled into a tight cylinder and secured with a rubber band: a second document. This one was a photograph—a high-quality print, not a snapshot, the kind produced by a professional surveillance photographer. It showed two people standing on the Harwick dock, facing each other, in conversation. The timestamp in the bottom corner read: 10/14/2018. 4:12 PM.

The day of the ferry crossing. Two hours before departure.

One of the figures was Marcus Cole. I recognized the suit, the posture, the set of his shoulders.

The other was Lena Aldric.

Not "Dr. Iris Vane." Not the silver-haired therapist with the bare feet and the penetrating gaze. This was Lena as she had been eighteen months ago—younger, darker-haired, unlined by the grief that would carve itself into her face in the weeks and months after. She was dressed casually—jeans, a canvas jacket—and she was looking at Marcus with an expression I couldn't read from the photograph but that her body language made clear.

Familiarity. Intimacy. The posture of two people who knew each other well.

Marcus and Lena had met before the ferry. On the day of the ferry. At the dock.

Marcus, who had booked his ticket three hours before departure. Lena, who had boarded with her daughter. Both of them at the dock, together, two hours before the crossing that would kill thirty-one people.

My hands were shaking. I rolled the photograph back into its cylinder, replaced the rubber band, and returned it to the boot. Then I stood in Juliet's dark room and pressed my palms against my eyes and thought.

Juliet had found these documents. She had brought them with her—which meant she'd had them before she came to the island. She hadn't found them in the Glass House. She'd arrived with them. Which meant Juliet Marsh had come to this retreat carrying evidence that connected Marcus and Lena—evidence that she'd either obtained independently or been given by someone.

Someone who wanted the truth exposed.

Someone who had their own agenda.

And Juliet had been stabbed. Not killed—the wound was designed to incapacitate, not to murder. To remove her from the game. To silence her before she could share what she knew.

By whom?

Bram, who had vanished—Bram, whose loyalty was to Lena, who would have known that Juliet's evidence threatened his sister-in-law's entire operation?

Or Marcus, who was in that photograph, whose connection to Lena predated the ferry, whose involvement in the sabotage was deeper than he'd admitted?

Or someone else entirely—someone whose secret I hadn't uncovered yet, whose role in this I hadn't identified, whose piece of the puzzle I hadn't found?

I left Juliet's room and moved down the corridor toward the main staircase. The storm had intensified again—the wind was screaming now, a sustained howl that made the glass walls vibrate at a frequency I could feel in my teeth. The house was shaking. Not violently, not dangerously, but perceptibly—a tremor, a shiver, the kind of movement that told you the structure was being tested and had not yet decided whether to hold.

I was halfway down the corridor when I heard it.

A sound that didn't belong to the storm. Not wind. Not rain. Not the groan of glass under pressure or the hum of the generator below.

Footsteps.

Above me. On the upper level. The living room, the kitchen, the Gangplank.

Someone was up there.

Not Bram—Bram had left the house. Not any of the others—they were all in the foundation level, arguing, tending to Juliet, doing the things that frightened people do when the world closes in around them.

Someone else.

I stood in the dark corridor and listened. The footsteps were careful, measured, moving across the glass floor of the living area with the deliberate tread of someone who was searching for something. I heard a drawer open. Close. Another drawer. A cabinet door. The soft rustle of papers being moved.

Then silence.

Then the footsteps again—quicker now, purposeful, moving toward the kitchen. I heard the click of a gas burner igniting. The soft whoosh of flame.

Someone was burning something.

I moved. Up the main staircase, fast, no longer caring about silence. The living area was dark—the same storm-filtered grayness, the same rain-streaked walls—but in the kitchen, a small, bright flame was visible. A gas burner on the stove, turned to its lowest setting, and above it, a hand holding a piece of paper. The paper was curling, blackening, the flame eating it from the bottom up with steady, methodical hunger.

"Stop."

The figure at the stove turned.

It was Asha.

She stood at the kitchen counter with a burning document in her hand and an expression on her face that I had never seen before—not on her, not on anyone. It was beyond fear, beyond guilt, beyond the fragile vulnerability she'd been showing us since she arrived. It was resolve. The hard, bright, terrible resolve of someone who has made a decision and is carrying it through regardless of consequence.

"Asha, put it down."

"I can't."

"You're destroying evidence."

"I'm destroying a lie." Her voice was steady—steadier than I'd ever heard it. The trembling, uncertain Asha of the confessions and the drawings and the soft, wounded whispers about her twin sister—that Asha was gone. In her place was someone harder. Older. Someone who had been hiding behind the performance of fragility the way I hid behind the performance of Sarah Finch.

"What lie?"

The paper was half-consumed. The flame reached her fingers and she dropped it into the sink, where it finished burning with a soft, final sigh of light and heat and destruction.

"The lie that Priya was innocent," Asha said.

I stared at her.

"Priya wasn't being coerced," Asha continued. "I told you—told everyone—that someone was threatening her. Forcing Kian to install the device. That wasn't true."

"You confessed this afternoon. In front of everyone. You said—"

"I said what I needed to say to protect her. The way I've always protected her. Even now. Even dead. Even gone." Asha's hands were shaking—the resolve in her voice didn't extend to her body. "Priya wasn't a victim, Nora. Priya was the architect."

The word—architect—detonated in the dark kitchen. The same word I'd used in my mind about Marcus. The same word that described the person who had designed and executed the sabotage of the Blackwater Ferry.

"Priya planned the sabotage," I said. Not a question.

"Priya planned everything. The blackmail was real—she and Kian really did discover the maintenance failures, and they really did approach Hargrove for money. But the sabotage wasn't coercion. It was Priya's idea. Her plan. She wanted to destroy the ferry—cause a catastrophic failure that would expose the company, generate lawsuits, create a scandal so large that Hargrove Holdings would be forced into bankruptcy. And from the lawsuits—from the settlements, from the insurance claims, from the class action—she and Kian would collect. As victims. As survivors."

"They were going to sabotage the ferry and then sue the company for damages."

"Yes."

"But Priya died. And Kian died. Their own sabotage killed them."

Asha's face crumpled. The resolve held for one more second—one more beat of the hard, bright certainty—and then it collapsed, and what was underneath was not fragility but agony. The pure, searing agony of a woman who had watched her twin sister plan a mass murder and had done nothing to stop it.

"The device was supposed to cause an engine failure," Asha whispered. "That's all. A stall. A breakdown. Enough to strand the ferry and force an investigation. Priya tested it—she had Kian run simulations, calculations. She was certain it was safe. She was certain no one would be hurt."

"But someone modified it."

"Someone changed the specifications. The device Kian installed was not the device Priya designed. It was stronger. More destructive. Calibrated to cause a catastrophic rupture rather than a controlled failure."

"Who changed it?"

"I don't know. That's what the document was. The one I burned." She looked at the sink, at the ashes of the paper, at the gray residue of a truth that was now beyond recovery. "It was a purchase order. For the modified actuator. Priya kept it—she kept everything, she was obsessive about records—and I found it in her apartment after she died. The order was placed by someone using Daniel Finch's company. Meridian Consulting."

"You've had this document for eighteen months."

"Yes."

"And you brought it here."

"I brought it here to destroy it. Because if Iris—Lena—whoever she is—found it, she would know. She would know that Priya wasn't a victim. She would know that my sister planned the sabotage that killed her daughter. And I can't—"

Her voice broke completely. She pressed her hands against her mouth and made a sound that was not quite a sob and not quite a scream but something between—the sound of a woman whose love for her dead sister was inseparable from her horror at what her dead sister had done.

"Asha." I stepped toward her. "The document is gone. But the truth isn't. You can burn the paper, but you can't burn what you know. And what you know is that someone modified Priya's device—someone turned a controlled sabotage into a massacre. That person is the real killer. Not Priya. Not Kian. The person who changed the specifications."

Asha looked at me through her tears. "And you think that person is here."

"I think Lena believes that person is here. And I think she might be right. And I think we need to find out who it is before someone else gets hurt."

Asha wiped her eyes. Straightened her spine. And something shifted in her expression—the hard, bright resolve returning, but tempered now, shaped by grief into something more complex and more useful.

"I know something else," she said. "Something I didn't burn."

"What?"

"The name of the person Priya was working with. The person inside Hargrove Holdings who facilitated the original blackmail payment. The person who connected Priya to Daniel Finch."

"Who?"

Asha looked at me.

"Vivienne Marsh."

CHAPTER 30

THE FERRY — THEN

The engine room in its final minutes was a cathedral of destruction.

Water streamed through every joint, every seam, every crack that the failing hull could offer. The twin diesels—each one the size of a small car—were submerged to their cylinders, their housings groaning as cold seawater met hot metal and the thermal shock created fractures that spread through the engine blocks like veins in marble. Steam hissed and billowed, mixing with the salt spray and the diesel fumes to create an atmosphere that was not quite air—a toxic, superheated vapor that burned the lungs and blinded the eyes and turned the engine room into a space that was actively hostile to human life.

Kian Farrow was in the water up to his chest.

He was working. Even now—even with the ferry breaking apart above him, even with the screams of passengers filtering down through the stairwells, even with the certain knowledge that he was going to die—he was working. His hands, submerged, were moving across the face of the device he'd bolted to the starboard engine three days ago. The device that was supposed to be a sensor. The device that was, he now understood, a bomb.

Not a bomb in the traditional sense. No explosives, no detonator, no shrapnel. A hydraulic actuator—a precision instrument designed to apply controlled force to a specific point. In its intended application, it would have slowly increased pressure in the engine's cooling system until a relief valve tripped and the engine shut down. A controlled failure. An inconvenience, not a catastrophe.

But this was not its intended application.

Someone had modified the actuator. Changed the pressure settings. Disabled the relief valve. Turned a carefully calibrated instrument of disruption into a machine of destruction. And Kian hadn't known—hadn't checked the specifications against the original order, hadn't tested the device after installation, hadn't done any of the things a careful engineer would have done—because he trusted Priya, and Priya had told him it was safe, and Priya was never wrong.

Except Priya was wrong. Or Priya had been lied to. Or Priya had been used, the way Kian had been used, the way Daniel Finch had been used—all of them tools in someone else's design, each one handling their piece of the machine without seeing the whole, without understanding what they were building, without knowing that the thing they were assembling was a weapon.

Kian's fingers found the mounting bolts. The ones he couldn't remove before, on the crossing with Elliot—the specialized bolts that required a specific torque wrench he didn't have. He didn't have it now either. But the water had changed things. The cold—the relentless, bone-deep, mind-killing cold—had contracted the metal differently than the heat of the running engine. The bolts were looser. Not loose enough to turn by hand. But loose enough, maybe, if he used the pipe wrench he'd grabbed from the tool rack before it went under.

He got the wrench on the first bolt. Turned. The bolt resisted—then gave, a quarter turn, a half turn, the threads releasing with a grinding reluctance that Kian felt in his wrists and elbows and shoulders.

The water reached his neck.

He tilted his head back. Drew a breath. The air was poison—steam and diesel and the chemical tang of engine coolant—but it was air, and air was everything, and he pulled it into his lungs with the desperate gratitude of a man who understood that he was breathing on borrowed time.

Second bolt. Turn. Resist. Give. The device shifted on its mount—a centimeter, two—and the red light on its face blinked faster. The countdown accelerating. Whatever was going to happen was going to happen soon.

Third bolt. The wrench slipped. Kian's numb fingers lost their grip, and the wrench fell into the water—gone, instantly, swallowed by the black flood. He plunged his hands in after it, searching, his fingers raking the submerged floor of the engine room, finding nothing but silt and debris and the cold, indifferent water.

The water reached his chin.

He tipped his head back. The ceiling of the engine room was eighteen inches above him—eighteen inches of air, diminishing. He could see the overhead pipes, the cable runs, the fluorescent light fixtures that were still, impossibly, glowing—powered by the emergency generator, which was on the upper deck, above the waterline, still running.

Priya.

He thought of her. Not the Priya who had planned this—the calculating, brilliant, reckless Priya who had seen the ferry company's negligence as an opportunity and had seized it with both hands. Not that Priya. The other one. The one who laughed in bed, who sang in the shower, who drew pictures of the islands they would visit when they had enough money—Santorini, Mykonos, the Amalfi Coast. The Priya who touched his face in the dark and whispered that she loved him and that everything was going to be okay.

Everything was not going to be okay.

The device clicked. The red light went solid—no longer blinking, no longer counting. Done. Ready. The actuator engaged, and Kian heard the sound of pressure building in the cooling system—a deep, rising hum, like a note being played on an instrument made of metal and force.

He had seconds.

He took the deepest breath of his life. The last eighteen inches of air. And then the water closed over his head, and the engine room went silent except for the hum and the sound of his own heartbeat in his ears, and in the dark, in the cold, in the absolute and final blackness—

The engine exploded.

And Kian Farrow, who had loved a woman who had used him, who had installed a device he didn't understand, who had tried at the last to undo the thing he had done—

Kian Farrow died trying.

The explosion killed him instantly. The pressure wave shattered his ribcage and pulverized his internal organs before his brain could register pain. He was, in the most literal sense, dead before he knew it. A mercy, perhaps. The only one the ocean offered that night.

His body was recovered three weeks later, on the ocean floor, thirty meters from the wreck site. The coroner's report listed the cause of death as "blunt force trauma consistent with explosive overpressure." It did not mention the pipe wrench, which was found nearby. It did not mention the partially removed mounting bolts on the shattered actuator. It did not mention that Kian Farrow, in his final minutes, had been trying to save the ferry he had helped to kill.

These details were noted in the NTSB report. Filed. Archived. Never made public.

But someone had read them. Someone who read everything, who followed every lead, who traced every thread back to its source with the patience and precision of a woman who had lost the one thing that mattered and had nothing left to lose.

Lena Aldric had read them.

And she had understood.

CHAPTER 31

NORA — NOW

Vivienne Marsh.

I stood in the kitchen of the Glass House, in the dark, in the storm, with the ashes of a burned document settling in the sink and Asha's revelation settling in my mind, and I turned the name over like a stone in my palm.

Vivienne Marsh. Juliet's mother. The former beauty queen. The society matriarch. The woman who had drowned on Deck C of the Blackwater Ferry.

The woman who had connected Priya Patel to Daniel Finch.

"Tell me everything," I said.

Asha spoke quickly, her voice low, her eyes checking the staircase every few seconds—watching for the others, watching for the moment when our private conversation became public.

"Vivienne Marsh sat on the board of the charitable foundation that was supposed to inherit her estate. The foundation was called the Marsh Maritime Trust. It provided scholarships and grants to families of maritime workers. Kian applied for a grant through the trust three years ago. That's how he met Vivienne."

"And Vivienne introduced him to Priya?"

"No. Kian and Priya were already together. But when Priya came up with the blackmail plan—when she decided to approach Hargrove Holdings—she needed a connection. Someone who moved in the right circles. Someone who knew the players."

"Vivienne."

"Vivienne knew everyone. She was old money, old connections, old power. She sat on boards, attended galas, had the kind of social reach that turns introductions into currency. And she had a grudge against Hargrove Holdings."

"What kind of grudge?"

"Her late husband—Juliet's father—had been an investor in Hargrove twenty years ago. He'd lost a substantial amount of money when one of their subsidiaries went bankrupt. Vivienne blamed the company for her husband's financial decline and, indirectly, for his death—he had a heart attack three years after the loss."

"So Vivienne had motive."

"Vivienne had motive and connections. She introduced Priya to the person at Hargrove who could make the payment happen. She brokered the deal. She took a cut—ten percent. And she introduced Priya to Daniel Finch, who she knew through the foundation's fundraising circles. Daniel became the intermediary."

I leaned against the kitchen counter. The pieces were falling into place—not neatly, not completely, but with enough coherence to form a shape. A conspiracy. A network of people, each with their own motive—money, revenge, desperation—connected by a woman who moved through society like a spider through a web, pulling threads, making connections, orchestrating.

"Did Vivienne know about the sabotage?" I asked. "Did she know Priya was planning to damage the ferry?"

"I don't know. Priya didn't tell me everything. She was—compartmentalized. She told different people different things. She told me about the blackmail. She told Kian about the device. She told Vivienne about the financial arrangements. But I don't know if anyone besides Priya had the full picture."

"And the modification—the change to the device that turned it from a controlled failure into a catastrophe. Did Priya know about that?"

"I've spent eighteen months asking myself that question." Asha's voice was a thread. Thin and tight and close to breaking. "I've gone through her texts, her emails, her notes. Everything I could find. And the answer is—I don't think so. I think she designed a controlled sabotage and someone changed it. Someone who wanted not a scandal but a disaster."

"Who benefits from a disaster?"

"The lawsuits. The settlements. The insurance claims. A controlled engine failure would have generated bad press for Hargrove. A disaster—thirty-one dead, a ferry at the bottom of the ocean—generated hundreds of millions in claims. Someone who positioned themselves correctly—who had the right insurance policies, the right legal representation, the right advance knowledge—could have profited enormously."

I thought about Vivienne Marsh's will. The one changed two weeks before the crossing. The old version: estate to the charitable foundation. The new version: everything to Juliet.

Fourteen million dollars.

What if the will change wasn't Vivienne's idea? What if someone had pressured her—threatened her, coerced her—into changing the beneficiary? What if Vivienne, who had brokered the blackmail deal, who had connected Priya to Daniel, who had taken her ten percent, had become a liability? What if someone decided that Vivienne knew too much, and the simplest way to silence her was to ensure she was on the ferry when it went down?

And what if that same someone had modified Priya's device—turning it from a tool of disruption into a weapon of mass murder—to guarantee that the ferry went down completely? Not a malfunction. Not a scandal. An annihilation.

"Asha," I said. "The person who modified the device—the person who changed Priya's plan from sabotage to massacre. That's the person Lena is looking for. That's the person who killed Maisie. And thirty others."

"Yes."

"And that person knew about Priya's plan. Knew about the blackmail. Knew about the device. Knew about the network—Vivienne, Daniel, Kian. That person was inside the conspiracy."

"Yes."

"So who was inside the conspiracy and had the knowledge and access to modify the device?"

Asha looked at me. Her dark eyes were pools of shadow in the dim kitchen, her face lit only by the faint blue flame of the gas burner she'd used to burn her evidence.

"That's what I can't figure out," she said. "Priya, Kian, Daniel, Vivienne—they're all dead. The only people left who knew about the plan are me and whoever Priya's contact at Hargrove was."

"Marcus."

"Maybe. He managed their money. He could have known about the blackmail payment."

"He admitted to facilitating it. He told me he paid Daniel $47,000 as a bribe—to delay a safety report."

"Then Marcus knew about the maintenance failures, knew about the blackmail, and had access to the financial infrastructure of Hargrove Holdings. He could have arranged the purchase of the modified device through Daniel's shell company."

"But Marcus was on the ferry," I said. "He was a passenger. If he'd planned the sabotage, why would he be on board?"

"Maybe for the same reason Daniel was on board. To be a victim. To collect. To be beyond suspicion."

"Daniel died."

"Daniel wasn't supposed to die. Priya designed the sabotage to cause a controlled engine failure—an inconvenience, a stranding, not a sinking. If Marcus was part of the plan, he would have expected the same thing. A disruption, not a disaster. He would have boarded the ferry confident that he'd be briefly inconvenienced and then heroically rescued. The perfect cover."

"But someone changed the plan."

"Someone changed the plan. And the ferry sank. And thirty-one people died. And Marcus Cole—if he was the one who modified the device—killed them."

Or Marcus Cole, like everyone else, had been played. Had been a pawn in someone else's game. Had boarded the ferry expecting a minor incident and found himself in a massacre, scrambling for survival, jumping into forty-one-degree water in his expensive suit while the ship broke apart around him.

I didn't know. I couldn't tell. The hall of mirrors that this weekend had become—the reflections within reflections, the lies inside lies, the truths that turned out to be deceptions and the deceptions that turned out to be truths—had made certainty impossible. Every answer opened three new questions. Every revelation concealed a deeper secret. And we were running out of time.

The storm was not letting up. The Coast Guard was hours away. Juliet was bleeding. Bram was missing. Lena's investigation had spiraled beyond her control. And somewhere in this house—or on this island, in the storm, in the dark—was the person who had turned a small crime into a catastrophe and was now watching the rest of us tear each other apart, waiting.

Patient. Calm. Unsuspected.

The way the water had been patient, that night on the ferry.

I turned off the gas burner. The kitchen went dark.

"We need to go back down," I told Asha. "We need to tell the others."

"Tell them what? That my sister planned the sabotage? That Juliet's mother brokered the deal? That Daniel—your husband—was the bagman?"

"Tell them everything. Every piece. Because Lena was right about one thing: these secrets are interconnected. They're pieces of a picture, and until all the pieces are on the table, we can't see what we're looking at."

"And if the picture shows one of us?"

"Then at least we'll know."

Asha looked at me for a long moment. Then she picked up her sketchpad from the counter where she'd left it, and she tucked it under her arm, and she followed me toward the staircase that led down into the stone belly of the Glass House.

Behind us, the kitchen window cracked.

Not the northeast window—a different one. A panel on the west side, facing the open Atlantic. The crack appeared without warning—a sharp, crystalline sound, like a bone breaking—and spread across the glass in a pattern that looked, in the faint storm-light, like a web.

Like a net.

Like a trap closing.

CHAPTER 32

NORA — NOW

Dawn came not as light but as a lessening of darkness.

The storm had spent itself through the night—not ending, exactly, but exhausting the worst of its fury, the way a fever breaks. The wind dropped from a scream to a moan. The rain thinned from sheets to curtains to a fine, persistent mist that clung to the glass walls of the house like a second skin. The ocean, visible now through the rain-streaked panels, was the color of pewter—heavy, dull, still heaving with long, slow swells that spoke of the violence that had passed.

I hadn't slept. None of us had—not really. The foundation level had become a foxhole, a bunker, a place where six people huddled in amber light and listened to the house above them creak and groan and absorb the storm's blows like a boxer in the late rounds. Tom had dozed in thirty-minute intervals, his hand on Juliet's wrist, waking each time to check her pulse, her breathing, the dressing on her wound. Marcus had sat against the wall with his eyes open, staring at the stone ceiling, calculating—always calculating—the angles of a situation that had moved far beyond calculation. Elliot had written in his journal for hours, pages and pages of small, dense handwriting that I couldn't read from where I sat but that I imagined was the raw material of the story he would write if he survived to write it.

Asha sat beside me. She didn't draw. She didn't speak. She sat with her arms around her knees and her eyes fixed on nothing, and I could feel the weight of what she'd told me in the kitchen pressing down on her like a physical thing—a gravity that bent her spine and rounded her shoulders and made her smaller with each passing hour.

Lena—I couldn't call her Iris anymore, couldn't maintain the fiction now that the fiction had been confessed—had retreated to the far end of the corridor. She sat in the doorway of the storage room, her back against the frame, her bare feet drawn up, her silver hair loose around her face. She looked, in the amber light, like a woman who had run out of plans and was waiting to see what would fill the space they'd occupied.

At 6:12 AM, I went upstairs.

The bedroom corridor was intact—the glass walls had held, the frosted panels were uncracked, the heated floors were working again now that Bram's last act before vanishing had been, apparently, to reset the circuit breakers. I walked the length of the corridor and climbed the main staircase to the living area.

The damage was contained but significant. The west-facing window—the one that had cracked as Asha and I left the kitchen—had held. The lamination had done its job, absorbing the fracture without failing, the way a windshield absorbs an impact and spiderwebs but doesn't shatter. But the crack was extensive—a web of fracture lines covering most of the panel, turning the glass from transparent to translucent, the ocean beyond reduced to a blur of gray movement.

The northeast panel—the one Tom had found cracked from the inside—was worse. The outer layer had given way during the night. A section roughly eighteen inches square had fallen out, leaving a gap through which the storm had driven rain and salt spray into the Gangplank corridor. The floor was wet, the steel frame was dripping, and the wind—reduced but not gone—whistled through the opening with a low, persistent note that sounded like someone humming.

I stepped carefully around the wet glass and moved to the front door. It opened with effort—debris had accumulated against it during the night, branches and leaves and chunks of peat torn from the cliff face by the wind. I pushed through and stepped outside.

The morning air was raw. Cold enough to sting my lungs, salt-laden, thick with the mineral smell of churned earth and broken vegetation. The cliff path was a ruin—the neat crushed-stone walkway Tom had walked the day before was gone, replaced by a mudslide of dark earth and exposed roots and pale limestone rubble. The spruce and birch trees that lined the path were battered, their branches stripped, their trunks leaning at angles that spoke of sustained, directional force.

But it was the ground that held my attention.

Footprints.

Not old ones—fresh, made within the last few hours, after the worst of the rain had stopped but before the mist had settled. Deep impressions in the dark mud, the tread pattern of heavy boots—work boots, the kind Bram wore. They led from the front of the house, down the ruined cliff path, and then veered north, away from the dock, away from the water, toward the interior of the island.

I followed them.

The path—if it could be called a path—wound through the battered spruce grove, the trees closing in overhead, their remaining needles forming a sparse canopy that filtered the gray morning light into something dimmer and more claustrophobic. The footprints were clear in the mud, each one a precise record of Bram's passage—the depth indicating his weight, the spacing indicating his stride, the direction indicating purpose. He had not been wandering. He had known where he was going.

After ten minutes of walking—maybe a quarter mile—the trees opened onto a clearing. And in the clearing stood a structure I hadn't seen from the house, hidden by the grove and the contour of the island's rocky spine.

It was a boathouse.

Not the kind of boathouse you see at lakeside cottages—a cheerful, painted thing with a dock and a canoe rack. This was industrial. Concrete block walls, corrugated metal roof, a wide rolling door facing a narrow channel that had been cut through the rock to the ocean. The channel was rough—natural, mostly, with some evidence of blasting to widen it—and at its mouth, protected from the open sea by a rock outcropping, the water was relatively calm.

Calm enough to launch a boat.

The rolling door was open. Inside, the boathouse was dim, lit by a single battery-powered lantern hanging from a ceiling hook. The space was large enough for a vessel of thirty feet or more, with a concrete ramp leading down into the water of the channel. Ropes and fenders hung from the walls. A workbench in the corner held tools—wrenches, pliers, electrical tape, a soldering iron.

And at the workbench, his back to me, his hands moving with the quiet concentration of someone performing a familiar task, was Bram.

He was assembling something. I couldn't see what—his broad shoulders blocked my view—but I could hear the sounds: the click of components fitting together, the scrape of metal on metal, the soft grunt of effort as he tightened something by hand.

"Bram."

He didn't startle. Didn't turn quickly, didn't flinch. He simply stopped what he was doing, set down whatever he was holding, and turned to face me with the slow, deliberate calm of a man who had been expecting company.

"You followed the prints," he said. Not a question.

"You didn't try to hide them."

"No."

"Why not?"

He looked at me. His face was unreadable—the same granite expression he'd worn since the dock in Harwick, the face of a man who had long ago decided that the world was not worth reacting to. But his eyes—dark, deep-set, shadowed by the heavy brow—were not empty. They held something. Not guilt. Not fear. Something older and heavier than either.

Resignation.

"Because it's time," he said.

"Time for what?"

He stepped aside, and I saw what he'd been working on.

It was a radio. Not the marine VHF that Lena had described—not the one in the utility room that she'd used to contact the Coast Guard. This was something different. Older. A high-frequency transceiver, the kind used by fishing vessels and research stations, capable of reaching much farther than a standard marine radio. It was partially disassembled—the casing open, the circuit boards exposed, wires dangling from connectors that Bram had been reattaching with the careful precision of someone who understood the machine's anatomy.

"The radio in the house," I said. "The marine VHF. Did you disable it?"

Bram's jaw tightened. A muscle worked in his cheek.

"Yes."

"When?"

"Last night. Before I left."

"Why?"

"Because Lena was going to use it. She was going to call the Coast Guard and tell them to come. And if they came—if they came before she had what she needed—" He stopped. His hands, hanging at his sides, curled into fists and then uncurled. The controlled violence of a man who had punched through a windshield with his bare hands, fighting the impulse to destroy. "She would have failed. And Maisie—Maisie's death would have been for nothing."

"So you disabled the house radio to keep the Coast Guard away."

"I disabled the house radio so Lena would have more time. Then I came here, to fix the long-range radio. The one that actually works. The one that can reach the mainland even in a storm."

"And have you? Fixed it?"

"Almost." He turned back to the workbench. His hands found the radio's innards again—sure, familiar, the hands of a man who had spent his life working with machines and understood them better than he understood people. "Another hour. Maybe less."

"Bram." I stepped into the boathouse. The concrete floor was wet, the air smelled of brine and engine oil, and the light from the single lantern cast our shadows long and wavering on the block walls. "Did you stab Juliet?"

He didn't answer immediately. He reconnected a wire, soldered it in place, tested the connection with a multimeter. The device beeped—a short, affirmative tone.

"No," he said.

"She said your name. Before she passed out."

"I know what she said. I heard her."

"You were there?"

"I was in the corridor. On the bedroom level. I heard a sound—a gasp, or a cry—and I went to look. Juliet was on the floor. Bleeding. I could see the wound. I could see she needed help." He set down the soldering iron. "And I could see that if I stayed—if I was found standing over a bleeding woman in the dark—no one would believe I hadn't done it."

"So you ran."

"I made a tactical decision." His voice was flat, stripped of emotion, the voice of a man delivering a report. "I am a large, foreign man with a criminal record—yes, I have one; assault, in Sarajevo, fifteen years ago, a bar fight that went too far. I do not speak much. I do not smile. I am exactly the kind of person who is blamed when something goes wrong, because blaming me is easy and convenient and requires no further investigation."

He turned to face me fully. In the lantern light, his eyes were not granite. They were wet.

"I loved Maisie," he said. "She called me Ujak—uncle. She would sit on my shoulders and pull my hair and laugh. She was the only person in my life who laughed." His voice cracked—a single, sharp fracture in the granite, there and gone. "I would not hurt anyone in Lena's house. But I knew—I knew in my bones—that I would be accused. So I left. I came here. To fix the radio. To do the one useful thing I could do."

I believed him.

Not because his story was flawless—it wasn't. Not because his explanation was complete—it raised more questions than it answered. I believed him because of the way he said Maisie's name. The tenderness in it. The way his mouth shaped the syllables as if they were made of glass and he was afraid of breaking them.

A man who said a child's name like that did not stab women in the dark.

Which meant someone else had.

"Bram. If you didn't stab Juliet, then someone in the house did. Someone who had a reason to silence her. She'd found something—documents, evidence. She was carrying them when she was attacked."

"What documents?"

"A bank statement. From Andrei's account."

Bram went very still. The controlled stillness of a man who has just heard a name he wasn't expecting.

"Andrei's account was closed after he died," Bram said. "Lena closed it."

"Someone reopened it. Or kept it open. And someone used it to receive payments from Hargrove Holdings. Sixty thousand dollars over three months. In the period before the ferry disaster."

Bram's face changed. The resignation was gone, replaced by something rawer—confusion, then comprehension, then a slow, building horror that moved across his features like a weather front.

"Lena," he whispered.

"I don't know. Maybe. The account is in Andrei's name, but Lena would have had access—passwords, security questions, signatory authority as the surviving spouse."

"She wouldn't—" He stopped. Restarted. "She loved Maisie. She would not have done anything that put Maisie in danger. She would have died first. She did die—the woman she was before the ferry, that woman died. What's left is—"

"What's left is a woman who lied about her identity, lured six strangers to an island, confiscated their phones, and is conducting an investigation that looks less like justice and more like revenge."

Bram said nothing. He stood at his workbench with his tools and his half-repaired radio and his crumbling certainties, and he said nothing, because there was nothing to say.

"Finish the radio," I told him. "Call the Coast Guard. Get them here. Whatever truth is in this house, we need help finding it before someone else gets hurt."

I turned and walked out of the boathouse, into the mist, into the gray morning, into the battered landscape of Calder Island. Behind me, I heard the soldering iron hiss as Bram returned to his work.

And ahead of me, through the spruce grove, the Glass House waited. Its walls streaked with rain, its windows cracked, its transparency compromised. A house designed to reveal everything, slowly revealing that the most important things had been hidden all along.

CHAPTER 33

TOM — NOW

Juliet opened her eyes at 7:43 AM.

Tom knew the exact time because he'd been tracking it—marking each pulse check, each breath count, each small shift in her condition against the minutes and hours as they passed. His firefighter's log, maintained in his head since the moment he'd dropped to his knees beside her bleeding body, was precise and complete: pulse rate, respiratory rate, level of consciousness, color of skin, temperature of extremities. A running record of a life hovering between survival and failure.

At 7:43, her eyelids fluttered. At 7:44, they opened. At 7:45, she spoke.

"Tom."

Her voice was a thread—thin, dry, barely there. But coherent. Alert. The voice of a woman who had surfaced from a deep place and was orienting herself in the light.

"I'm here," Tom said. He leaned forward, one hand still on her wrist, the other reaching for the water bottle beside the mattress. "Don't try to move. You've been stabbed. I've sutured the wound, but you need to stay still."

"I know I've been stabbed." A ghost of the old Juliet—the dry, controlled, immaculately armored Juliet—flickered across her face. "I was there."

"Can you tell me what happened?"

"Water first."

He held the bottle to her lips. She drank—small sips, carefully, the way someone drinks when they're not sure their body will cooperate. Her color was better than it had been during the night—still pale, but with a faint warmth returning to her cheeks, a pinkness that suggested the bleeding had stopped and the body was beginning to recover.

"Juliet. Who stabbed you?"

She closed her eyes. Not in unconsciousness—in thought. The deliberate, careful thought of a woman who was about to say something important and wanted to say it correctly.

"I went upstairs," she said. "During the night. I couldn't sleep. The stone walls—I needed air. Space. Glass." She opened her eyes. "I know that sounds insane, given that the glass house is what we were supposed to be hiding from. But I needed to see out. I needed to see the sky, even if the sky was nothing but storm."

"You went to the bedroom level."

"Yes. My room. I wanted my bag. There were things in it—documents I'd brought with me. Things I needed to look at."

"What documents?"

Juliet's gaze shifted. She looked past Tom, toward the alcove entrance, checking who was listening. The corridor beyond was empty—the others were upstairs, in the kitchen, assembling a breakfast from the remaining supplies. Tom had stayed with Juliet. He'd told them he would call if anything changed.

"Before I came to this island," Juliet said, her voice dropping to barely a whisper, "I received a package. No return address. Postmarked from Bangor, Maine. Inside were two documents: a bank statement and a photograph."

"What were they?"

"The bank statement showed payments from Hargrove Holdings to an account in the name of Andrei Aldric—Lena's dead husband. Sixty thousand dollars over three months before the ferry disaster."

Tom processed this. The implications assembled themselves in his mind with the mechanical clarity of structural analysis—cause, effect, load, failure point.

"And the photograph?"

"Marcus Cole and Lena Aldric. Together. On the Harwick dock. The day of the crossing. Two hours before departure."

"They knew each other."

"They more than knew each other. The body language in the photo—the proximity, the angle of their heads, the way she was touching his arm—they were intimate. Close. Familiar in a way that goes beyond professional acquaintance."

Tom sat back. The mattress creaked beneath him. In the amber light of the foundation level, Juliet's face was a study in controlled pain—the wound hurting her, yes, but the information hurting her more.

"My mother was involved," Juliet said. "In the blackmail. In the conspiracy. Asha told us that much last night. Vivienne Marsh—my mother, the woman who measured my waist at breakfast and criticized my posture at dinner and told me I would never be enough—was brokering deals with blackmailers and saboteurs."

"Juliet—"

"Let me finish." Her jaw tightened. The control was back—the Juliet who did not cry, who did not bend, who absorbed every blow and converted it to steel. "I suspected before I came here. Not the specifics—not the blackmail, not the device, not the engineering of it. But I suspected that my mother's death was not simple. That her presence on the ferry that night was not coincidental."

"Why?"

"Because of the will. The change, two weeks before the crossing. Fourteen million dollars redirected from the foundation to me. My mother did nothing without a reason, Tom. Every action was calculated, every decision was strategic. She changed her will because she knew something was going to happen. She was preparing."

"Preparing for what?"

"I don't know. That's what I came here to find out. When Lena's invitation arrived—'Dr. Iris Vane,' the famous therapist, the retreat, the healing—I recognized it for what it was. Not therapy. An investigation. And I thought: Good. Let someone investigate. Let someone dig into the truth of that night. Maybe they'll find what I can't."

"But you brought evidence of your own."

"I brought the documents I'd been sent. The bank statement and the photograph. I brought them because I thought they might be useful—to Lena, to whoever was trying to uncover the truth. I didn't know what they meant. I still don't. Not fully."

"Juliet." Tom leaned forward again. "Who stabbed you?"

She was quiet for a long moment. The amber light hummed. The stone walls held their silence.

"I was in my room," she said. "I'd taken the documents from my bag. I was looking at the photograph—Marcus and Lena on the dock—and trying to understand what it meant. And then I heard a sound behind me. In the corridor. A footstep."

"Did you see who it was?"

"I turned. It was dark—the emergency lights were out on the bedroom level. I could see a shape. A silhouette. Large. Moving toward me."

"Bram."

"I thought it was Bram. The size was right. The shape was right. But—" She hesitated. "Tom, I need you to understand something. I said Bram's name when Nora found me. I said it because that's who I thought I saw. But I was in pain. I was bleeding. The corridor was dark. And the silhouette—"

She closed her eyes again.

"The silhouette wasn't moving like Bram. Bram moves deliberately. Heavily. Like a man carrying something. This person moved quickly. Lightly. They covered the distance between the end of the corridor and my doorway in seconds. And when they reached me—when the knife went in—" She pressed her hand against the dressing on her side. "The hand that held the knife was not Bram's hand."

"How do you know?"

"Because I grabbed it. Instinct—when the blade went in, I reached down and grabbed the hand that held it. For one second, maybe two, I felt it. The fingers. The wrist. The skin."

She opened her eyes and looked at Tom with an intensity that made the amber light seem to brighten around her.

"The hand was small," she said. "Slender. Not a man's hand."

Tom's breath stopped.

"And on the wrist," Juliet continued, "I felt something. A raised line. A scar. Or a—"

"A tattoo," Tom said.

Juliet nodded.

They looked at each other in the amber light of the underground, and the name they were both thinking settled between them like a dropped match.

Asha.

CHAPTER 34

NORA — NOW

I returned to the house to find Marcus in the kitchen, alone.

The others were in the living room—Elliot examining the damaged windows with the careful attention of someone cataloging evidence, Asha on the sofa with her sketchpad, drawing with the focused, mechanical intensity that I now recognized as not processing but performing. Performing fragility. Performing the grief-stricken twin. Performing the vulnerable, broken woman who wept at confessions and clutched her pencils and made everyone in the room want to protect her.

The finest disguise is vulnerability.

I knew this because I'd used it myself. Not the same way—my disguise was competence, composure, the calm surface of a woman who had her life together. But the principle was the same. You show people what they expect to see. You give them a narrative that satisfies their assumptions. And they stop looking.

We had all stopped looking at Asha.

Marcus was standing at the kitchen counter with a cup of coffee he wasn't drinking, staring at the cracked west-facing window. The mist outside was thickening, pressing against the damaged glass like something trying to get in.

"We need to talk," I said.

"Agreed."

"Not here."

He looked at me. Assessed. Decided.

"The library alcove," he said. "It's the closest thing to privacy this house offers."

We went. The alcove was unchanged—three walls of bookshelves, one wall of glass, the small space that had become our unofficial conference room. Marcus leaned against the shelves. I stood by the glass wall. Between us, the distance was small enough for whispered conversation, large enough for the illusion of safety.

"Juliet had documents," I said. "A bank statement from Andrei Aldric's account showing payments from Hargrove Holdings. And a photograph of you and Lena together on the Harwick dock the day of the ferry."

Marcus's expression didn't change. But his hands—the ones holding the coffee cup he wasn't drinking from—went very still.

"She told you this?"

"I found the documents in her room last night. Before she was stabbed."

"And you have them now?"

"I have the bank statement. The photograph I left in her boot."

"Give me the bank statement."

"No."

His jaw tightened. "Nora—"

"The bank statement shows sixty thousand dollars from Hargrove Holdings to Lena's dead husband's account. You managed Hargrove's finances. You facilitated the $47,000 blackmail payment through Daniel. And you were photographed with Lena on the dock the day of the crossing." I kept my voice level, measured, controlled. The voice of Elena Carr, who had talked her way out of worse situations than this. "Tell me about your relationship with Lena."

Marcus set the coffee cup on the shelf. Deliberately. Precisely. The cup centered on the wood, the handle at three o'clock. The habits of a man who controlled everything because losing control was unthinkable.

"I met Lena Aldric eighteen months before the ferry," he said. "At a fundraiser for the Marsh Maritime Trust. Vivienne introduced us. Lena was working as a forensic accountant—freelance, mostly, after her husband died. She was looking for clients. I was looking for someone who could audit Hargrove's books without reporting to the board."

"An off-the-record audit."

"I knew there were problems. The maintenance deferrals, the budget cuts, the shell companies siphoning funds. I needed to understand the scope before I decided what to do about it. Lena was perfect—smart, discreet, no connections to the maritime industry. She could look at the numbers without anyone knowing."

"And she found what you expected."

"She found worse. The ferry fleet was a liability disaster. Three of four vessels were operating with expired safety certifications. The maintenance logs had been falsified for years. And the money that should have gone to upkeep had been redirected into executive compensation through the shell companies I'd helped set up."

"Shell companies you'd helped set up," I repeated.

"Yes. I'm not pretending innocence. I designed the financial architecture that made the cost-cutting possible. I didn't know—or didn't want to know—that the cost-cutting extended to passenger safety. But I knew the money was being moved, and I knew where it was going."

"So Lena audited Hargrove and found that the ferries were deathtraps. And then what?"

"Then she came to me. Privately. Not as an auditor—as something else. She was angry. Not professionally angry—personally angry. She said her daughter rode those ferries. That Maisie took the crossing twice a month to visit Andrei's family on the islands. That every time her daughter boarded one of those boats, she was being put at risk by men who valued profit over safety."

He paused. His hand moved to the shelf behind him, finding the edge, gripping it.

"She wanted to go public. I convinced her not to. I told her I could fix it—that if she gave me time, I could redirect the funds, update the fleet, bring everything up to standard without the public scandal that would destroy Hargrove and everyone connected to it. Including me."

"And the sixty thousand?"

"I paid her. From Hargrove's discretionary fund. For the audit work, officially. But really—for her patience. For her silence. For giving me six months to make it right."

"You paid her into her dead husband's account."

"She asked me to. She said she didn't want the payments in her own name—too easy to trace, too easy for someone at Hargrove to discover that an unauthorized audit had been conducted. Using Andrei's account created a layer of separation."

"Or a layer of deniability."

"Both. Yes. Both."

I studied Marcus. His posture, his hands, his eyes. Looking for the tells—the micro-expressions, the involuntary signals that separate truth from performance. Marcus was good. One of the best I'd ever encountered. But no one was perfect, and the stress of this weekend—the sleeplessness, the fear, the accumulating pressure of confession after confession—had worn his polish thin.

I saw it in his eyes. The hazel irises—green at the edges, brown at the center—were bloodshot, the pupils slightly dilated. Not the dilation of deception. The dilation of exhaustion. Of a man who had been carrying a weight for eighteen months and was, finally, setting it down.

"The photograph," I said. "You and Lena on the dock. The day of the crossing."

"I met her there. That afternoon. She'd called me the night before—frantic. She said she'd discovered something in the Hargrove files. Something that went beyond negligence. She said someone inside the company was actively sabotaging the fleet—making the vessels less safe, not through neglect but through deliberate action. Disabling safety systems. Weakening structural components. She said it looked like someone was engineering a disaster."

"And she told you this on the dock. Two hours before the ferry went down."

"She told me she had evidence. Documents. She said she was going to take the ferry to the mainland and bring the evidence to the Coast Guard. She wanted me to come with her. To corroborate. To testify."

"So you booked a last-minute ticket."

"I booked a last-minute ticket. And I boarded the ferry with Lena Aldric and her nine-year-old daughter, and two hours later, thirty-one people were dead, including Maisie, and Lena was in the ocean, and I was in a life raft, and everything I thought I understood about what was happening turned out to be wrong."

"Wrong how?"

Marcus's grip on the shelf tightened. His knuckles whitened.

"Because the evidence Lena had—the documents she was carrying to the Coast Guard—went down with the ferry. Her bag. Her laptop. Everything. Lost. And in the eighteen months since, Lena has reconstructed some of it from memory, from re-auditing, from the obsessive investigation she's been conducting. But the original evidence—the documents that would have proven deliberate sabotage—is at the bottom of the Blackwater Strait."

"Convenient," I said, echoing the word Marcus had used about Juliet's claim to have felt her mother die. It landed the same way—sharp, uncomfortable, pointed.

"I know how it sounds. But it's the truth."

"Marcus. You've told me three versions of your involvement since we got here. First, you were a portfolio manager with no operational knowledge. Then, you were a facilitator who paid a bribe through Daniel Finch. Now, you're a reformer who was trying to fix the company from the inside with the help of a forensic accountant you were paying from a slush fund. Which version is real?"

"All of them. Each one is a layer. Each one is true as far as it goes."

"And how far does the final layer go?"

He looked at me. And in his eyes, beneath the exhaustion and the fear and the eighteen months of accumulated guilt, I saw something I hadn't expected.

Grief.

Not the showy, demonstrative grief that people perform for audiences. The quiet, contained, deeply private grief of a man who has lost something he can't name and can't replace.

"I was falling in love with her," he said. "With Lena. That's the layer I haven't told anyone. We'd been meeting for months—for the audit, for the planning, for the strategy of how to fix Hargrove without blowing everything up. And somewhere in those meetings, in those late-night conversations, in the shared project of trying to do the right thing in a system designed to prevent it—I fell in love with her."

He paused.

"And then her daughter died on a ferry that my company owned, because of failures that my financial engineering had made possible. And I couldn't—" His voice broke. A hairline fracture in the steel. "I couldn't face her. After. I couldn't look at the woman I loved and know that I was part of the machine that had killed her child."

"So you disappeared."

"I disappeared. Went back to New York. Threw myself into work. Waited for the investigation, the lawsuits, the consequences. They came—the bankruptcy, the indictment of the CEO, the settlements. But no one came for me. My name was buried in the corporate structure, layers deep, protected by the same shell companies I'd built. I was invisible."

"Until Lena's invitation."

"Until Dr. Iris Vane's invitation. Which I recognized immediately as Lena's. Because I knew her handwriting. I'd seen it on a hundred audit reports, a hundred late-night notes, a hundred—" He stopped himself. "I came because I owed her. I came because I loved her. And I came because I wanted to know if she'd brought me here to heal me or to punish me."

"And which is it?"

"Both. It's always both."

I stood in the library alcove with Marcus Cole, and the truth—the multilayered, contradictory, heartbreaking truth—settled around us like dust. Not clean. Not simple. Not the kind of truth that resolves into a verdict. The kind that expands, that complicates, that makes the world larger and harder and more painfully human.

And I thought: This is what Lena doesn't understand. This is what her investigation can't accommodate. The truth isn't a single thing. It's not a saboteur and a victim. It's a web. A network. A system of compromises and cowardices and good intentions gone wrong, and the disaster that emerges from it is not one person's crime but everyone's failure.

But Maisie was still dead. And thirty others. And someone had modified the device. Someone had turned Priya's sabotage into a massacre.

That person was not everyone. That person was one.

And we still didn't know who.

CHAPTER 35

THE FERRY — THEN

She saw the face.

In the final moments—after the ferry broke apart, after the stern section slid beneath the surface, after the ocean swallowed everything that could be swallowed and left the rest—she saw the face of the person standing at the stern railing. The person who had watched the disaster unfold with the composed attention of someone observing an experiment. The person with the phone, the dark screen, the message sent into the darkness.

It's done.

The face was illuminated by the phone's screen—a brief, blue-white glow that lasted no more than two seconds before the figure pocketed the device and turned away. Two seconds. Enough time for the light to catch the planes and angles of a human face and burn them into her memory with the permanence of a brand.

She had replayed those two seconds ten thousand times in the eighteen months since. In therapy sessions she'd attended under her real name. In sleepless nights in the apartment she'd rented on Calder Island, the walls covered with photographs and string. In the shower, in the car, in the moments between waking and sleeping when the mind is most honest and most cruel.

Two seconds. A face. A confirmation.

The face belonged to none of the people she'd invited to the Glass House.

This was the thing she hadn't told anyone. The piece she'd held in reserve—the card she'd kept face-down on the table while she played the others, one by one, probing and testing and pressuring, building her case from their confessions and their lies.

Because the face at the stern was not Tom, not Marcus, not Juliet, not Asha, not Elliot, not Nora.

The face at the stern belonged to someone she had not invited.

Someone who was supposed to be dead.

The face at the stern belonged to Daniel Finch.

She had been certain. Not immediately—in the immediate aftermath, floating on her debris raft, half-frozen, half-drowned, her daughter's hand a phantom sensation on her fingers—she had not been capable of certainty about anything. But later. Days later, weeks later, when the shock receded and the grief compressed into something hard and functional and the investigation began in earnest—she had returned to those two seconds and examined them with the forensic precision of a woman whose career had been built on examining things that others overlooked.

The face was Daniel Finch's.

She was certain because she had met him. Once, briefly, at a fundraiser for the Marsh Maritime Trust—the same event where Vivienne had introduced her to Marcus. Daniel had been there with his wife—the real Sarah, the sharp-nosed, quick-laughing woman who would die on Deck C two months later. They'd exchanged pleasantries. Shaken hands. She'd noticed his face because it was a noticeable face—handsome, careless, the kind of face that made you look twice not because it was beautiful but because it was alive.

That face, on the stern of the sinking ferry. Lit by a phone screen. Calm. Unsurprised. Watching.

But Daniel Finch was dead. His body had been recovered from the wreck site. Dental records had confirmed the identification. The death certificate had been filed, the estate had been settled, the life insurance had been paid.

Unless.

Unless the body identified as Daniel Finch was not Daniel Finch.

Unless the dental records had been falsified. Or switched. Or misread.

Unless Daniel Finch had planned his own death—faked it, engineered it, disappeared into the chaos of a disaster he had helped create—and left behind a body that would be identified as his, and a wife whose grief would foreclose any further inquiry, and a trail of evidence that pointed to a dead man who could never be questioned, never be charged, never be held accountable.

The perfect crime. The perfect escape. The perfect death.

Except that Lena Aldric had seen his face.

And Lena Aldric did not forget faces.

This was why she had invited the six guests. Not because she suspected one of them of sabotage—though she had, initially, and the investigation had revealed that several of them were implicated in the conspiracy to varying degrees. She had invited them because each one of them had a connection to Daniel Finch. Each one of them might know something—a detail, a conversation, an observation—that could confirm what she already believed.

That Daniel Finch was alive.

That Daniel Finch had sabotaged the Blackwater Ferry, killed thirty-one people, faked his own death, and disappeared.

And that the woman calling herself Nora Finch—the woman who had assumed his dead wife's identity, who was living in his house, spending his money, performing his widow's grief—was either his accomplice or his next victim.

NORA — NOW

The marine radio was in the utility room, bolted to a metal shelf above the generator. She'd lied.

The silence that followed was not empty. It was filled with the ghost of something I recognized—the pathology of twins, of enmeshment, of two people so deeply intertwined that separation feels like death and the only escape is to make the metaphor literal.

Asha, who had lived her entire life as half of a whole. Asha, who had followed Priya into everything—relationships, decisions, crimes. Asha, whose identity was so fused with her sister's that losing her was not loss but amputation.

What if Asha hadn't been trying to destroy the ferry?

What if she'd been trying to destroy Priya?

I thought of the tattoo. Two birds. Mirror images. One flying up. One flying down.

They're not together, Elliot had said during the Mirror Exercise. They're going in opposite directions.

What if the modification of the device—the change from controlled failure to catastrophic destruction—was not about money or revenge or ideology? What if it was about a twin who had decided, consciously or unconsciously, that the only way to be free was to ensure that her other half did not survive?

It was monstrous. It was unthinkable. It was the kind of truth that, once spoken, could not be unspoken—a truth that would redefine every conversation, every confession, every moment of vulnerability that Asha had offered since she arrived.

"And Asha?" Tom asked.

"Asha is upstairs. With Lena. Right now."

The three of us looked at each other. The same thought in each of our minds, the same calculation, the same fear.

Two women, alone in a glass house. One of them a grieving mother seeking justice for her dead child. The other, possibly, the person who killed that child.

We moved.

NORA — NOW

We found them in the living room.

The scene was almost peaceful—if you didn't know what we knew, if you couldn't see the architecture of suspicion and danger that had been assembling itself all night like a structure built in the dark. Lena was in the armchair by the dead fireplace, her bare feet tucked beneath her, a cup of tea in her hands. Asha was on the floor—her default position, cross-legged, sketchpad open, pencil moving in the small, sure strokes that I now recognized not as therapy but as camouflage. The busy hands of a woman who wanted to appear occupied. The downcast eyes of a woman who didn't want to be looked at too closely.

The morning light—gray, diffused, weakened by the mist—filled the living room through the intact glass panels, making the space feel like the inside of a cloud. The damaged panels on the west and northeast walls were opaque with fracture lines, but enough glass remained that the house still functioned as Lena had designed it: a space of exposure. A space where hiding was supposed to be impossible.

And yet.

Tom entered first. His stride was measured—not aggressive, not urgent, but purposeful. The walk of a man crossing a room with intention. Elliot followed, journal in hand, his restless energy for once directed and contained. I came last, positioning myself by the main staircase—not blocking the exit, but aware of it. Aware of all the exits. Aware that the next few minutes would either clarify everything or detonate it.

Lena looked up. She read the room instantly—I watched it happen, the flicker of assessment in her dark eyes, the rapid calculation of body language and positioning and the particular quality of silence that precedes confrontation.

"Something's happened," she said.

"Several things have happened," Tom said. "Most of them overnight. Most of them while you were telling us the marine radio had reached the Coast Guard."

Lena set down her tea. The cup made a small, precise sound on the glass table—the sound of a woman who controlled even the way she set things down.

"The radio—"

"Is dead. Cable cut. Bram did it, on your instructions or on his own initiative—we can debate that later. What matters is that there's no Coast Guard coming. There never was. You lied."

I watched her hands.

"I didn't instruct Bram to cut the radio," Lena said. Her voice was controlled but strained, the composure thinning, the real woman beneath it pressing against the surface. "He acted on his own. He—"

The geometry of interrogation—I'd learned it not from any manual but from a lifetime of being interrogated, of sitting in police stations and social workers' offices and courtrooms, reading the spatial dynamics of rooms designed to make you confess.

Asha's hands tightened on her pencil. The knuckles whitened. A micro-expression—barely visible, lasting less than a second—crossed her face. Not surprise. Not relief.

Calculation.

Asha didn't move. Didn't blink. Didn't look at me or at Lena or at Tom or at Elliot. She looked at her sketchpad, at the drawing she'd been making—a face, I could see now, rendered in quick, impressionistic lines. A woman's face. Dark-haired. Familiar.

Priya's face.

"Asha," Tom said. His voice was gentle—the gentleness of a man who had held her while she sobbed, who had put his arm around her and let her collapse against him, who had believed her grief because it was real and who was now confronting the possibility that real grief and real guilt could coexist in the same person. "No."

She opened her sketchpad. Not to the drawing she'd been making—to a page near the beginning, one she'd done on the first day. The view from the kitchen window: the cliff, the ocean, the sky. The fractured, abstracted landscape with hands reaching from the water and faces half-submerged.

She turned the page. The next drawing was different. Not a landscape but a portrait. Two figures, standing close together in a narrow space—a corridor, rendered in tight, claustrophobic lines. One figure was smaller, darker—Priya. The other was taller, broader, with the solid build of an engineer.

Kian.

"I followed Priya down to the lower deck," Asha said. "She went to find Kian. To stop him—or so she told me. But when I got to the engine room corridor, Priya wasn't stopping Kian. She was helping him."

The device.

"I'm saying I saw her hands on it. I'm saying she and Kian were working on it together, in the engine room, in the minutes before the ferry began to sink. The word fell like a stone into the silence.

She paused.

"My sister was."

LENA — NOW

She had been wrong.

She had been wrong about the modification. Wrong about the unknown saboteur. Wrong about the premise that had sustained her for eighteen months—the belief that someone had taken Priya Patel's controlled disruption and weaponized it without her knowledge.

Priya had weaponized it herself.

Priya Patel—twenty-seven years old, bright, reckless, desperate—had sat in the engine room of the Blackwater Ferry with her boyfriend and deliberately configured a device to destroy the vessel. Not as an accident. Not as a miscalculation. As a choice.

And Maisie had died because of that choice.

Lena sat in the armchair and felt the grief—the grief she had locked away, the grief she had converted into purpose and fury and the relentless machinery of investigation—break free of its containment and flood through her like the water that had flooded the ferry. Black. Cold. Patient. Total.

Mommy, the water's cold.

She pressed her hands against her eyes. Her breath came in short, controlled bursts—the breathing technique she'd learned in the months after, when the panic attacks came like weather, sudden and uncontrollable, and the only defense was to ride them out.

"Lena." Nora's voice. Close. Careful. "Lena, are you with us?"

She lowered her hands. The room came back into focus—the glass walls, the gray light, the faces watching her. Tom, solid and watchful. Elliot, documenting. Nora went white.

Not the gradual paling of shock—the instantaneous, total loss of color that comes with a blow to the solar plexus, the body's response to an impact so fundamental that the cardiovascular system reroutes every drop of blood to the core in preparation for collapse.

Watching through the glass walls.

Seeing everything.

"I'm going to the boathouse. Bram should have the radio working by now. We need to contact the mainland. We need help."

"I'll go with you," Lena said.

I looked at her. This woman who had lied to me, manipulated me, studied me, deconstructed my identity with the clinical precision of a forensic accountant auditing a fraudulent return. This woman who had lost her daughter and built a crusade on the loss and brought six strangers to an island to break them open. This woman who was, in her own way, as much a fraud as I was—wearing a dead therapist's name, performing a dead therapist's methods, using transparency as a weapon while hiding more than any of us.

"Fine," I said. "Let's go."

"What about me?" Asha's voice, from the floor. Small again. The vulnerability creeping back, reflexive, automatic—the default setting of a woman who had learned that appearing weak was the most effective form of strength.

"Stay here," I said. "Help Tom with the windows."

"You don't trust me."

"I don't trust anyone. That's not personal. That's survival."

Asha held my gaze for a moment. And in her eyes—in the dark, complicated depths behind the performance—I saw something that surprised me.

Respect.

CHAPTER 39

NORA — NOW

The path to the boathouse was a ruin.

What had been a faint trail through the spruce grove was now a mudslide, the dark earth churned by the storm into a viscous slurry that sucked at our shoes and made each step a negotiation with gravity. The trees were battered—branches stripped, trunks scarred by the wind, the remaining needles wet and dripping, creating a steady percussion that sounded like applause or like rain or like the ticking of a clock counting down to something.

Lena walked beside me. She'd put on shoes—finally, after two days of bare feet, the therapist's affectation abandoned along with the therapist's identity—and a canvas jacket that was too large for her, probably Bram's. Her silver hair was pulled back, her face bare, her expression the focused, interior expression of a woman engaged in rapid, continuous calculation.

"You said Daniel was your lover," she said, as we picked our way through the mud. Not an accusation. A calibration.

"For two years."

"And you believed he was going to leave Sarah."

"Yes."

"Because he told you so."

"Because I wanted to believe it. Which, I now understand, is not the same thing."

She was quiet for a few steps. The spruce grove was dense here, the trees pressing close, their wet branches creating a tunnel that filtered the gray light into something dimmer and greener.

"I liked Daniel," Lena said. "When I met him. At the fundraiser. He was charming—genuinely charming, not performatively. He listened. He asked questions. He made you feel like the most interesting person in the room."

"That was his gift."

"That was his technique. Charm is a tool. It opens doors. It creates trust. It makes people tell you things they shouldn't tell you and do things they wouldn't normally do."

"I know."

"Do you?" She glanced at me. "Because from where I'm standing, it looks like Daniel used his charm to recruit you—not as a partner, not as a lover, but as a piece of his exit strategy.
