"That's what I keep telling myself." He picked up his bag—military-style, olive green, packed tight. "I figure if I survived the Blackwater Ferry, I can survive a fishing boat. The math is on my side."

"Is it?"

"Probably not. I was never good at math."

I almost smiled. Almost.

"There are the others," Tom said, nodding toward the parking lot.

I turned and saw them. Three figures making their way down the dock, as distinct from each other as notes in a chord.

The first was a woman in her late twenties, small and dark, with paint-stained fingers and a canvas bag slung across her body. She walked with the careful deliberation of someone navigating a world that was slightly too loud, too bright, too much. She was looking at the water, and her expression wasn't fear—it was something older and heavier than fear. Recognition.

Behind her, a man in a charcoal suit that cost more than my car. He walked the way expensive men walk—as if the ground beneath him was his and he was merely allowing the rest of us to stand on it. His hair was dark, slicked back, and he was talking into his phone even though I'd been told there was no signal on the island. Force of habit, maybe. Or performance.

And behind them both, another woman. Tall. Blonde. Beautiful in the way that expensive things are beautiful—precisely, deliberately, without warmth. She wore a cashmere coat the color of cream and sunglasses despite the overcast sky, and she moved through the diesel-and-fish-stink of the dock as if she were crossing the lobby of the Four Seasons.

"Quite the group," Tom murmured.

The young woman reached us first. "Hi," she said softly. "I'm Asha. Asha Patel." She tucked a strand of black hair behind her ear, revealing a tattoo on the inside of her wrist—two birds in flight, mirror images of each other. "Are we all...?"

"Ferry survivors," Tom said. "Looks like it."

Asha nodded. Her eyes drifted to the water again. "My sister and I used to love boats," she said, to no one in particular. "Before."

The man in the suit reached us and pocketed his phone. "Marcus Cole," he said, not offering his hand. His eyes moved over us with the quick, categorizing sweep of someone accustomed to assessing the value of a room. I could see him doing the calculation—what we were worth, what we could offer, what we might threaten. "I was told there would be six of us. I count four."

"There's one more coming," said the blonde woman, arriving at the group with the unhurried grace of someone who has never been rushed in her life. "Juliet Marsh." She lowered her sunglasses and surveyed the dock. "Please tell me the boat is more impressive than this dock."

"It's a fishing boat," I said.

Juliet's expression didn't change, but something behind her eyes tightened. "Of course it is."

"We're still missing someone," Marcus said, checking his watch—a Breitling, I noticed, heavy and gold. "And whoever's driving the boat."

As if summoned, a figure appeared at the far end of the dock. Not from the parking lot—from the water side, stepping up from a boat I hadn't noticed moored behind the larger vessels. He was big. Not tall-big like Tom, but wide-big, thick through the chest and shoulders, with hands like dinner plates and a face that had been lived in hard. His dark hair was pulled back in a short ponytail, and he wore a canvas jacket over a fisherman's sweater, both flecked with salt spray.

He didn't introduce himself. He just looked at us, counted heads, and said, "One more."

His accent was faint—Eastern European, maybe. His voice was the texture of gravel.

"And you are?" Marcus asked, with the particular tone of a man who expected everyone to answer to him.

"Bram."

"Bram what?"

Bram looked at Marcus the way a boulder looks at rain. Then he turned and began loading our bags onto his boat without asking permission.

It was another ten minutes before the last member of our group appeared, and he arrived at a jog, laptop bag banging against his hip, curly brown hair wild from the wind. Younger than I'd expected—early thirties, with sharp features and the restless, darting eyes of someone who was always looking for the next thing.

"Sorry, sorry," he said, slightly breathless. "Elliot Greaves. Missed my exit. Twice." He grinned, and it was the kind of grin designed to make you like him instantly, which made me instantly suspicious.

"You're the journalist," Marcus said. It wasn't a question.

Elliot's grin faltered. "I'm a lot of things."

"You wrote that piece in the Atlantic. About the ferry company's safety record."

"Among other pieces, yes."

Marcus's jaw tightened. "And you were on the ferry."

"I was."

"Researching."

"Living. I was commuting." Elliot met Marcus's stare evenly. "The research came after."

There was a silence that lasted three seconds too long. Then Bram said, "Boat's ready," and we followed him like obedient children.

The boat was a sturdy, graceless thing—a working vessel, not a passenger craft. Bram had laid out bench cushions and rigged a canvas shelter over the stern, but the wind cut through it like it wasn't there. We sat in a rough circle, six strangers bound by the worst night of our lives, and watched the Harwick dock shrink behind us.

No one spoke for the first mile.

I kept my eyes on the horizon—the therapist-approved technique for managing nausea on the water. But the horizon wasn't steady. It tilted and rocked, and with it tilted and rocked the memory I kept locked in a room in the back of my mind: the sound of the water coming in, the screaming, the dark.

The cold.

God, the cold.

"You alright?" Tom sat beside me, his voice low.

"Fine."

"You're gripping that railing hard enough to leave prints."

I looked down. My knuckles were white. I forced my fingers to relax, one by one.

"I keep thinking about the math," I said.

"What math?"

"Forty-seven passengers on the Blackwater Ferry. Thirty-one died. That's a sixty-six percent fatality rate." I watched the water. "There are seven of us on this boat, including Bram. Statistically—"

"Don't," Tom said gently. "The math isn't the same twice."

Ahead of us, Calder Island emerged from the gray like a surfacing whale. Dark rock, dark trees, a jagged coastline that looked like it had been torn from the mainland by angry hands. And there, on the island's highest point, perched on the cliff edge like a dare—

The Glass House.

Even from a distance, it was extraordinary. A structure that seemed to be made entirely of light—walls of glass catching the pale October sky and throwing it back, so that the house looked less like a building and more like a wound in the landscape, a place where the world had been peeled open to reveal something bright and raw beneath.

"Jesus," Elliot breathed.

Juliet lowered her sunglasses. For the first time, she looked impressed.

Asha was sketching it already, her pencil moving across a small pad with quick, certain strokes.

Marcus stared at it and said nothing.

And I stared at it and felt something I couldn't name—a pull, almost gravitational, as if the house were not a destination but an inevitability. As if I had always been heading here.

As if the house had been waiting.

CHAPTER 2

NORA — NOW

The dock on Calder Island was newer than the one in Harwick—pressure-treated lumber, stainless steel cleats, a proper gangway with rope railings. Money had been spent here. Money and intention.

Bram secured the boat with the efficient, wordless competence of someone who had done this a thousand times. He unloaded our bags onto the dock and then stood beside them like a sentry, waiting.

"Is someone meeting us?" Juliet asked, eyeing the steep path that wound up from the dock through a thicket of windblown spruce trees.

"Dr. Vane is at the house," Bram said.

"And we're expected to carry our own luggage up that?" Marcus gestured at the path, which was not so much a path as a suggestion made of crushed stone and exposed tree roots.

Bram picked up two of the heaviest bags—one in each hand, as if they were filled with feathers—and started walking.

"Guess so," Tom said, and grabbed his bag.

We climbed in single file. The path was steeper than it looked, switchbacking through the spruce and birch, the air sharp with salt and pine. Below us, the ocean gnawed at the cliff face with tireless appetite. I could hear it—that rhythmic, ancient sound that is somehow both soothing and threatening, depending on what the ocean has already taken from you.

Asha walked ahead of me, her canvas bag bouncing against her hip. She was humming something—a melody I almost recognized, something that tugged at a memory I couldn't place.

"What song is that?" I asked.

She glanced back. "Hmm? Oh—sorry. I didn't realize I was doing it. It's something my sister used to sing. I don't even know the name. She'd just... hum it. All the time. Like breathing." She paused. "I started doing it after she died. Like it transferred to me."

"Your twin sister," I said.

Asha's step faltered, just slightly. "Priya. Yes." She touched the tattoo on her wrist—the two mirrored birds. "Identical. People always asked if we could feel each other's pain. We couldn't. I know that now because I didn't feel anything when she—" She stopped. "Sorry. I don't know why I'm telling you this."

"It's okay."

"Maybe it's why we're here. To say the things we haven't said."

Or to hear the things we haven't heard, I thought. But I didn't say that.

The trees thinned, and the Glass House rose before us.

Up close, it was even more disorienting than it had been from the water. The house was built on three levels, cascading down the cliff face like a frozen waterfall. Each level was enclosed almost entirely in glass—floor-to-ceiling panels held in minimalist steel frames, so thin they were nearly invisible. The effect was of standing inside and outside simultaneously, of being sheltered and exposed at the same time.

Through the walls, I could see everything. The living room on the main level, furnished in pale wood and white linen. A long dining table set for seven. A kitchen with marble countertops and copper fixtures. Bookshelves, a fireplace—yes, a fireplace, somehow incorporated into a glass wall, the chimney rising through the transparent ceiling like a spine.

Below, on the second level, I could make out a corridor with doors—the bedrooms, presumably. And below that, half-buried in the rock of the cliff itself, a third level whose windows were smaller, more opaque. The foundation. The hidden part.

"It's like a dollhouse," Asha said.

"It's like a fishbowl," Marcus said.

"It's like a statement," Elliot said, already taking photos with his eyes, storing details the way journalists do.

Juliet said nothing. But I noticed her hands—she was clenching them at her sides, her manicured nails digging into her palms. For someone who projected such elaborate calm, her hands told a different story.

The front door—the only opaque element of the facade, a slab of dark oak—opened before we reached it.

And there she was.

Dr. Iris Vane was not what I expected. I'd seen her author photo on the back of her book—The Mirror Method—and in the handful of interviews she'd given. In those images, she was severe. Sharp cheekbones, sharp eyes, silver hair pulled back tight. A face that suggested clinical precision, medical authority, the cool detachment of someone who spent her life studying pain without being touched by it.

In person, she was warmer. Smaller. Her silver hair was down, falling past her shoulders in soft waves. She wore a simple blue dress and no shoes—her bare feet on the cold stone threshold struck me as either deliberately disarming or genuinely eccentric. Her eyes were dark brown, almost black, and when they settled on me, I felt seen in a way that was not entirely comfortable.

"Welcome," she said. Her voice was low, textured, the kind of voice that made you lean in. "All of you. Welcome to the Glass House."

She stepped aside, and we entered.

The interior was warm—heated floors, I realized, feeling the warmth through my shoes. The air smelled faintly of cedar and something herbal, lavender maybe. Soft music played from somewhere—ambient, barely there, like the memory of a song.

"Please," Iris said, "make yourselves comfortable. Leave your bags here—Bram will take them to your rooms." She gestured toward the living area, where a low fire burned in the impossible glass-wall fireplace. "Tea, coffee, water on the sideboard. I'll give you a few minutes to settle in, and then we'll talk about why you're here."

She smiled, and it was the kind of smile I'd learned to distrust—the kind that said I know more than you think I do.

"One thing first," she added, her tone gentle but unmistakable in its authority. "Your phones, please."

A beat of silence.

"Excuse me?" Marcus said.

"There's no cell service on the island—I'm sure you've already noticed. But I ask all my retreat participants to surrender their devices regardless. The temptation to check, to scroll, to reach for the familiar—it's a barrier to the work we'll be doing. I need you present. Fully, uncomfortably present."

She produced a wooden box, lined with felt, and held it out.

Elliot was the first to comply, dropping his phone in with the casual ease of someone who'd anticipated this. Asha followed. Tom. Me—I placed my phone in the box and felt the loss of it immediately, like a phantom limb.

Juliet hesitated. Then she removed her phone from her coat pocket, powered it down with deliberate slowness, and placed it in the box as if she were surrendering a piece of jewelry to a mugger.

Marcus was last. He stared at the box, then at Iris. "My firm needs to be able to reach me."

"Your firm will survive the weekend, Mr. Cole."

"You don't know my firm."

"I know that you didn't fly from New York to Maine because your firm is functioning well without you. Phone, please."

Something flickered across Marcus's face—surprise, maybe, at being out-maneuvered so cleanly. He put his phone in the box.

Iris closed the lid. "Thank you. Now—shall we begin?"

CHAPTER 3

IRIS — NOW

She watched them settle into the living room like birds on an unfamiliar wire—cautious, alert, ready to take flight at the first sign of danger.

Good. They should be alert. They should be afraid.

Not of her. Not yet. But of this place, this weekend, the rawness of what she was asking them to do. Fear was a useful tool. It stripped away pretense. It made people honest—or, more precisely, it made their lies louder and easier to identify.

Iris—she had to think of herself as Iris now, had been practicing for months, writing the name until her hand formed the letters without conscious thought, answering to it in the mirror until her face no longer flinched at someone else's name—stood at the head of the living room and studied her guests.

Six of them. Sixteen survivors of the Blackwater Ferry, and she had chosen these six with the precision of a surgeon selecting instruments. Each one for a reason. Each one carrying a piece of the truth.

Tom Rafferty. Sitting near the fire, legs apart, hands on his knees. The body language of a man who took up space unapologetically, who was accustomed to walking into burning buildings and telling people what to do. He was the hero of the official narrative—retired firefighter, saved four passengers, tried to save more. His eyes were the giveaway. Heroes didn't carry that particular quality of haunting. Heroes slept at night.

Asha Patel. Cross-legged on the floor despite the available chairs, her sketchpad open, her pencil moving in small, unconscious circles. The twin who survived. The mirror that had lost its reflection. She was the one Iris was least certain about—the one whose role in this was still unclear. But she had been on the lower deck that night, near the engine room. She had seen something. Iris was sure of it.

Marcus Cole. Standing by the window—always standing, never sitting, as if sitting were a concession to vulnerability. He held his teacup like a prop, hadn't drunk from it. His suit was armor. His confidence was armor. Everything about Marcus Cole was armor, which meant everything about Marcus Cole was defense, which meant Marcus Cole was afraid. The question was: of what?

Juliet Marsh. On the sofa, ankles crossed, posture immaculate. Her beauty was a weapon she'd been trained to wield since childhood—Iris knew this from the research. Juliet's mother, Vivienne Marsh, had been a former beauty queen turned society matriarch, the kind of woman who measured her daughter's waist at the breakfast table and withheld love like a currency. Vivienne had drowned on Deck C of the Blackwater Ferry. Juliet had not cried publicly. Not once.

Elliot Greaves. In the armchair by the bookshelf, already scanning the titles, already looking for information, for angles, for the story. He'd been investigating the ferry disaster since the day after it happened—the shoddy maintenance records, the cost-cutting, the ignored safety inspections. His articles had led to the ferry company's bankruptcy and the criminal indictment of its CEO. He was relentless, brilliant, and hiding something. His survivor's guilt was too polished, too practiced. It concealed a sharper guilt beneath.

And Nora Finch.

Iris's gaze lingered on Nora longest.

She sat in the corner of the sectional sofa, her legs tucked beneath her, her hands wrapped around a mug of tea she wasn't drinking. She was pretty in an understated way—brown hair, brown eyes, a face that was easy to look at and easier to forget. The kind of woman who moved through the world without leaving marks.

Except that Nora Finch was not Nora Finch.

Iris had discovered this three months ago, buried in the details. The real Sarah Finch—Daniel's wife—had died on the ferry. Dental records confirmed it. But the woman who had stepped forward to claim Daniel Finch's estate, who had grieved publicly and photogenically, who had given tearful interviews about her "husband"—that woman was not Sarah. She was someone else entirely. Someone who had seen an opportunity in the chaos and the body count and had reached for it with both hands.

Iris didn't know her real name yet. She would, by the end of this weekend. She would know everything, about all of them, by the end of this weekend.

She owed Maisie that much.

The thought of her daughter's name sent a crack through Iris's composure—she felt it run through her like a fracture in ice, and she held very still until it passed. She could not think about Maisie now. She could not think about the small hand that had slipped from hers in the black water, the weight that had been there and then was not, the silence that had replaced the screaming.

She could not think about any of that until the work was done.

"Thank you all for coming," Iris said, and her voice was steady, warm, exactly calibrated. "I know this wasn't easy. For some of you, getting on a boat was an act of courage. For others, the courage was in admitting you need help. Both are valid. Both are necessary."

She paused, letting the silence do its work.

"I want to start by telling you what this weekend is not. It's not group therapy, at least not in the traditional sense. I won't be asking you to sit in a circle and share your feelings while the rest of us nod sympathetically. That has its place, but it's not what we're doing here."

Marcus shifted by the window. "Then what are we doing here?"

"We're going to find the truth."

Another silence. Heavier this time.

"The truth about the ferry?" Elliot asked, leaning forward.

"The truth about yourselves. About what happened to you that night—not the version you've told investigators, or therapists, or journalists, or yourselves. The real version. The one you've been carrying alone."

"I've told my version," Tom said. "To more people than I can count."

"You've told a version, Tom. Everyone in this room has told a version. But versions are edited. They're curated. They're shaped by what we think people want to hear, and by what we can bear to say." Iris looked at each of them in turn. "The Glass House is designed to make editing impossible. You've noticed the walls."

She gestured at the transparent surfaces surrounding them. The ocean was visible on three sides—gray, churning, vast. The sky pressed against the ceiling. Even the floor in some sections was glass, offering a vertigo-inducing view of the cliff face below.

"There are no curtains here. No blinds. No walls you can hide behind. The architecture is the method. When you can be seen from every angle, you stop performing. It takes time—a day, sometimes two. But eventually, the transparency of this house seeps into you, and the walls you've built inside yourself begin to feel... unnecessary."

"Or we just feel like we're in a zoo," Marcus said.

Iris smiled. "That's the first stage. Resistance. It means it's working."

"What's the second stage?" Nora asked. Her voice was quiet but precise. She was watching Iris with an intensity that might have been mistaken for admiration but that Iris recognized as something else entirely.

She's studying me, Iris thought. The way I'm studying her.

"The second stage is vulnerability," Iris said. "And the third is truth."

"And after truth?" Nora pressed.

Iris held her gaze. "That depends on what the truth is."

CHAPTER 4

NORA — NOW

My room was on the second level, halfway down the corridor of glass. The walls were frosted from waist-height down—enough privacy to change clothes without putting on a show, not enough to feel enclosed. Above the frost line, the glass was clear, and from my bed I could see directly into the corridor and, through the corridor's outer wall, out to the ocean.

I could also see, faintly, the silhouettes of the rooms on either side of mine. Asha was to my left—I could see her moving behind the frosted glass, unpacking her bag with careful, ritualistic slowness. To my right, someone else—Juliet, I thought, based on the height of the silhouette.

The room itself was minimal. A bed with white linens. A wooden chair. A small desk. A bathroom behind the only solid door in the room, and even that was made of wood so pale it was almost translucent, as if solidity were an afterthought.

I sat on the bed and pressed my palms flat against my thighs and breathed.

What are you doing here, Nora?

That was the question, wasn't it? Not the surface question—I'd rehearsed that answer until it was smooth as a stone. I'm here because I lost my husband. I'm here because I haven't slept through the night in eighteen months. I'm here because a renowned therapist offered to help me, and I am desperate enough to accept help from a stranger on an island.

But the real question. The one that hummed beneath the rehearsed answer like a wire under voltage.

What are you doing here, Nora?

You, who are not Nora at all.

I closed my eyes. Daniel's face surfaced behind my eyelids—not the way he looked at the end, which I hadn't seen, which I could only imagine (and did, nightly, in exquisite and unbearable detail), but the way he looked at the beginning. Laughing. Hair falling across his forehead. The gap between his front teeth that he hated and I loved.

"Come away with me," he'd said, that last week. "We'll take the ferry, drive up the coast, find some inn where nobody knows us."

Nobody knows us. That had been the whole point—the foundation of our relationship. Nobody knew us. We existed only in hotel rooms and parked cars and borrowed apartments, in the negative space of his real life, the life he lived with his wife. With Sarah. Dependable, unsuspecting Sarah, who had Daniel's last name and his health insurance and his presence at the breakfast table every morning, and who had no idea that three nights a week, he was in my bed.

I wasn't proud of it. But pride is a luxury, and I'd spent most of my life too busy surviving to afford luxuries.

When the ferry went down, Daniel went with it. So did Sarah. And in the aftermath—the chaos, the hospitals, the bodies being pulled from the water and identified by dental records and jewelry and the contents of their pockets—a gap opened. A space between what had been and what could be. Sarah Finch was dead. Daniel Finch was dead. But someone needed to claim the estate, the life insurance, the brownstone in Brooklyn Heights. Someone needed to be the grieving widow.

And I was already so good at being someone I wasn't.

I'd taken the name the way you take a coat from a rack—quickly, without examining it too closely, trusting that it would fit well enough. I had Sarah's general coloring, her approximate build. Daniel had kept our relationship so secret that none of his friends or colleagues had ever met me, which meant none of them could say with certainty that I wasn't Sarah. His family was small and estranged. Her family was smaller. A few forged documents, a convincing performance of grief, and the gap closed around me like water around a stone.

Eighteen months. Eighteen months of being Sarah-who-goes-by-Nora, the widow, the survivor, the woman who lost everything on the Blackwater Ferry. Eighteen months of sleeping in Sarah's bed and wearing Sarah's clothes and answering to a name that was neither Sarah's nor my own.

Eighteen months of waiting for someone to see through me.

And then the letter from Dr. Iris Vane.

I know that the healing of these six people is interconnected.

What did she know? What did interconnected mean? Was it therapy-speak, or was it something else—a warning, a threat, a hook designed to catch exactly the kind of fish I was?

I'd come because not coming felt more dangerous than coming. If Iris Vane suspected something about me, better to face it on her territory than wait for it to arrive on mine.

A knock on my door—the frosted glass kind, the one that separated my room from the corridor. Through it, I could see a shape. Tall, slim. Not Asha. Not Juliet.

"Nora?" Elliot's voice. "Dinner's in twenty minutes. Iris asked me to let everyone know."

"Thank you."

He didn't leave. His silhouette stood there, one hand raised as if he were about to knock again.

"Can I ask you something?" he said.

"You can ask."

"Did you know any of them? Before, I mean. Before tonight. Any of the other guests."

I hesitated. The truth was complicated—it always was—so I gave him the simple version. "No."

"Me neither." A pause. "That's interesting, don't you think? Sixteen survivors. Why these six?"

"Iris said our healing is interconnected."

"Right. Interconnected." I could hear the journalist in his voice—the skepticism, the instinct to pull at threads. "What do you think that means?"

"I think it means she has a method, and we're the subjects."

"Subjects. Not patients."

"Is there a difference?"

"Patients are people you're trying to help," Elliot said. "Subjects are people you're trying to study."

He left, and I sat in my glass room and watched the ocean darken as the sun set behind the clouds, and I thought about the difference between being helped and being studied, and whether there was a third option that Elliot hadn't mentioned.

Being hunted.

CHAPTER 5

THE FERRY — THEN

Eighteen months earlier.

The couple caught her eye almost immediately.

They were sitting three rows apart, which was unusual for a couple—most people who boarded together sat together, drawn by habit or obligation or the simple animal comfort of proximity. But these two had separated themselves with deliberate precision, the way magnets push apart when you flip one over. Same poles. Same charge. Repelling.

The woman was in Row 4, window seat. Pretty, in her thirties, brown hair pulled back in a functional ponytail. She was staring out the window at the dark water, her hands in her lap, her fingers working at something—a ring, twisting it around and around her finger like a rosary. Her face was the particular kind of calm that isn't calm at all but rather the aftermath of a storm, the eerie stillness that follows screaming.

The man was in Row 7, aisle seat. Handsome, in a careless way—the kind of looks that came easily and were therefore taken for granted. He was reading something on his phone, his knee bouncing with restless energy. Every few minutes, he glanced up at the woman in Row 4. Not with love. Not with anger. With something more calculated than either.

Monitoring, she thought. He's monitoring her.

Between them, the ferry hummed. The engine was a low, constant vibration, felt more than heard, a heartbeat beneath the floor. The café sold bad coffee and worse sandwiches. A family with two small children occupied the rear section, the kids squirming with the barely contained energy of small bodies in confined spaces. A group of college students played cards. An elderly man slept, his chin on his chest, rising and falling with his breath.

Forty-seven passengers. Twelve crew. A Tuesday evening in November.

Nothing to mark it as different from any other crossing. Nothing to suggest that in forty-three minutes, the water would come in and the world would split in two—before and after—and thirty-one of these unremarkable, precious, irreplaceable lives would end.

She bought a coffee. Found a seat near the middle of the cabin. Watched.

That was what she was good at—watching. Noticing the things other people missed because they were too busy living their lives to examine them. The nervous crew member who checked the same door three times. The maintenance hatch in the floor that didn't sit flush with its frame. The faint, almost imperceptible list of the ferry to starboard that shouldn't have been there on a calm sea.

Small things. Things that meant nothing by themselves but together formed a sentence she couldn't yet read.

The woman in Row 4 stood up. She walked past the man in Row 7 without looking at him and headed toward the restrooms at the back of the cabin. The man watched her go. Then he stood up too, but instead of following her, he went the other way—toward the stairs that led to the lower deck.

The stairs that led to the engine room.

Interesting, she thought.

She sipped her coffee. It was terrible.

Six minutes later, the man came back up the stairs. He was walking differently now—faster, his hands in his pockets, his jaw set. He returned to his seat and picked up his phone and began typing with an urgency that hadn't been there before.

One minute after that, the vibration changed.

The coffee in her cup trembled.

And the water began its patient, inevitable work.

PART ONE: ARRIVAL

CHAPTER 1

NORA — NOW

The dock smelled like diesel and dead fish, and I was going to be sick.

Not from the smell—I'd grown up around docks, had spent summers with salt in my hair and engine grease under my nails. The nausea was something else. Something that lived in my chest now, coiled like a fist, and tightened every time I saw open water.

The Blackwater Strait stretched before me, gray-green and restless, whitecaps flickering like static on an old television. Somewhere out there, three miles off the coast of Maine, was Calder Island. And on Calder Island was the Glass House. And in the Glass House was Dr. Iris Vane, who had promised, in her handwritten letter, that she could make me whole again.

Dear Nora, the letter had begun. Not Mrs. Finch. Not Ms. Finch. Nora. As if she already knew me. As if we were already past the formalities.

I have followed your story since the disaster. I believe I can help you in ways that traditional therapy cannot. My retreat is unconventional, but I have seen it work miracles for people who have survived the unsurvivable. I am inviting only six passengers from the Blackwater Ferry—six people whose healing, I believe, is interconnected. Please consider joining us.

The weekend of October 14th. Calder Island. All expenses covered.

Yours,

Dr. Iris Vane

I'd almost thrown it away. I'd almost thrown it away six separate times, actually, which is how I knew I was going to say yes. The things that terrify us most are the things we can't stop touching.

Now I stood on the Harwick dock with a duffel bag over my shoulder and a bottle of Dramamine in my coat pocket, watching a fishing boat bob against the pilings and telling myself I could still turn around.

"You look like you're about to run."

I turned. The man behind me was tall—well over six feet—with broad shoulders and the kind of weathered, kind face that belongs on the cover of a veterans' charity calendar. Gray hair cropped short. Blue eyes that crinkled when he smiled, which he was doing now, though the smile didn't quite reach those eyes.

"Tom Rafferty," he said, extending a hand. His grip was warm and calloused. "I'm guessing you're here for the same boat I am."

"Nora Finch." My voice came out steadier than I felt. "Dr. Vane's retreat?"

"That's the one." He glanced at the water. Something shifted in his expression—just for a moment, like a cloud passing over the sun. "Hell of a thing, isn't it? Getting on another boat."

"It's a three-mile crossing," I said.

"That's what I keep telling myself." He picked up his bag—military-style, olive green, packed tight. "I figure if I survived the Blackwater Ferry, I can survive a fishing boat. The math is on my side."

"Is it?"

"Probably not. I was never good at math."

I almost smiled. Almost.

"There are the others," Tom said, nodding toward the parking lot.

I turned and saw them. Three figures making their way down the dock, as distinct from each other as notes in a chord.

The first was a woman in her late twenties, small and dark, with paint-stained fingers and a canvas bag slung across her body. She walked with the careful deliberation of someone navigating a world that was slightly too loud, too bright, too much. She was looking at the water, and her expression wasn't fear—it was something older and heavier than fear. Recognition.

Behind her, a man in a charcoal suit that cost more than my car. He walked the way expensive men walk—as if the ground beneath him was his and he was merely allowing the rest of us to stand on it. His hair was dark, slicked back, and he was talking into his phone even though I'd been told there was no signal on the island. Force of habit, maybe. Or performance.

And behind them both, another woman. Tall. Blonde. Beautiful in the way that expensive things are beautiful—precisely, deliberately, without warmth. She wore a cashmere coat the color of cream and sunglasses despite the overcast sky, and she moved through the diesel-and-fish-stink of the dock as if she were crossing the lobby of the Four Seasons.

"Quite the group," Tom murmured.

The young woman reached us first. "Hi," she said softly. "I'm Asha. Asha Patel." She tucked a strand of black hair behind her ear, revealing a tattoo on the inside of her wrist—two birds in flight, mirror images of each other. "Are we all...?"

"Ferry survivors," Tom said. "Looks like it."

Asha nodded. Her eyes drifted to the water again. "My sister and I used to love boats," she said, to no one in particular. "Before."

The man in the suit reached us and pocketed his phone. "Marcus Cole," he said, not offering his hand. His eyes moved over us with the quick, categorizing sweep of someone accustomed to assessing the value of a room. I could see him doing the calculation—what we were worth, what we could offer, what we might threaten. "I was told there would be six of us. I count four."

"There's one more coming," said the blonde woman, arriving at the group with the unhurried grace of someone who has never been rushed in her life. "Juliet Marsh." She lowered her sunglasses and surveyed the dock. "Please tell me the boat is more impressive than this dock."

"It's a fishing boat," I said.

Juliet's expression didn't change, but something behind her eyes tightened. "Of course it is."

"We're still missing someone," Marcus said, checking his watch—a Breitling, I noticed, heavy and gold. "And whoever's driving the boat."

As if summoned, a figure appeared at the far end of the dock. Not from the parking lot—from the water side, stepping up from a boat I hadn't noticed moored behind the larger vessels. He was big. Not tall-big like Tom, but wide-big, thick through the chest and shoulders, with hands like dinner plates and a face that had been lived in hard. His dark hair was pulled back in a short ponytail, and he wore a canvas jacket over a fisherman's sweater, both flecked with salt spray.

He didn't introduce himself. He just looked at us, counted heads, and said, "One more."

His accent was faint—Eastern European, maybe. His voice was the texture of gravel.

"And you are?" Marcus asked, with the particular tone of a man who expected everyone to answer to him.

"Bram."

"Bram what?"

Bram looked at Marcus the way a boulder looks at rain. Then he turned and began loading our bags onto his boat without asking permission.

It was another ten minutes before the last member of our group appeared, and he arrived at a jog, laptop bag banging against his hip, curly brown hair wild from the wind. Younger than I'd expected—early thirties, with sharp features and the restless, darting eyes of someone who was always looking for the next thing.

"Sorry, sorry," he said, slightly breathless. "Elliot Greaves. Missed my exit. Twice." He grinned, and it was the kind of grin designed to make you like him instantly, which made me instantly suspicious.

"You're the journalist," Marcus said. It wasn't a question.

Elliot's grin faltered. "I'm a lot of things."

"You wrote that piece in the Atlantic. About the ferry company's safety record."

"Among other pieces, yes."

Marcus's jaw tightened. "And you were on the ferry."

"I was."

"Researching."

"Living. I was commuting." Elliot met Marcus's stare evenly. "The research came after."

There was a silence that lasted three seconds too long. Then Bram said, "Boat's ready," and we followed him like obedient children.

The boat was a sturdy, graceless thing—a working vessel, not a passenger craft. Bram had laid out bench cushions and rigged a canvas shelter over the stern, but the wind cut through it like it wasn't there. We sat in a rough circle, six strangers bound by the worst night of our lives, and watched the Harwick dock shrink behind us.

No one spoke for the first mile.

I kept my eyes on the horizon—the therapist-approved technique for managing nausea on the water. But the horizon wasn't steady. It tilted and rocked, and with it tilted and rocked the memory I kept locked in a room in the back of my mind: the sound of the water coming in, the screaming, the dark.

The cold.

God, the cold.

"You alright?" Tom sat beside me, his voice low.

"Fine."

"You're gripping that railing hard enough to leave prints."

I looked down. My knuckles were white. I forced my fingers to relax, one by one.

"I keep thinking about the math," I said.

"What math?"

"Forty-seven passengers on the Blackwater Ferry. Thirty-one died. That's a sixty-six percent fatality rate." I watched the water. "There are seven of us on this boat, including Bram. Statistically—"

"Don't," Tom said gently. "The math isn't the same twice."

Ahead of us, Calder Island emerged from the gray like a surfacing whale. Dark rock, dark trees, a jagged coastline that looked like it had been torn from the mainland by angry hands. And there, on the island's highest point, perched on the cliff edge like a dare—

The Glass House.

Even from a distance, it was extraordinary. A structure that seemed to be made entirely of light—walls of glass catching the pale October sky and throwing it back, so that the house looked less like a building and more like a wound in the landscape, a place where the world had been peeled open to reveal something bright and raw beneath.

"Jesus," Elliot breathed.

Juliet lowered her sunglasses. For the first time, she looked impressed.

Asha was sketching it already, her pencil moving across a small pad with quick, certain strokes.

Marcus stared at it and said nothing.

And I stared at it and felt something I couldn't name—a pull, almost gravitational, as if the house were not a destination but an inevitability. As if I had always been heading here.

As if the house had been waiting.

CHAPTER 2

NORA — NOW

The dock on Calder Island was newer than the one in Harwick—pressure-treated lumber, stainless steel cleats, a proper gangway with rope railings. Money had been spent here. Money and intention.

Bram secured the boat with the efficient, wordless competence of someone who had done this a thousand times. He unloaded our bags onto the dock and then stood beside them like a sentry, waiting.

"Is someone meeting us?" Juliet asked, eyeing the steep path that wound up from the dock through a thicket of windblown spruce trees.

"Dr. Vane is at the house," Bram said.

"And we're expected to carry our own luggage up that?" Marcus gestured at the path, which was not so much a path as a suggestion made of crushed stone and exposed tree roots.

Bram picked up two of the heaviest bags—one in each hand, as if they were filled with feathers—and started walking.

"Guess so," Tom said, and grabbed his bag.

We climbed in single file. The path was steeper than it looked, switchbacking through the spruce and birch, the air sharp with salt and pine. Below us, the ocean gnawed at the cliff face with tireless appetite. I could hear it—that rhythmic, ancient sound that is somehow both soothing and threatening, depending on what the ocean has already taken from you.

Asha walked ahead of me, her canvas bag bouncing against her hip. She was humming something—a melody I almost recognized, something that tugged at a memory I couldn't place.

"What song is that?" I asked.

She glanced back. "Hmm? Oh—sorry. I didn't realize I was doing it. It's something my sister used to sing. I don't even know the name. She'd just... hum it. All the time. Like breathing." She paused. "I started doing it after she died. Like it transferred to me."

"Your twin sister," I said.

Asha's step faltered, just slightly. "Priya. Yes." She touched the tattoo on her wrist—the two mirrored birds. "Identical. People always asked if we could feel each other's pain. We couldn't. I know that now because I didn't feel anything when she—" She stopped. "Sorry. I don't know why I'm telling you this."

"It's okay."

"Maybe it's why we're here. To say the things we haven't said."

Or to hear the things we haven't heard, I thought. But I didn't say that.

The trees thinned, and the Glass House rose before us.

Up close, it was even more disorienting than it had been from the water. The house was built on three levels, cascading down the cliff face like a frozen waterfall. Each level was enclosed almost entirely in glass—floor-to-ceiling panels held in minimalist steel frames, so thin they were nearly invisible. The effect was of standing inside and outside simultaneously, of being sheltered and exposed at the same time.

Through the walls, I could see everything. The living room on the main level, furnished in pale wood and white linen. A long dining table set for seven. A kitchen with marble countertops and copper fixtures. Bookshelves, a fireplace—yes, a fireplace, somehow incorporated into a glass wall, the chimney rising through the transparent ceiling like a spine.

Below, on the second level, I could make out a corridor with doors—the bedrooms, presumably. And below that, half-buried in the rock of the cliff itself, a third level whose windows were smaller, more opaque. The foundation. The hidden part.

"It's like a dollhouse," Asha said.

"It's like a fishbowl," Marcus said.

"It's like a statement," Elliot said, already taking photos with his eyes, storing details the way journalists do.

Juliet said nothing. But I noticed her hands—she was clenching them at her sides, her manicured nails digging into her palms. For someone who projected such elaborate calm, her hands told a different story.

The front door—the only opaque element of the facade, a slab of dark oak—opened before we reached it.

And there she was.

Dr. Iris Vane was not what I expected. I'd seen her author photo on the back of her book—The Mirror Method—and in the handful of interviews she'd given. In those images, she was severe. Sharp cheekbones, sharp eyes, silver hair pulled back tight. A face that suggested clinical precision, medical authority, the cool detachment of someone who spent her life studying pain without being touched by it.

In person, she was warmer. Smaller. Her silver hair was down, falling past her shoulders in soft waves. She wore a simple blue dress and no shoes—her bare feet on the cold stone threshold struck me as either deliberately disarming or genuinely eccentric. Her eyes were dark brown, almost black, and when they settled on me, I felt seen in a way that was not entirely comfortable.

"Welcome," she said. Her voice was low, textured, the kind of voice that made you lean in. "All of you. Welcome to the Glass House."

She stepped aside, and we entered.

The interior was warm—heated floors, I realized, feeling the warmth through my shoes. The air smelled faintly of cedar and something herbal, lavender maybe. Soft music played from somewhere—ambient, barely there, like the memory of a song.

"Please," Iris said, "make yourselves comfortable. Leave your bags here—Bram will take them to your rooms." She gestured toward the living area, where a low fire burned in the impossible glass-wall fireplace. "Tea, coffee, water on the sideboard. I'll give you a few minutes to settle in, and then we'll talk about why you're here."

She smiled, and it was the kind of smile I'd learned to distrust—the kind that said I know more than you think I do.

"One thing first," she added, her tone gentle but unmistakable in its authority. "Your phones, please."

A beat of silence.

"Excuse me?" Marcus said.

"There's no cell service on the island—I'm sure you've already noticed. But I ask all my retreat participants to surrender their devices regardless. The temptation to check, to scroll, to reach for the familiar—it's a barrier to the work we'll be doing. I need you present. Fully, uncomfortably present."

She produced a wooden box, lined with felt, and held it out.

Elliot was the first to comply, dropping his phone in with the casual ease of someone who'd anticipated this. Asha followed. Tom. Me—I placed my phone in the box and felt the loss of it immediately, like a phantom limb.

Juliet hesitated. Then she removed her phone from her coat pocket, powered it down with deliberate slowness, and placed it in the box as if she were surrendering a piece of jewelry to a mugger.

Marcus was last. He stared at the box, then at Iris. "My firm needs to be able to reach me."

"Your firm will survive the weekend, Mr. Cole."

"You don't know my firm."

"I know that you didn't fly from New York to Maine because your firm is functioning well without you. Phone, please."

Something flickered across Marcus's face—surprise, maybe, at being out-maneuvered so cleanly. He put his phone in the box.

Iris closed the lid. "Thank you. Now—shall we begin?"

CHAPTER 3

IRIS — NOW

She watched them settle into the living room like birds on an unfamiliar wire—cautious, alert, ready to take flight at the first sign of danger.

Good. They should be alert. They should be afraid.

Not of her. Not yet. But of this place, this weekend, the rawness of what she was asking them to do. Fear was a useful tool. It stripped away pretense. It made people honest—or, more precisely, it made their lies louder and easier to identify.

Iris—she had to think of herself as Iris now, had been practicing for months, writing the name until her hand formed the letters without conscious thought, answering to it in the mirror until her face no longer flinched at someone else's name—stood at the head of the living room and studied her guests.

Six of them. Sixteen survivors of the Blackwater Ferry, and she had chosen these six with the precision of a surgeon selecting instruments. Each one for a reason. Each one carrying a piece of the truth.

Tom Rafferty. Sitting near the fire, legs apart, hands on his knees. The body language of a man who took up space unapologetically, who was accustomed to walking into burning buildings and telling people what to do. He was the hero of the official narrative—retired firefighter, saved four passengers, tried to save more. His eyes were the giveaway. Heroes didn't carry that particular quality of haunting. Heroes slept at night.

Asha Patel. Cross-legged on the floor despite the available chairs, her sketchpad open, her pencil moving in small, unconscious circles. The twin who survived. The mirror that had lost its reflection. She was the one Iris was least certain about—the one whose role in this was still unclear. But she had been on the lower deck that night, near the engine room. She had seen something. Iris was sure of it.

Marcus Cole. Standing by the window—always standing, never sitting, as if sitting were a concession to vulnerability. He held his teacup like a prop, hadn't drunk from it. His suit was armor. His confidence was armor. Everything about Marcus Cole was armor, which meant everything about Marcus Cole was defense, which meant Marcus Cole was afraid. The question was: of what?

Juliet Marsh. On the sofa, ankles crossed, posture immaculate. Her beauty was a weapon she'd been trained to wield since childhood—Iris knew this from the research. Juliet's mother, Vivienne Marsh, had been a former beauty queen turned society matriarch, the kind of woman who measured her daughter's waist at the breakfast table and withheld love like a currency. Vivienne had drowned on Deck C of the Blackwater Ferry. Juliet had not cried publicly. Not once.

Elliot Greaves. In the armchair by the bookshelf, already scanning the titles, already looking for information, for angles, for the story. He'd been investigating the ferry disaster since the day after it happened—the shoddy maintenance records, the cost-cutting, the ignored safety inspections. His articles had led to the ferry company's bankruptcy and the criminal indictment of its CEO. He was relentless, brilliant, and hiding something. His survivor's guilt was too polished, too practiced. It concealed a sharper guilt beneath.

And Nora Finch.

Iris's gaze lingered on Nora longest.

She sat in the corner of the sectional sofa, her legs tucked beneath her, her hands wrapped around a mug of tea she wasn't drinking. She was pretty in an understated way—brown hair, brown eyes, a face that was easy to look at and easier to forget. The kind of woman who moved through the world without leaving marks.

Except that Nora Finch was not Nora Finch.

Iris had discovered this three months ago, buried in the details. The real Sarah Finch—Daniel's wife—had died on the ferry. Dental records confirmed it. But the woman who had stepped forward to claim Daniel Finch's estate, who had grieved publicly and photogenically, who had given tearful interviews about her "husband"—that woman was not Sarah. She was someone else entirely. Someone who had seen an opportunity in the chaos and the body count and had reached for it with both hands.

Iris didn't know her real name yet. She would, by the end of this weekend. She would know everything, about all of them, by the end of this weekend.

She owed Maisie that much.

The thought of her daughter's name sent a crack through Iris's composure—she felt it run through her like a fracture in ice, and she held very still until it passed. She could not think about Maisie now. She could not think about the small hand that had slipped from hers in the black water, the weight that had been there and then was not, the silence that had replaced the screaming.

She could not think about any of that until the work was done.

"Thank you all for coming," Iris said, and her voice was steady, warm, exactly calibrated. "I know this wasn't easy. For some of you, getting on a boat was an act of courage. For others, the courage was in admitting you need help. Both are valid. Both are necessary."

She paused, letting the silence do its work.

"I want to start by telling you what this weekend is not. It's not group therapy, at least not in the traditional sense. I won't be asking you to sit in a circle and share your feelings while the rest of us nod sympathetically. That has its place, but it's not what we're doing here."

Marcus shifted by the window. "Then what are we doing here?"

"We're going to find the truth."

Another silence. Heavier this time.

"The truth about the ferry?" Elliot asked, leaning forward.

"The truth about yourselves. About what happened to you that night—not the version you've told investigators, or therapists, or journalists, or yourselves. The real version. The one you've been carrying alone."

"I've told my version," Tom said. "To more people than I can count."

"You've told a version, Tom. Everyone in this room has told a version. But versions are edited. They're curated. They're shaped by what we think people want to hear, and by what we can bear to say." Iris looked at each of them in turn. "The Glass House is designed to make editing impossible. You've noticed the walls."

She gestured at the transparent surfaces surrounding them. The ocean was visible on three sides—gray, churning, vast. The sky pressed against the ceiling. Even the floor in some sections was glass, offering a vertigo-inducing view of the cliff face below.

"There are no curtains here. No blinds. No walls you can hide behind. The architecture is the method. When you can be seen from every angle, you stop performing. It takes time—a day, sometimes two. But eventually, the transparency of this house seeps into you, and the walls you've built inside yourself begin to feel... unnecessary."

"Or we just feel like we're in a zoo," Marcus said.

Iris smiled. "That's the first stage. Resistance. It means it's working."

"What's the second stage?" Nora asked. Her voice was quiet but precise. She was watching Iris with an intensity that might have been mistaken for admiration but that Iris recognized as something else entirely.

She's studying me, Iris thought. The way I'm studying her.

"The second stage is vulnerability," Iris said. "And the third is truth."

"And after truth?" Nora pressed.

Iris held her gaze. "That depends on what the truth is."

CHAPTER 4

NORA — NOW

My room was on the second level, halfway down the corridor of glass. The walls were frosted from waist-height down—enough privacy to change clothes without putting on a show, not enough to feel enclosed. Above the frost line, the glass was clear, and from my bed I could see directly into the corridor and, through the corridor's outer wall, out to the ocean.

I could also see, faintly, the silhouettes of the rooms on either side of mine. Asha was to my left—I could see her moving behind the frosted glass, unpacking her bag with careful, ritualistic slowness. To my right, someone else—Juliet, I thought, based on the height of the silhouette.

The room itself was minimal. A bed with white linens. A wooden chair. A small desk. A bathroom behind the only solid door in the room, and even that was made of wood so pale it was almost translucent, as if solidity were an afterthought.

I sat on the bed and pressed my palms flat against my thighs and breathed.

What are you doing here, Nora?

That was the question, wasn't it? Not the surface question—I'd rehearsed that answer until it was smooth as a stone. I'm here because I lost my husband. I'm here because I haven't slept through the night in eighteen months. I'm here because a renowned therapist offered to help me, and I am desperate enough to accept help from a stranger on an island.

But the real question. The one that hummed beneath the rehearsed answer like a wire under voltage.

What are you doing here, Nora?

You, who are not Nora at all.

I closed my eyes. Daniel's face surfaced behind my eyelids—not the way he looked at the end, which I hadn't seen, which I could only imagine (and did, nightly, in exquisite and unbearable detail), but the way he looked at the beginning. Laughing. Hair falling across his forehead. The gap between his front teeth that he hated and I loved.

"Come away with me," he'd said, that last week. "We'll take the ferry, drive up the coast, find some inn where nobody knows us."

Nobody knows us. That had been the whole point—the foundation of our relationship. Nobody knew us. We existed only in hotel rooms and parked cars and borrowed apartments, in the negative space of his real life, the life he lived with his wife. With Sarah. Dependable, unsuspecting Sarah, who had Daniel's last name and his health insurance and his presence at the breakfast table every morning, and who had no idea that three nights a week, he was in my bed.

I wasn't proud of it. But pride is a luxury, and I'd spent most of my life too busy surviving to afford luxuries.

When the ferry went down, Daniel went with it. So did Sarah. And in the aftermath—the chaos, the hospitals, the bodies being pulled from the water and identified by dental records and jewelry and the contents of their pockets—a gap opened. A space between what had been and what could be. Sarah Finch was dead. Daniel Finch was dead. But someone needed to claim the estate, the life insurance, the brownstone in Brooklyn Heights. Someone needed to be the grieving widow.

And I was already so good at being someone I wasn't.

I'd taken the name the way you take a coat from a rack—quickly, without examining it too closely, trusting that it would fit well enough. I had Sarah's general coloring, her approximate build. Daniel had kept our relationship so secret that none of his friends or colleagues had ever met me, which meant none of them could say with certainty that I wasn't Sarah. His family was small and estranged. Her family was smaller. A few forged documents, a convincing performance of grief, and the gap closed around me like water around a stone.

Eighteen months. Eighteen months of being Sarah-who-goes-by-Nora, the widow, the survivor, the woman who lost everything on the Blackwater Ferry. Eighteen months of sleeping in Sarah's bed and wearing Sarah's clothes and answering to a name that was neither Sarah's nor my own.

Eighteen months of waiting for someone to see through me.

And then the letter from Dr. Iris Vane.

I know that the healing of these six people is interconnected.

What did she know? What did interconnected mean? Was it therapy-speak, or was it something else—a warning, a threat, a hook designed to catch exactly the kind of fish I was?

I'd come because not coming felt more dangerous than coming. If Iris Vane suspected something about me, better to face it on her territory than wait for it to arrive on mine.

A knock on my door—the frosted glass kind, the one that separated my room from the corridor. Through it, I could see a shape. Tall, slim. Not Asha. Not Juliet.

"Nora?" Elliot's voice. "Dinner's in twenty minutes. Iris asked me to let everyone know."

"Thank you."

He didn't leave. His silhouette stood there, one hand raised as if he were about to knock again.

"Can I ask you something?" he said.

"You can ask."

"Did you know any of them? Before, I mean. Before tonight. Any of the other guests."

I hesitated. The truth was complicated—it always was—so I gave him the simple version. "No."

"Me neither." A pause. "That's interesting, don't you think? Sixteen survivors. Why these six?"

"Iris said our healing is interconnected."

"Right. Interconnected." I could hear the journalist in his voice—the skepticism, the instinct to pull at threads. "What do you think that means?"

"I think it means she has a method, and we're the subjects."

"Subjects. Not patients."

"Is there a difference?"

"Patients are people you're trying to help," Elliot said. "Subjects are people you're trying to study."

He left, and I sat in my glass room and watched the ocean darken as the sun set behind the clouds, and I thought about the difference between being helped and being studied, and whether there was a third option that Elliot hadn't mentioned.

Being hunted.

CHAPTER 5

THE FERRY — THEN

Eighteen months earlier.

The couple caught her eye almost immediately.

They were sitting three rows apart, which was unusual for a couple—most people who boarded together sat together, drawn by habit or obligation or the simple animal comfort of proximity. But these two had separated themselves with deliberate precision, the way magnets push apart when you flip one over. Same poles. Same charge. Repelling.

The woman was in Row 4, window seat. Pretty, in her thirties, brown hair pulled back in a functional ponytail. She was staring out the window at the dark water, her hands in her lap, her fingers working at something—a ring, twisting it around and around her finger like a rosary. Her face was the particular kind of calm that isn't calm at all but rather the aftermath of a storm, the eerie stillness that follows screaming.

The man was in Row 7, aisle seat. Handsome, in a careless way—the kind of looks that came easily and were therefore taken for granted. He was reading something on his phone, his knee bouncing with restless energy. Every few minutes, he glanced up at the woman in Row 4. Not with love. Not with anger. With something more calculated than either.

Monitoring, she thought. He's monitoring her.

Between them, the ferry hummed. The engine was a low, constant vibration, felt more than heard, a heartbeat beneath the floor. The café sold bad coffee and worse sandwiches. A family with two small children occupied the rear section, the kids squirming with the barely contained energy of small bodies in confined spaces. A group of college students played cards. An elderly man slept, his chin on his chest, rising and falling with his breath.

Forty-seven passengers. Twelve crew. A Tuesday evening in November.

Nothing to mark it as different from any other crossing. Nothing to suggest that in forty-three minutes, the water would come in and the world would split in two—before and after—and thirty-one of these unremarkable, precious, irreplaceable lives would end.

She bought a coffee. Found a seat near the middle of the cabin. Watched.

That was what she was good at—watching. Noticing the things other people missed because they were too busy living their lives to examine them. The nervous crew member who checked the same door three times. The maintenance hatch in the floor that didn't sit flush with its frame. The faint, almost imperceptible list of the ferry to starboard that shouldn't have been there on a calm sea.

Small things. Things that meant nothing by themselves but together formed a sentence she couldn't yet read.

The woman in Row 4 stood up. She walked past the man in Row 7 without looking at him and headed toward the restrooms at the back of the cabin. The man watched her go. Then he stood up too, but instead of following her, he went the other way—toward the stairs that led to the lower deck.

The stairs that led to the engine room.

Interesting, she thought.

She sipped her coffee. It was terrible.

Six minutes later, the man came back up the stairs. He was walking differently now—faster, his hands in his pockets, his jaw set. He returned to his seat and picked up his phone and began typing with an urgency that hadn't been there before.

One minute after that, the vibration changed.

The coffee in her cup trembled.

And the water began its patient, inevitable work.

CHAPTER 6

NORA — NOW

Dinner was served at a table made of glass.

Of course it was. Everything in this house was glass or transparency or the absence of walls—every surface designed to remind you that you were visible, that hiding was not an option, that whatever you carried inside you was only one layer of frost away from being seen.

The table was long and rectangular, set for seven. White plates, heavy silverware, cloth napkins in a shade of gray that matched the ocean outside. Candles in low glass holders cast warm circles of light that did nothing to soften the darkness pressing against the walls. Beyond the glass, the night was total. No moon. No stars. Just the black Atlantic and the sound of waves hitting the cliff face sixty feet below us like a fist against a door.

Iris sat at the head. Bram had prepared the meal—roasted chicken, root vegetables, a salad of bitter greens—and had retreated to wherever Bram retreated to. The kitchen, presumably. Or the shadows. He seemed equally at home in both.

"Let's eat first," Iris said. "Talk after. There's nothing worse than cold food and hot emotions."

So we ate. And in the eating, I watched.

Tom ate the way former first responders eat—quickly, efficiently, without tasting. A man accustomed to meals that could be interrupted at any moment by a siren or a scream. He cleaned his plate in eight minutes and then sat back, hands folded, waiting. Patient.

Asha barely ate at all. She pushed food around her plate in slow, deliberate patterns—a piece of carrot here, a leaf of arugula there—creating arrangements that were more art than appetite. Twice, she started to lift her fork to her mouth and then set it down again, as if the act of feeding herself required a permission she hadn't been granted.

Marcus ate with the performative pleasure of a man accustomed to business dinners. He complimented the chicken. He complimented the wine—a Sancerre, crisp and flinty, the kind of bottle that costs sixty dollars and tastes like mineral water with ambition. He was performing normalcy, and performing it well, but his eyes never stopped moving. Scanning the table, the room, the dark beyond the glass. Cataloging exits.

Juliet ate precisely three bites of chicken, no carrot, no potato, and all of her salad. She drank water, not wine. Her self-control was so absolute it functioned as its own form of violence—a war waged against appetite, against need, against the body's insistence on being fed. I wondered how long she'd been fighting that war. I wondered if she was winning.

Elliot ate everything and asked for seconds. He was the kind of person whose nervous energy burned calories faster than he could consume them—always moving, always thinking, his mind a furnace that required constant fuel. Between bites, he made notes in a small leather journal he'd produced from his back pocket. Not a phone. Analog. Iris hadn't confiscated notebooks.

And I—I ate slowly, carefully, the way I did everything now. Measuring each bite. Tasting each flavor. Being present in the act because presence was performance and performance was survival. This is what a normal person looks like when they eat dinner, I told myself. This is what Sarah Finch looks like. This is what a widow looks like. Composed. Graceful. Sad but healing. Eat another bite. Good. Now sip your wine. Good.

The plates were cleared. The candles burned lower. And Iris folded her hands on the glass table and said, "Now. Let's begin."

The shift in the room was immediate. Spines straightened. Breath shortened. The pleasant numbness of food and wine evaporated, replaced by the sharp, cold awareness of being watched by someone who watched for a living.

"I'd like to start with a simple exercise," Iris said. "I'm going to ask each of you one question. Just one. You can answer it honestly, or you can lie. But I want you to understand something." She paused, letting the silence build. "In this house, lies are visible. They show in your hands, your posture, your breathing, the micro-expressions you don't know you're making. I've spent thirty years studying deception. I am very, very good at seeing it."

She smiled, and it was the warmest, most terrifying smile I had ever seen.

"So by all means—lie to me. It will tell me more than the truth ever could."

No one spoke. The waves crashed below us.

"Tom," Iris said. "Let's start with you."

Tom sat forward. His blue eyes were steady. "Shoot."

"You saved four people that night. You pulled them from the water. You performed CPR on a twelve-year-old boy for eleven minutes until the Coast Guard arrived. You are, by every measure, a hero." Iris tilted her head. "My question is: Who didn't you save?"

The color left Tom's face. Not gradually—all at once, like a tide going out. His jaw worked, the muscles in his neck tightening, and for a long moment, the only sound was the ocean and the soft hiss of the candles.

"I couldn't save everyone," he said. His voice was rough. "No one could have."

"That's not what I asked."

"You asked who I didn't save."

"Yes."

"Thirty-one people. I didn't save thirty-one people."

"But there's one in particular, isn't there? One face. One name. The one you see when you close your eyes."

Tom's hands, resting on the table, curled into fists. Slowly. Deliberately. As if he were gathering something invisible and holding it tight.

"Her name was Claire," he said. "Claire Davenport. She was twenty-six. She was trapped under a beam on Deck C. The water was coming in fast—knee-deep, then waist-deep. I could hear her. She was calling for help. But the beam was steel, and it had fallen at an angle, and I couldn't—" His voice broke. He paused. Rebuilt it. "I couldn't move it. Not alone. And there was no one else. Everyone else was already in the water or already dead. I tried for—I don't know. Three minutes. Five. The water reached her chest. She looked at me, and she said, 'It's okay. Go.' And I—"

He stopped.

"You went," Iris said softly.

"I went."

The silence that followed was not empty. It was full of Claire Davenport—her voice, her face, the weight of the beam that wouldn't move and the water that wouldn't stop.

"Thank you, Tom," Iris said. And then, without pause, without giving the room time to recover: "Asha."

Asha looked up from her plate, where she'd been tracing patterns in a smear of sauce with her fingertip. Her dark eyes were wide, luminous. Exposed.

"Your twin sister, Priya, died on Deck B," Iris said. "You were both on the lower level when the water began coming in. You were separated in the chaos. The official report says Priya was found in the engine room corridor, which was fully submerged within the first four minutes. You were found on the stairwell, one level up."

Iris leaned forward.

"My question is: Why were you and Priya on the lower deck at all? Passengers weren't allowed down there."

Asha's hand stopped moving. The patterns on her plate froze mid-stroke.

"We got lost," she said.

"Lost."

"We were looking for the restroom. We went through the wrong door."

"The door to the lower deck was clearly marked as crew only. It required a keycard to open."

"It was propped open. Someone had left it propped open."

Iris held Asha's gaze. Three seconds. Five. Then she nodded, once, and moved on.

"Marcus."

Marcus was already braced. He stood slightly straighter, his hands in his pockets, his chin lifted. The posture of a man about to be cross-examined.

"You were traveling alone that night," Iris said. "According to the passenger manifest, you booked your ticket three hours before departure. A last-minute trip. Unusual for a man who, by all accounts, schedules every minute of his day with military precision."

"I had a meeting on the island," Marcus said. "It came together quickly."

"A meeting. With whom?"

"A client."

"At nine o'clock on a Tuesday night? On an island with a population of two hundred?"

"My clients value discretion."

"Clearly." Iris paused. "My question isn't about the meeting, Marcus. My question is this: The ferry company, Blackwater Marine Transit, was a subsidiary of Hargrove Holdings. Hargrove Holdings was, at the time of the disaster, a client of your firm, Cole & Associates. You were their primary financial advisor."

The room shifted. Elliot sat up straighter. Even Juliet turned her head.

Marcus's expression didn't change. Not a muscle. Not a flicker. The stillness was so complete it was almost inhuman.

"That's not a question," he said.

"You're right. Here's the question: Did you know the ferry was unsafe before you boarded it?"

"No."

"You managed the books for a company that owned the ferry line. A company that had been cutting maintenance budgets for three consecutive years. A company whose internal memos—which Elliot here obtained and published—explicitly discussed the 'acceptable risk threshold' of deferred maintenance on their fleet. And you didn't know?"

"I managed their investment portfolio. Not their operations."

"But you had access."

"Access isn't knowledge."

Iris smiled. That warm, terrible smile. "You're right. Access is just opportunity."

She turned to Juliet before Marcus could respond.

"Juliet. Your mother, Vivienne Marsh, was on the ferry that night. She died on Deck C. You survived. You were found on the upper deck, dry, uninjured. You hadn't gone below to help. You hadn't searched for her."

Juliet's expression was porcelain. Beautiful and blank and giving nothing away.

"The water came too fast," Juliet said. "By the time I understood what was happening, the lower decks were already flooding. Going down would have been suicide."

"Other passengers went down. Tom went down. He saved four people."

"Tom is a firefighter. I sell real estate."

"Your mother was drowning."

"My mother was already dead."

The words landed like stones in still water. Ripples moved outward through the room.

"How did you know that?" Iris asked.

"I didn't know it. I felt it." Juliet uncrossed and recrossed her ankles. One millimeter of adjustment. Total control. "My mother and I had that kind of relationship. I always knew where she was, what she was feeling. Even when I didn't want to. Especially when I didn't want to." She paused. "When the ferry started going down, I felt her die. I felt the exact moment. It was like a string being cut. And I knew—I knew—that going down there would only mean two dead Marshes instead of one."

"That's very convenient," Marcus said, and immediately seemed to regret it.

Juliet turned to him with the slow, lethal precision of a gun turret finding its target. "Convenient," she repeated. "Is that the word you'd use?"

"I didn't mean—"

"My mother is dead, Mr. Cole. I felt her die. There is nothing convenient about any of this."

"Juliet—" Iris began.

"Ask your question, Dr. Vane." Juliet's voice had dropped to a register that was almost intimate. Almost dangerous. "The real one. Not the warmup."

Iris studied her for a moment. Then: "Your mother's will was changed two weeks before the crossing. The previous version left her estate—roughly fourteen million dollars—to a charitable foundation. The new version left everything to you. Did you know about the change?"

"Yes."

"Did you request it?"

"My mother did many things on her own. She didn't need my encouragement."

"That's not an answer."

"It's the only one I have."

Iris held her gaze for a long beat, then turned to Elliot.

"Elliot. You were commuting. You've said this many times. An ordinary crossing, an ordinary day."

"That's right."

"But you had your recorder with you. Your digital voice recorder. The one you use for interviews. And you turned it on at approximately 5:17 PM—twenty-three minutes before the ferry began to sink."

The room went very still.

Elliot's perpetual restlessness ceased. He became, for the first time since I'd met him, completely motionless.

"How do you know that?" he asked.

"The recorder was recovered. It was in a waterproof case. The Coast Guard has the original, but the audio was included in the NTSB investigation files, which I obtained."

"Those files aren't public."

"No. They're not." Iris didn't blink. "My question, Elliot: Why did you turn on your recorder twenty-three minutes before the disaster? What were you expecting to hear?"

Elliot's mouth opened. Closed. He reached for his wine glass, realized it was empty, and set it down again.

"I turned it on by accident," he said. "I was getting it out of my bag to check the battery, and I hit the record button. That's all."

"Twenty-three minutes of accidental recording."

"Yes."

"During which time, the recording captures you leaving your seat, walking to the crew area, and having a conversation with someone whose voice has never been identified. A conversation in which you say, and I quote: 'Is it going to happen tonight?'"

Dead silence.

Elliot's face had gone the color of old paper. His eyes darted around the table—not in guilt, I thought, but in calculation. He was assessing who had heard this, who believed it, what it meant for the narrative he'd constructed.

"That's out of context," he said.

"Then give me the context."

"I was—" He stopped. Started again. "I was working on a story. Not the ferry story. A different story. About smuggling operations on the island ferries. I had a source. A crew member. I was meeting them to discuss whether a drop was going to happen that night."

"And this source—do they have a name?"

"I protect my sources."

"Your source may be dead, Elliot. Thirty-one people died. Including seven crew members."

"My source survived."

"Then their name shouldn't be dangerous to share."

"Every source's name is dangerous to share. That's why they're called sources."

Iris let it sit. She didn't press, didn't push. She simply turned to me.

My turn.

"Nora," she said. "You lost your husband, Daniel, that night."

"Yes."

"You'd been married for three years."

"Four." The lie came easily. I'd told it so many times it had calcified into something that felt like truth.

"You were both on the ferry. You were found on the upper deck. Daniel was found on Deck C, near the bow. The official cause of death was drowning."

"Yes."

"My question—" Iris paused. In that pause, I felt the air change. The way the air changes before a storm, before a blow, before the doctor says the word you've been dreading. "My question is about your marriage, Nora. Specifically, about the three months before the crossing. During which time, your husband withdrew forty-seven thousand dollars from your joint savings account in a series of small cash withdrawals. No explanation. No paper trail."

My heart stopped. Not figuratively—for one instant, I felt the muscle in my chest seize, as if a hand had reached through my ribs and squeezed.

The money. She knew about the money.

I hadn't known about the withdrawals until after Daniel died—after I'd become Sarah, after I'd gained access to the accounts and the records and the full landscape of the life I'd stolen. Forty-seven thousand dollars, withdrawn in increments of two and three thousand over twelve weeks. Gone. No trace.

"I didn't know about it until after he died," I said. This, at least, was true.

"And when you discovered it—what did you think?"

"I thought a lot of things."

"Such as?"

Such as: Was this why he wanted to leave Sarah? Was this his escape fund? Was he planning to disappear, and was I the fool he was going to disappear with? Or was he planning to disappear from me, too?

"I thought maybe he was in trouble," I said. "Financial trouble. Maybe gambling. I don't know. He didn't tell me."

"He didn't tell you that forty-seven thousand dollars was missing from your savings?"

"Daniel and I didn't discuss money."

"Every couple discusses money, Nora."

"Not every couple is the same."

Iris's dark eyes held mine. I could feel her looking—not at my face, but into it, past the skin and the performance and the careful construction of Nora Finch, into whatever lay beneath.

Can she see it? I thought. Can she see that I'm nobody? That Nora Finch is a costume I wear, and underneath it there's nothing—no name, no history, no self? Just a woman who has been pretending for so long she's forgotten what she's pretending to be?

"Thank you, Nora," Iris said. And the weight of her gaze lifted, and I could breathe again.

She stood.
