NORA — NOW

We left the hidden room separately—Elliot first, then me five minutes later—and returned to the main level through different routes. The house, for all its transparency, had blind spots. The spiral staircases created moments of invisibility as you turned between floors, and the stone foundation level was entirely hidden from the glass-walled upper levels. As long as no one had watched us descend together, our visit to Iris's secret office would remain our own secret.

For now.

The afternoon session was scheduled for two o'clock. Lunch had been another silent, tense affair—sandwiches and soup that Bram had prepared and left on the kitchen counter before vanishing. I'd eaten standing up, watching the storm through the glass walls. The rain had intensified throughout the morning, and the wind had shifted direction, coming now from the northeast—a weather pattern that, even with my limited nautical knowledge, I recognized as ominous. Nor'easters were the great equalizers of the Maine coast: they didn't care about your plans, your schedule, your need to leave an island by Sunday.

Marcus found me in the library alcove.

It was a small space—three walls of bookshelves and one wall of glass, facing east toward the open Atlantic. The books were curated: psychology, architecture, maritime history, trauma studies. A few novels. A complete set of Oliver Sacks. Everything chosen, I suspected, to reinforce the fiction that this was a legitimate therapeutic retreat.

Marcus appeared in the doorway, his suit jacket finally removed, his sleeves rolled to the elbows. Without the armor of full formal dress, he looked different. Younger. More human. The forearms that his rolled sleeves revealed were muscular, lightly tanned, with a thin white scar running from his left wrist to his elbow—a scar I hadn't noticed before, too straight and too clean to be accidental.

"We need to talk," he said.

"About what?"

"About the fact that you're not Sarah Finch."

The air left my lungs. Not dramatically—I didn't gasp, didn't flinch, didn't give him the visible reaction he was looking for. But inside, the floor dropped away, and for one vertiginous second, I was falling.

Marcus stepped into the alcove and leaned against the bookshelf across from me. He crossed his arms. The posture was casual, but his eyes were anything but.

"Daniel showed me a photo once," he said. "Of his wife. The real one. We were having drinks at his apartment—Sarah was away for the weekend—and he was talking about her, the way men talk about their wives when they're feeling guilty about something. He showed me a picture on his phone. A candid. She was laughing, holding a glass of wine, and the light was catching her face at an angle that showed everything—the shape of her nose, the line of her jaw, the way her ears sat against her head."

He paused.

"Your ears are different."

Of all the things. The nose, the jaw—those were the differences I'd cataloged and worried about and compensated for. But the ears. I'd never even considered the ears.

"Daniel was a client," I said carefully. "Not a friend. You said you met him through work."

"I said a lot of things last night. So did you." Marcus uncrossed his arms and put his hands in his pockets. "Let's skip the part where we both pretend and get to the part where we're honest with each other. You're not Sarah. I know it. You know I know it. The question is: what happens next?"

I had, in my life, been in many dangerous conversations. I'd talked my way out of arrest at seventeen, out of a violent relationship at twenty, out of a fraud charge at twenty-four. I understood the geometry of verbal confrontation—when to advance, when to retreat, when to hold your ground and make the other person come to you.

This was a hold-your-ground moment.

"What do you want, Marcus?"

"Information. You were close to Daniel. Closer than Sarah, obviously—close enough to take her place after she died. Which means Daniel told you things. Things he might not have told anyone else."

"Such as?"

"Such as why I paid him forty-seven thousand dollars three weeks before the ferry went down."

The room was very quiet. The storm was muffled here, insulated by the bookshelves and the stone wall behind them. I could hear Marcus's breathing—controlled, steady, but slightly faster than it should have been.

"You're asking me to tell you something you already know," I said.

"I'm asking you to tell me what Daniel said about it. What he told you. Whether he told you who the money was for."

"The money wasn't for Daniel?"

"The money passed through Daniel. He was an intermediary. I gave it to him because I needed someone clean—someone with no connection to Hargrove Holdings—to deliver a payment."

"A payment for what?"

Marcus hesitated. The hesitation lasted three seconds—an eternity, for a man who moved through the world with such decisive speed.

"A payoff," he said. "Someone at Blackwater Marine had discovered the maintenance issues. The real ones—not the ones Elliot published, which were bad enough, but the deeper ones. "Someone found out and you paid them to stay quiet."

"I paid them to give us time. Time to fix the problems, to bring the fleet up to standard, to avoid the kind of public scandal that would have destroyed Hargrove Holdings and everyone connected to it. Including me."

"That's not a payoff. That's a bribe."

"Call it what you want. The point is: someone at Blackwater took the money and was supposed to delay their report to the Coast Guard by six months. That would have given us time. Six months to refit the fleet, address the safety issues, make it right."

"But you didn't make it right."

"The ferry went down three weeks later. There was no time."

"Because someone sabotaged it."

Marcus's jaw tightened. "I didn't know about the sabotage. Not then. Not until after."

"But you suspect."

"I suspect that the person I bribed—the person Daniel delivered the money to—decided that silence was worth more than forty-seven thousand dollars. I suspect they decided to create a catastrophe that would destroy Blackwater Marine completely, rather than waiting six months for Hargrove to quietly fix its problems. Because a catastrophe means lawsuits, means settlements, means money. A lot more than forty-seven thousand."

"Who did Daniel deliver the money to?"

Marcus looked at me. In his hazel eyes—green at the edges, brown at the center—I saw something I hadn't expected. Fear. Real, unguarded, undisguised fear. Not the controlled anxiety of a man managing a crisis, but the deep, existential terror of a man who has been outmaneuvered and knows it.

"I don't know," he said. "Daniel wouldn't tell me. He said it was safer that way—safer for me, safer for him. He said the less I knew about the contact, the better."

"And you accepted that?"

"Daniel was convincing. And I was desperate."

I leaned against the bookshelf. My mind was racing—processing, sorting, building a framework for what Marcus had told me and testing it against what I already knew.

Daniel had been an intermediary. A bagman. He'd carried $47,000 from Marcus Cole to an unknown person inside Blackwater Marine Transit—a person who had discovered the company's safety failures and had been paid to delay their report. But instead of delaying, that person had used the information—and possibly the money—to orchestrate the ferry's destruction.

A saboteur. A mass murderer. And Daniel had been their connection to Marcus Cole, which made Daniel complicit, which made me—as Daniel's supposed widow—a loose end.

"Does Iris know this?" I asked.

"She knows about the wire transfer. She doesn't know the context—or if she does, she hasn't told me."

"She knows more than you think. She has files on all of us. Detailed files. In a room downstairs."

Marcus's expression shifted. The fear was still there, but something else was joining it—anger, cold and focused, the anger of a man who is accustomed to controlling his environment and has suddenly realized he's not in control.

"Show me," he said.

"No. Not yet." I held up a hand. "If Iris knows we've seen her files, she'll accelerate whatever she's planning. We need time. We need to figure out who the saboteur is before she does—or before the saboteur figures out how close she is."

"You think the saboteur is here? In this house?"

"Iris does. And I'm starting to think she's right."

Marcus was quiet for a long moment. Then he said something I didn't expect.

"You should know that Daniel talked about you."

My heart stuttered. "What?"

"Not by name. Never by name. But he talked about someone—someone he was seeing, someone he was in love with. He said she was the most perceptive person he'd ever met. He paused.

"He said she scared him sometimes. Because she saw everything and gave away nothing. He said being with her was like being with a mirror that only reflected outward."

I didn't speak. I couldn't. Daniel's voice was in my head—his real voice, not the one I'd constructed from memory and grief, but the one that existed only in the past, in hotel rooms and parked cars and the narrow margins of his double life.

You see too much, he'd said once, lying beside me in the dark. It's like being with someone who has X-ray vision. You look at me and I feel naked.

You are naked, I'd said.

Not that kind of naked.

"He loved you," Marcus said. "For whatever that's worth."

"It's worth nothing," I said. "He's dead."

"Then it's worth everything. Because the dead can't change their minds."

He left the alcove. I stayed, leaning against the bookshelf, my hands shaking in a way I couldn't control. The storm pressed against the glass wall, rain streaming down in sheets, turning the world outside into a watercolor blur of gray and green and black.

Daniel had loved me. Daniel had used me. Daniel had been carrying forty-seven thousand dollars in hush money for a man who was trying to cover up the negligence that would eventually kill thirty-one people.

And the person he'd delivered that money to—the unnamed contact inside Blackwater Marine—was, if Iris was right, a murderer.

A murderer who was in this house.

The room was completely still. The storm raged outside, rain hammering the glass, wind howling around the corners of the house. But inside, the stillness was total—the kind of stillness that precedes an explosion, when all the energy in the room is directed inward, compressed, waiting.

"That's why we were on the lower deck," Asha said. "That night. On the ferry. Priya went down to find Kian. To stop him. To talk him out of whatever he'd been asked to do. And I went with her because I always went with her. Because that's what twins do."

She looked at her hands. At the broken pencil halves she'd placed on the counter that morning, parallel, like rails.

"I didn't stop her," she said. "I knew she was in danger. I knew Kian was in danger. I knew something terrible was going to happen, and I didn't stop her. I followed her into the dark, and the dark swallowed her, and I survived, and she didn't, and I don't know if she stopped him or if he did what they asked or if none of it mattered because the water came too fast—"

She stopped. Her shoulders shook. She pressed her hands against her face and wept—not the controlled, dignified tears of the morning, but deep, convulsive sobs that shook her entire body, that came from a place so far inside her that the sound of them was barely human.

Tom moved first. He got up from his chair and crossed the room and sat down beside Asha on the floor and put his arm around her, and she collapsed against him—this small, fierce, broken woman—and sobbed into his flannel shirt while the storm beat against the glass and the fire crackled and the rest of us sat in the wreckage of what she'd just revealed.

Forty-seven thousand dollars. A blackmail scheme turned into a weapon. A couple used as pawns. A ferry sabotaged not by accident, not by negligence, but by design—by someone who had taken Priya and Kian's small, desperate crime and turned it into a massacre.

I looked at Marcus. His face was gray. His hands, in his pockets, were balled into fists.

He hadn't known. Or he had known and was performing ignorance with the same polished expertise he brought to everything. I couldn't tell. And the inability to tell—the uncertainty, the not-knowing—was more frightening than any single truth could have been.

Because if Marcus had known that his $47,000 was being used to buy not silence but sabotage—if he had known that Priya and Kian were being coerced, that the ferry was being systematically weakened, that thirty-one people were going to die—

Then Marcus Cole was not just complicit.

He was the architect.

CHAPTER 21

TOM — NOW

The storm broke the island's last connection to the mainland at 4:47 PM.

Tom felt it before anyone announced it—a shift in the house's ambient sound, a deepening of the silence beneath the wind. The generator changed pitch: a subtle modulation from steady hum to strained whine, the sound of a system working harder than it was designed to.

Bram appeared.

He materialized in the living room doorway the way he always did—suddenly, without warning, as if he had been summoned from a shadow rather than walking through a door. His canvas jacket was soaked, his dark hair plastered to his skull, his face set in an expression of grim practicality.

"Dock's gone," he said.

The room stopped.

"Storm surge took it. Pilings held, but the gangway's in the water. Can't launch or land a boat until it's repaired."

"Depends on the storm. Could be a day. Could be two." Bram paused. His dark eyes moved across the group, resting briefly on each face—assessing, cataloging, the way a guard dog assesses movement in a yard. "We've got supplies. Generator's diesel—tank's full. Water comes from a well. We're not in danger."

"Not from the storm," Elliot murmured. Quietly enough that only Tom, sitting beside him, heard it.

Bram turned and left. No explanation of where he was going. No reassurance beyond the practical minimum. Tom watched him disappear down the corridor toward the foundation level and felt a prickle at the back of his neck—the instinct that had saved his life in a dozen burning buildings, the animal awareness that something in the environment had changed and the change was dangerous.

"Well," Juliet said, from her armchair. "We're stuck."

"We were already stuck," Marcus said. "This just makes it official."

"There's a radio," Iris said. "In the utility room. Marine band. If there's an emergency, we can contact the Coast Guard."

"Define emergency," Elliot said. His voice was light, but his eyes were not.

"Fire. Medical crisis. Structural failure of the house." Iris paused. "Not discomfort. Not anxiety. Not the desire to leave."

"What if one of us wants to leave?" Nora asked.

"Then they're welcome to swim."

The joke—if it was a joke—landed badly. No one laughed. The sound of the storm filled the silence, rain and wind collaborating in a sustained assault on the Glass House that made the walls vibrate and the floor hum and the entire structure feel, for the first time, fragile.

Tom stood. "I'm going to check the perimeter."

"The perimeter?" Juliet raised an eyebrow. "This isn't a military base."

"Every structure has a perimeter. And every storm tests it." He zipped his fleece and headed for the door. "I've seen houses that looked solid until the wind found the weak point. One broken window, one compromised seal, and the whole thing starts coming apart."

"Tom." Iris's voice stopped him at the door. "Be careful on the cliff path. The footing will be treacherous."

"I know how to walk in bad weather."

"I'm sure you do. But this house sits on a cliff edge, and the cliff is limestone, and limestone becomes slick as ice when it's wet. I've lost a goat to that cliff."

"You keep goats?"

"I did. Briefly."

He almost smiled. Then he pushed the door open and stepped into the storm.

The wind hit him like a wall. Not a metaphor—an actual, physical impact that staggered him backward and forced him to brace against the doorframe. The rain was horizontal, driven by gusts that must have been fifty, sixty miles an hour, and the air was so saturated with salt spray that breathing felt like drinking.

He loved it.

This was what he understood—the physical world, the world of force and pressure and resistance. Not the psychological labyrinth of Iris's exercises, not the hall of mirrors that this weekend had become, but the simple, honest violence of weather against structure. He knew how to assess this. He knew how to fight this. He knew how to win.

He circled the Glass House clockwise, hunched against the wind, his boots finding purchase on the wet stone path. The house's foundation was solid—poured concrete anchored into the limestone bedrock, with steel reinforcement visible at the corners. The glass panels were tempered and laminated—he could tell by the slight greenish tinge—and each one was set in a steel frame with silicone gaskets. Professionally installed. Built to withstand coastal weather.

But.

At the northeast corner, where the Gangplank jutted out over the cliff, he found something.

One of the glass panels—a floor-to-ceiling section approximately four feet wide—was cracked. Not storm-cracked. Not impact-cracked from a blown branch or a piece of debris. The crack pattern was wrong for external force. It originated from a single point on the inside surface and radiated outward in a starburst pattern, the way glass cracks when something strikes it from within.

Tom crouched and examined the point of origin. At the center of the starburst, there was a small, circular impression—the kind of mark left by a hard, round object. A ball bearing, maybe. Or the tip of a center punch—the tool firefighters used to shatter car windows during extractions.

Someone had struck this window from inside the house.

Deliberately. With a tool designed for the purpose.

The crack hadn't penetrated the outer layer—the lamination had held—but the inner layer was compromised. If the wind shifted, if the pressure differential changed, if anyone applied force to this panel from either side, it would shatter completely. And a shattered window in a storm like this would turn the Glass House into a wind tunnel. The pressure change alone could blow out other panels, create a cascading failure, turn this beautiful, transparent structure into a death trap of broken glass and horizontal rain.

Tom stood up. The wind screamed around him. The ocean hurled itself at the cliff below.

Someone had done this on purpose. Someone inside the house had taken a tool to this window and created a weakness—a pressure point, a vulnerability—that the storm could exploit. Not an escape attempt. Not vandalism. Something more calculated.

A way to make the house itself dangerous.

A way to ensure that if the storm got worse—or if someone needed it to look like the storm had done the damage—the Glass House could come apart.

Tom pressed his hand against the cracked panel. It hummed under his palm—the vibration of wind against compromised glass, a sound like a held breath, a sound like waiting.

He thought about Claire Davenport. About the beam he couldn't move. About the choice he had made—the choice Juliet had seen, the choice he'd been lying about for eighteen months.

She was right. He had chosen. Not between saving Claire and saving no one—between saving Claire and saving someone else. Someone whose life, in that terrible, impossible moment, he had valued more.

His daughter.

His daughter, Lily, seventeen years old, who had been on the ferry that night because she'd come to meet him at the dock. His daughter, who had been on Deck B when the water came, who had been trapped in the flooding corridor, who had screamed for him—Dad! Dad! DAD!—and he had gone to her. He had gone to her and pulled her out and carried her up the stairs, and while he was doing that, Claire Davenport was drowning on Deck C, calling for help that never came.

He had saved his daughter. He had let Claire die.

And he had told no one that Lily was on the ferry, because Lily was supposed to be at her mother's house, because Tom's ex-wife would have used it as evidence of his negligence, because the custody battle was already brutal and the revelation that Tom had brought their daughter onto an unsafe ferry and nearly gotten her killed would have ended it. He would have lost Lily. Not to the ocean. To the court.

So he'd lied. He'd told Lily to say she wasn't there. He'd told the investigators he was alone. He'd accepted the hero narrative—pulled four strangers from the water, tried to save a fifth, couldn't—and buried the truth so deep that even he, sometimes, forgot it was there.

But Juliet had seen it. Without knowing the details, she'd seen the shape of it—the choice, the guilt, the lie.

And now someone was cracking the windows of this glass house, and the storm was closing in, and there was no way off the island, and Tom Rafferty stood in the rain and the wind and the dark and realized that the most dangerous thing on Calder Island was not the weather.

It was the truth.

And it was coming for all of them.

CHAPTER 22

NORA — NOW

Tom came in from the storm looking like a man who had seen a ghost. Or become one—his skin was the color of wet concrete, his silver hair plastered to his skull, his clothes so saturated that water pooled beneath him on the heated glass floor, spreading in a slow, dark circle like something bleeding.

"We have a problem," he said.

He told us about the cracked window. He was precise, clinical, his firefighter's training taking over—describing the damage in terms of structural integrity, pressure differentials, failure cascades. He didn't speculate about who had done it or why. He laid out the facts the way he would have laid out a fire scene for an investigation team: Here is what I found. Here is what it means. Here is what happens next.

What happened next was this: Iris moved us.

Not out of the Glass House—there was nowhere to go, not in this storm, not with the dock destroyed and the cliff paths slick with rain and the wind strong enough to knock a person off their feet. But out of the exposed sections—the Gangplank, the bedroom corridor with its floor-to-ceiling windows, the main living area where the northeast-facing wall was now a liability.

"The foundation level," Iris said. "The stone rooms. They're the only fully enclosed spaces in the house."

"Underground," Juliet said. Her voice was flat, but I could see the effort it cost her—the suppression of claustrophobia or something deeper, a reluctance to descend, to go below ground, to place herself in a space with limited exits.

"Below ground level, yes. But dry, warm, and structurally sound. The stone walls are two feet thick. They'll hold against anything short of an earthquake."

"And the cracked window?" Marcus asked. "If it blows, what happens to the rest of the house?"

"If one panel fails, the pressure change could compromise adjacent panels," Tom said. "Depends on the wind direction and speed. Best case, we lose one window and the rain gets in. Worst case—"

"Cascading failure," Iris finished. "The house opens up. Like a flower."

"Or a wound," Elliot said.

Iris looked at him. Something passed between them—a recognition, a shared understanding that the metaphor was not accidental. This house, with its transparent walls and its insistence on visibility, had been designed as a therapeutic tool. But tools can be turned. Weapons can be made of anything. And a house of glass, in a storm, was not shelter.

It was exposure.

We gathered what we needed—blankets, pillows, water, food—and descended the spiral staircase to the foundation level. Bram had already been there, preparing. The utility room had been cleared and rearranged: mattresses from the bedrooms laid on the floor, lanterns placed in the corners, the generator humming steadily behind its concrete housing. The storage room had been opened, its supplies accessible. The corridor was lit by battery-powered emergency lights that cast a warm, amber glow against the stone walls.

It should have felt safe. Underground, enclosed, sheltered from the storm. The kind of primitive security that humans have sought since the first cave, since the first night, since the first time the darkness outside became something to hide from.

It didn't feel safe.

It felt like a trap.

Six people—seven, if you counted Bram, who was somewhere in the utility room, his presence marked by the occasional clank of tools and the low murmur of the generator—sealed underground on an island in a storm, with no way off, no way to call for help, and the growing, suffocating certainty that one of us had killed thirty-one people and was not yet finished.

The locked door—the one Elliot had picked open that morning—was now closed again. I noticed it as we filed into the corridor. Closed and, presumably, relocked. Iris's hidden office. Her conspiracy wall. Her eighteen months of obsession, pinned to stone with pushpins and string.

Did she know Elliot had been in there? Did she know I had?

If she did, she gave no sign. She moved through the corridor with the calm authority of a hostess managing an inconvenience—adjusting blankets, distributing water bottles, making sure each person had a space. Her bare feet on the cold stone floor. Her silver hair catching the amber light. Her dark eyes missing nothing.

"We'll be comfortable enough here for the night," she said. "The storm should pass by morning. In the meantime—" She paused, and I saw it again: that flash of recalculation, the chess player adjusting her strategy mid-game. "In the meantime, I think we should continue our work."

"Our work," Marcus repeated. He was leaning against the stone wall, arms crossed, his shirt still immaculate despite everything. The suit jacket was gone—left upstairs, a casualty of the evacuation—and without it, he looked diminished. Smaller. More human. But no less dangerous.

"The confession exercise was interrupted this afternoon," Iris said. "Asha shared something extraordinary. Something that changes the shape of everything we thought we knew about the ferry. I think we owe it to her—and to ourselves—to continue."

"You want us to have group therapy in a bunker during a hurricane," Juliet said.

"It's a nor'easter, not a hurricane. And yes."

"Why?"

"Because we're trapped. Because there's nowhere to go, nothing to distract us, no escape from each other or ourselves. These are ideal conditions for truth."

"Or for violence," Elliot said quietly.

Iris turned to him. The amber light caught her face at an angle that deepened every line, every shadow, transforming her from the composed therapist into something older and harder. A woman carved by loss.

"Violence is just truth that hasn't found words yet," she said. "Give it words, and it becomes something else."

"What?"

"Confession."

CHAPTER 23

IRIS — NOW

She had planned this differently.

The original design—the one she'd spent eighteen months constructing with the same obsessive precision she'd applied to the wall in her hidden office—had been a slow burn. Three days of carefully escalated pressure, each exercise building on the last, each revelation leading to the next, until the accumulated weight of truth became too heavy for anyone to carry alone and the weakest link broke.

The storm had changed the timeline. The cracked window had changed it further. And Asha's confession—that breathtaking, devastating, game-changing confession about the blackmail scheme and the $47,000—had torn a hole in the sequence that Iris couldn't repair. The information was out. The connections were visible. The group was beginning to see the pattern, and if she didn't control the narrative now, someone else would.

Someone who might have very good reasons for wanting the narrative to go in a different direction.

She looked at her guests, arranged along the stone corridor on their makeshift beds and borrowed blankets, and calculated.

Tom: compromised. The Mirror Exercise had cracked him open, and Juliet's insight about the choice he'd made had gone deeper than Iris had anticipated. He was emotional, destabilized, operating from guilt rather than reason. Useful, but unpredictable.

Asha: exposed. Her confession had cost her everything she'd been holding in reserve—the armor of her secret, the protection of her silence. She was raw, open, vulnerable in a way that could be either powerful or perilous. She had given Iris the key piece—the connection between the $47,000 and the blackmail—but she had also revealed herself as someone who had known about the sabotage threat and hadn't reported it. That knowledge made her both victim and accomplice, and the ambiguity was dangerous.

Marcus: cornered. Nora had seen through his armor during the Mirror Exercise, and whatever had passed between them in the library alcove had shifted his allegiance. He was no longer a solitary operator. He was working with Nora now—Iris had seen it in their body language, the way they'd positioned themselves on the same sofa, the glances they exchanged that carried more information than words. Alliance or conspiracy. Either way, Marcus was no longer fully under her control.

Juliet: the wildcard. Iris had expected Juliet to be the hardest to crack—the woman's emotional defenses were the most sophisticated she'd ever encountered, a lifetime of training in the art of invulnerability. But the Mirror Exercise had revealed something unexpected: Juliet's capacity for insight. Her reading of Tom had been surgically precise. She saw people. She understood them. And understanding, in Iris's experience, was the precursor to either empathy or manipulation.

Elliot: the variable she was most concerned about. He'd been too quiet since the lunch break. The restless energy that usually radiated from him like heat from a furnace had gone cold, contained, redirected. He was thinking. Planning. And a journalist who was thinking and planning in a situation like this was either the most valuable person in the room or the most dangerous.

And Nora. Elena. The impostor.

Iris watched her settle onto a mattress near the corridor wall, her back against the stone, her knees drawn up, her brown eyes scanning the room with that constant, quiet vigilance that Marcus had identified during the exercise. She watches. She gathers. She's always two moves ahead.

Iris needed to get ahead of her. Needed to accelerate. Needed to reveal the thing she'd been holding in reserve—the piece of evidence that would detonate the careful architecture of lies and alliances and secrets that these six people had built around themselves—before the storm passed and the opportunity closed.

"Before we continue the confessions," Iris said, "I need to tell you something."

She sat on the floor, cross-legged, the way Asha sat—a deliberate mirroring, designed to create intimacy, to lower the register of authority, to say I am one of you.

She was not one of them. But they didn't need to know that yet.

"I told Nora this morning, and I'll tell the rest of you now. I lost my daughter on the Blackwater Ferry. Her name was Maisie. She was nine years old."

The silence was immediate and total. Even the storm seemed to pause—a lull in the wind, a brief reprieve from the hammering rain, as if the weather itself were listening.

"I have spent the last eighteen months investigating the disaster. Not the official investigation—I've read those reports, and they're thorough in their way, but they reach the wrong conclusion. They conclude that the ferry sank due to cascading mechanical failure exacerbated by deferred maintenance. Negligence, not malice. An accident, not a crime."

She paused. Let the words breathe.

"I believe they're wrong. I believe the Blackwater Ferry was deliberately sabotaged. And I believe the person who did it is in this room."

She had expected shock. She had expected denial, outrage, the reflexive defensiveness of people who have been accused of the unthinkable.

What she got was silence.

A long, pressurized silence in which six people looked at each other and at her and at the stone walls closing in around them, and no one spoke, because the accusation was not, for any of them, entirely surprising.

They had been feeling it. All of them. Since the Mirror Exercise, since Asha's confession, since the moment they'd stepped off Bram's boat and onto this island and felt the gravity of the Glass House pulling them in. They had known, on some level below language, that this weekend was not what it claimed to be. That the therapy was a pretense. That the transparency was a weapon. That they had been brought here not to heal but to be examined, tested, broken open.

And now the pretense was gone.

"The device that was placed on the starboard engine," Iris continued, "was a modified hydraulic actuator. Industrial grade. The kind used in heavy machinery—construction equipment, manufacturing presses, naval engineering. It wasn't a bomb in the traditional sense. It didn't explode. What it did was systematically increase the pressure in the engine's cooling system over a period of approximately twenty minutes, until the system ruptured catastrophically."

She reached into her pocket and produced a photograph. Small, printed on matte paper, taken from a maritime investigation file that was not, technically, accessible to the public. She held it up.

"This is the device. Or what was left of it. Recovered from the wreck site by NTSB divers six weeks after the disaster."

The photograph showed a mangled piece of metal—twisted, blackened, barely recognizable as machinery. But the serial number was visible. Partially. Three digits and a letter, stamped into the casing.

"The serial number led to a manufacturer in Germany. Klein Hydraulik. They make precision actuators for industrial and marine applications. This particular model was sold through a distributor in Portland, Maine, in August of 2018—three months before the ferry went down."

She paused. Looked at each of them in turn.

"The purchase was made with a credit card. The card was in the name of a company called Meridian Consulting LLC. Meridian Consulting was registered in Delaware—a shell company, no physical address, no employees, no tax filings. But the credit card had a billing address. An apartment in Portland."

Another pause. Longer this time.

"The apartment was leased to Daniel Finch."

The name detonated in the silence like a depth charge.

I felt it—felt the concussion wave move through my body, through my chest, through the architecture of every lie I'd built and every truth I'd buried. Daniel. Daniel's apartment. The apartment I'd never been to, the one he'd said was a "workspace," the one he'd kept separate from his life with Sarah and his life with me, a third space, a neutral zone, a place where he could be whoever he needed to be without either of us watching.

Daniel bought the device.

Not directly. Through a shell company, through a credit card, through layers of obfuscation designed to create distance between the purchase and the purchaser. But the trail led to him. To his address. To his name.

"You're saying Daniel Finch sabotaged the ferry," Marcus said. His voice was controlled, but barely. The cords in his neck were standing out like cables.

"I'm saying the device was purchased through a company linked to Daniel Finch. I'm not yet saying who placed it on the engine. Those may be different people."

"Daniel is dead," I heard myself say. My voice sounded far away, as if it were coming from the other end of a long tunnel. "He died on the ferry. Why would he sabotage a vessel he was on?"

"That's an excellent question," Iris said. And she looked at me with those dark, penetrating eyes, and I understood—with a clarity that was almost physical, almost painful—that she already had an answer.

She just wanted to hear mine first.

NORA — NOW

I needed to think.

I needed to be alone, to process what Iris had just revealed, to dismantle it and examine each piece and reassemble it into something that made sense. But there was no alone in the foundation level—no privacy, no space, no glass walls to create the illusion of separation. We were packed together in a stone corridor like sardines in a tin, and every breath I took was shared air, and every thought I had was visible on my face if I wasn't careful.

I was always careful.

But I was reaching the limits of careful. The limits of performance. The limits of being someone I wasn't in a place designed to strip away every layer of protection and leave nothing but the raw, unedited truth.

Daniel had bought the device. Or his name had been used to buy it. Or his apartment had been used as a billing address. What felt real was the weight of it. The accumulation of revelations—the $47,000, the blackmail scheme, the shell company, the actuator—each one adding to the load I was carrying until my spine bowed and my shoulders ached and the floor beneath me felt like it was tilting, slowly, inevitably, the way the ferry had tilted before it broke apart.

I waited until the group fragmented. Iris had called a pause—even she, with her relentless therapeutic agenda, recognized that the revelation about Daniel needed time to settle. People retreated to their mattresses, their blankets, their private silences. Tom sat with his back against the wall, his eyes closed, his hands folded in his lap like a man praying. Marcus pays Daniel $47,000. Daniel converts it to cash. The cash reaches Kian and Priya, through channels I couldn't yet trace. "But it wasn't a sensor."

"No."

"And thirty-one people died."

"Yes."

I opened my eyes and looked at Elliot. He was staring at the floor, his hands clasped between his knees, his shoulders curved inward. The posture of a man bearing a weight he'd been carrying for eighteen months—the weight of knowing, of having been told, of having been on that ferry with information that might have saved thirty-one lives if he'd acted on it differently.

"You could have pulled the fire alarm," I said. "You could have told the captain. You could have done something."

"I had twenty-three minutes. I didn't believe him—not fully. He was scared, and scared people exaggerate. And I was a journalist, not a first responder. My training was to verify, to corroborate, to get the full story before acting. By the time I understood—by the time the vibration started and the lights went out—it was too late."

"Twenty-three minutes," I repeated.

"I know." His voice was raw. "I know how long twenty-three minutes is. I've counted every second of it, every day, for eighteen months. Thirty-one lives divided by twenty-three minutes. That's the math I live with. That's the equation I can't solve."

I sat with him in the storage room, surrounded by canned tomatoes and bottled water and the provisions for a siege that was no longer about weather, and I thought about the things we carry. The secrets that become load-bearing walls—remove them and the whole structure collapses. The lies that become oxygen—stop breathing them and you suffocate.

Elliot's secret was that he'd known. Not enough, not in time, not with enough certainty to act. But he'd known. And the knowing was eating him alive.

My secret was that I was no one. That the woman Elliot was confiding in—the woman Marcus had allied with, the woman Tom had opened up to, the woman Iris was watching with such focused intensity—didn't exist. Elena Carr was a ghost. Nora Finch was a costume. And underneath both names, underneath all the performances and impersonations and stolen identities, there was nothing. A void. A blank space where a person should have been.

"We should go back out," I said. "Before someone notices we're both gone."

Elliot nodded. He picked up his camera, tucked it into his back pocket. At the door, he stopped.

"Nora—or whatever your name is."

I went very still.

"I'm a journalist," he said. "I notice things. I noticed the photos on Iris's wall—the side-by-side comparisons. I noticed the red circles. I know you're not Sarah Finch."

My hand tightened on the doorframe. The stone was cold and rough under my fingers.

"I don't care," he said. "Whatever your reasons, whatever you did, I don't care. Right now, we're in a house with a murderer, and you're the smartest person in the room, and I need you on my side. So your secret is safe with me. For now. Until we get off this island."

"And after?"

He looked at me with those kind, deceptive, complicated eyes.

"After is after," he said. "Let's survive this first."

He opened the door and walked out, and I stood alone in the storage room and pressed my forehead against the cold stone wall and breathed.

CHAPTER 25

THE FERRY — THEN

The final minutes of the Blackwater Ferry were measured not in seconds but in losses.

Loss of light: the emergency strips failed at 5:43 PM, plunging the vessel into a darkness so complete that the difference between eyes open and eyes closed was theoretical. The only illumination was the distant, diffused glow of the Harwick shoreline—a smear of amber on the horizon, three miles away, impossibly far, a mockery of safety visible but unreachable.

Loss of sound: the human sounds—the screaming, the crying, the desperate calls for help—were swallowed by the ocean's roar as water claimed the lower decks and surged upward through stairwells and doorways and the broken windows that Tom Rafferty had shattered with a fire extinguisher. The sea had its own voice, and it was louder than any human throat, and it did not stop.

Loss of structure: the ferry broke apart at 5:47 PM. Not all at once—a ferry is not a glass, dropped and shattered. It came apart in stages, like a body dying. First the superstructure separated from the hull, the welds failing in sequence, each one releasing a sound like a gunshot. Then the bow section twisted and tore free, dragging with it a tangle of railing and deck plating and the bodies of passengers who had been clinging to the forward rail. Then the stern section tilted vertical—straight up, like a finger pointing at the sky—and hung there for three impossible seconds before sliding beneath the surface with a sound that was not a splash but a swallow. The ocean opening its throat and taking everything.

She was in the water by then.

She had jumped—or been thrown; the distinction, in the chaos, was meaningless—from the port-side deck as the superstructure began to separate. The water was forty-one degrees Fahrenheit. Cold shock hit her like an electrical current: every muscle contracting, every nerve firing, her lungs seizing in a gasp that she barely kept above the waterline. The life jacket she'd grabbed from a storage bin on the upper deck held her up—barely—her chin at the surface, waves slapping her face, the taste of diesel and salt filling her mouth.

Around her, the water was full of debris and people. Some alive, some not. The alive ones were making sounds—low, animal moans, the involuntary vocalizations of bodies in extremis. The not-alive ones were silent, floating face-down, their arms spread, their hair fanning in the current like dark seaweed.

She swam. Not toward the shore—too far, too cold, the current pulling northeast—but toward a cluster of debris that was large enough to climb onto. A section of deck plating, roughly six feet square, floating on the surface like a raft. She reached it, hauled herself up, lay flat on her stomach, gasping.

From this vantage point—elevated eighteen inches above the waterline—she could see the full scope of the disaster. The ferry was gone. In its place, a spreading field of wreckage—planks, life jackets, seat cushions, personal belongings, bodies. The life rafts had deployed—two of them, orange, inflated, already crowded with survivors. A third raft was overturned, its bottom visible like the belly of a dead fish.

And there—at the edge of the wreckage field, fifty meters from the nearest life raft—she saw the figure again.

The same figure. The one from the engine room corridor. The one from the stern railing.

They were in the water, but not struggling. Swimming. Steady, purposeful strokes, moving away from the wreckage, away from the survivors, away from the life rafts. Moving toward something. A shape in the darkness that she squinted to identify—low, dark, barely visible against the black water.

A boat.

Not the ferry. Not a life raft. A small boat—a skiff or a dinghy—sitting a hundred meters outside the wreckage field, its running lights off, its engine silent. Waiting.

The figure reached the boat. Hands appeared over the gunwale—someone inside, helping them aboard. The figure climbed in. And then the boat's engine started—a low, guttural rumble, barely audible over the sound of the sea—and it moved. Slowly at first, then faster, heading northeast. Away from the wreck. Away from the dying. Into the dark.

She watched it go.

And in the moment before the boat disappeared into the darkness, a wave lifted her debris raft and turned it slightly, and the distant glow of the Harwick shoreline caught the stern of the retreating boat at an angle that illuminated, for one brief instant, a name painted on the hull.

MERIDIAN.

The name of the shell company. The name on the credit card that had purchased the device. Painted on the hull of the escape boat, as blatant as a signature, as damning as a confession.

Meridian.

She filed it away. Locked it in the vault of her memory, alongside the face she'd seen in the engine room corridor and the figure at the stern railing and the red blinking light of the device that had killed thirty-one people and would have killed her too if she hadn't been paying attention, if she hadn't been watching, if she hadn't spent her entire life preparing for the moment when the world broke and she was the only one left standing.

The cold was taking her. Hypothermia—she recognized the stages. Shivering first, then numbness, then confusion, then a warmth that felt like safety but was actually death. She was at stage two. The numbness spreading from her extremities inward, her fingers useless, her toes gone, the core of her body hoarding heat like a miser hoarding gold.

She lay on her debris raft and stared at the sky and thought about Maisie.

Not the memory she wanted—not the smiling girl with the violin—but the other one. The real one. The last one. Maisie's hand in hers, small and warm and trusting. Maisie's eyes, wide and dark, looking up at her in the flooding cabin. Maisie's voice: Mommy, the water's cold.

And then the hand slipping free. The fingers uncurling. The warmth replaced by the Atlantic, forty-one degrees, black as ink, patient as stone.

Mommy, the water's cold.

She closed her eyes. The numbness reached her chest. Her breathing slowed. The sounds of the disaster faded—the moans, the waves, the distant wail of a Coast Guard siren that might have been real or might have been the beginning of the end.

She didn't die. The Coast Guard reached her fifty-three minutes later, her body temperature at eighty-nine degrees, her heart rate at forty-two beats per minute, her eyes open but unseeing. They loaded her onto the cutter and wrapped her in thermal blankets and called her "ma'am" and asked her name.

She didn't answer.

Because the woman who had boarded the Blackwater Ferry that evening—the woman with the dark hair and the dark eyes and the nine-year-old daughter in the blue velvet dress—had died in the water. Not physically. Not medically. But in every way that mattered, she had died when Maisie's hand slipped from hers, and what the Coast Guard had rescued from that debris raft was not a person but a purpose.

A purpose that would, eighteen months later, bring her to Calder Island. To the Glass House. To six strangers who thought they were attending a therapy retreat.

Her name—her real name, the one she'd buried beneath "Dr. Iris Vane"—was Lena Aldric. She was forty-three years old. She had a degree in forensic accounting from Boston University. She had worked for the IRS for twelve years before transitioning to private investigation. She had a gift for numbers, for patterns, for following money the way a bloodhound follows scent.

And she had a daughter who was dead.

And she was going to find the person who killed her.

CHAPTER 26

NORA — NOW

It happened at 2:17 AM.

I know the time because I had been counting the minutes—a habit I'd developed on the ferry, in the water, in the months after, when sleep was a territory I could no longer safely enter. Counting was control. Counting was presence. Counting was the thin, bright line between consciousness and the dark water that waited on the other side.

The foundation level was quiet. The storm had settled into a steady, sustained assault—less violent than the initial onslaught, but more relentless, a grinding persistence that wore at the house the way the ocean wore at the cliff. The generator hummed. The emergency lights glowed. Six people breathed in the darkness, each one performing sleep with varying degrees of conviction.

Tom was actually asleep—I could tell by his breathing, deep and even, the breathing of a man whose body had overruled his mind. Juliet was motionless on her mattress in the alcove, but I suspected she was awake—her stillness was too perfect, too controlled, the stillness of a woman who was listening rather than resting.

Marcus was on his back, one arm over his eyes, his chest rising and falling with mechanical regularity. Awake or asleep? Impossible to tell. Marcus's default state was performance, and he performed unconsciousness with the same polished expertise he brought to everything else.

Elliot was on his side, facing the wall, his leather journal tucked under his pillow. Asleep, I thought. The emotional toll of the day had drained even his inexhaustible energy.

Asha was—

I sat up.

Asha's mattress was empty.

The blanket was pushed aside, the pillow still indented with the shape of her head. Her sketchpad was on the floor beside the mattress, closed, her pencils lined up beside it in a neat row. Her shoes were gone.

I scanned the corridor. The utility room door was ajar—Bram was in there, I assumed, though I hadn't seen him in hours. The storage room door was closed. The locked door—Iris's hidden office—was closed.

Asha was not in the corridor.

I got up quietly. Bare feet on cold stone. I moved to the spiral staircase that led up to the bedroom level and listened. The staircase amplified sound, funneling it downward like a speaking tube. Through it, I could hear the storm—wind and rain and the occasional crack of a branch or a loosened piece of debris. But beneath the storm sounds, something else.

Footsteps.

Soft, careful footsteps on the heated glass floor of the bedroom corridor, one level above me.

Asha had gone upstairs. Into the part of the house that Tom had declared unsafe. Into the glass rooms with their transparent walls and their exposure to the compromised northeast window. Into the dark.

I should have woken someone. Tom, at least—Tom, who was solid and capable and trained for emergencies. Or Elliot, who was my ally, my co-conspirator, the person who knew my secret and had agreed to keep it.

I didn't. I went alone.

The staircase was cold—the heated floors didn't extend to the stairs—and I climbed quickly, my hand on the steel railing, my feet finding each step by memory and touch. The bedroom level was dark. The emergency lighting up here had failed—storm-related, probably, a circuit breaker tripped by the wind or the water. The only light was the faint, diffused glow of the storm itself, filtering through the rain-streaked glass walls in shifting patterns of gray and silver.

"Asha?" My voice was a whisper. It traveled down the corridor and came back to me, distorted by glass and distance, sounding like someone else's voice.

No answer.

I moved down the corridor. Past my room—the frosted glass door was open, the room beyond dark and empty. Past Asha's room—also open, also empty. Past Juliet's room, Marcus's room, Elliot's room. All open. All empty. The evacuation had left them like abandoned cells, the mattresses gone, the personal belongings scattered.

At the end of the corridor, the door to the main staircase—the one leading up to the living area on the top level. It was open.

I climbed.

The main living area was a different world at night. By day, it was all light and openness—the glass walls admitting the sky and the sea, the space feeling infinite. By night, in the storm, with the emergency lighting dead, it was a cavern. The glass walls were opaque with rain, streaming sheets of water that turned the exterior into an abstract wash of darkness. The fireplace was cold, its embers dead. The furniture was shadows.

And in the center of the room, standing on the glass floor that looked down through the bedroom level and into the foundation level below—standing on the transparent surface through which, if the lights were on, you could see all three levels of the house at once, like looking into the stacked chambers of a heart—

Was Asha.

She was barefoot. She was wearing the oversized T-shirt and leggings she'd slept in. Her dark hair was loose, falling past her shoulders, and her arms were at her sides, and she was standing perfectly still, looking down through the glass floor at something I couldn't see.

"Asha."

She didn't move. Didn't turn. Didn't acknowledge my voice.

I crossed the room carefully. The glass floor was cold under my feet—the heating system had failed with the emergency lighting, or had been turned off. Each step was a small act of faith: trusting the glass, trusting the steel frame, trusting that the transparent surface beneath me was solid enough to hold my weight despite every visual cue suggesting otherwise.

I reached Asha and looked down.

Through the glass floor, through the transparent ceiling of the bedroom level, I could see the corridor below. The frosted glass of the bedroom doors. The faint glow of the emergency lights on the foundation level, one story further down.

And on the floor of the bedroom corridor, directly beneath our feet, a shape.

A human shape.

Lying on the glass floor of the corridor. Motionless. One arm extended, the other tucked beneath the body. Blonde hair spread across the transparent surface like spilled light.

Juliet.

"Asha," I said again, and this time I touched her arm.

She flinched. Turned. Her eyes were wide, pupils blown, the dark irises almost entirely consumed by black. She looked at me as if she didn't recognize me—as if I were a stranger, or a ghost, or a fragment of a dream that had followed her into waking.

"She's not moving," Asha whispered.

I looked down again. Juliet was on her back. Her eyes were closed. Her cream-colored sweater was dark across the midsection—a spreading stain that, in the dim light, could have been water or wine or shadow.

Or blood.

"Stay here," I told Asha. "Don't move."

I ran.

Down the main staircase, through the living area, down to the bedroom corridor. The emergency lights on this level were working—dim, amber, barely adequate—and in their glow, I could see what I couldn't see from above.

Juliet was lying in the middle of the corridor. On her back. Her eyes were closed. Her right arm was extended, fingers curled around something small and dark—a phone. Not her phone—Iris had confiscated those. A different phone. A burner, maybe, or a satellite phone, something she'd brought secretly, something she'd hidden.

The stain on her sweater was blood.

Not a lot. Not the dramatic, spreading pool of violence that movies depict. A contained stain, roughly the size of a dinner plate, centered over her left side, just below her ribs. The blood was dark—almost black in the amber light—and it wasn't spreading anymore. It had stopped.

Which meant either the bleeding had been controlled.

Or her heart had stopped pumping.

I dropped to my knees beside her. Pressed two fingers against her neck. The skin was warm. The pulse was there—faint, thready, too fast. Alive.

"Juliet." I shook her shoulder gently. "Juliet, can you hear me?"

Her eyelids fluttered. Her lips moved. No sound.

I pulled up her sweater. The wound was small—a puncture, not a slash. Clean edges, minimal tearing. A blade, thin and sharp. A knife wound. Deliberately placed—just below the ribs, angled upward, targeting the spleen or the kidney. A wound designed to cause internal bleeding. A wound designed to kill slowly.

"Help!" I shouted. My voice echoed through the glass corridors, bouncing off every transparent surface, multiplying. "I need help up here! Now!"

Footsteps on the stairs. Tom first—always Tom first, the responder, the man who ran toward danger. Then Elliot. Then Marcus. Then Iris, barefoot, her silver hair wild, her dark eyes taking in the scene with a single, sweeping glance.

"She's been stabbed," I said. "Left side, below the ribs. She's losing blood."

Tom was beside me in an instant, his hands moving to Juliet's wound with the practiced efficiency of a man who had treated injuries in the field. "I need towels. Clean towels. And pressure. Something I can use for a compress."

Elliot was already moving. Marcus stood in the corridor, his face the color of ash, his hands hanging at his sides. Not frozen—calculating. Even now, even in the face of blood and crisis, Marcus Cole was calculating.

Iris knelt beside Tom. "How bad?"

"Can't tell without seeing the depth. If it's hit the spleen, she needs surgery. If it's just muscle and tissue, she needs stitches and antibiotics. Either way, she needs a hospital."

"The dock is destroyed. The radio—"

"Get the radio. Call the Coast Guard. Tell them we have a stabbing victim on Calder Island. Tell them we need a helicopter evacuation." Tom's voice was steel. The voice of command, of authority, of a man who had spent thirty years making life-and-death decisions in seconds. "Now, Iris."

Iris went.

I stayed with Juliet. Held her hand—the one that wasn't clutching the phone—and watched Tom apply pressure to the wound, his big hands steady, his jaw set, his blue eyes focused with an intensity that burned through everything else. In this moment, Tom Rafferty was not a man haunted by guilt or secrets or the ghosts of people he hadn't saved. He was a rescuer. Pure. Simple. Doing the one thing he knew how to do better than anything else.

Juliet's eyes opened.

They found mine. And in them, I saw something I didn't expect.

Not pain. Not fear.

Recognition.

She knew something. She had found something—upstairs, in the dark, in the part of the house she'd gone to while the rest of us slept. She had found something that someone didn't want her to find. And she had been stabbed for it.

Her lips moved. I leaned close. Her breath was warm and irregular against my ear.

One word. Barely a whisper. More shape than sound.

"Bram."

Then her eyes closed, and her hand went limp in mine, and the phone slipped from her fingers and clattered to the glass floor, its screen cracked, its secrets locked behind a password I didn't know.

CHAPTER 27

TOM — NOW

The human body contains approximately five liters of blood.

Tom knew this the way he knew the flash point of diesel fuel or the load-bearing capacity of a two-by-six stud—not as abstract knowledge but as operational fact, the kind of information that meant the difference between saving a life and watching one end. Five liters. Ten and a half pints. Roughly eight percent of total body weight. Lose fifteen percent and you get dizzy. Lose thirty percent and you go into shock. Lose forty percent and you die.

Juliet Marsh was losing blood.

Not catastrophically—the wound was small, the blade thin, and whatever had punctured her had missed the major vessels. Tom could tell by the color: dark red, almost maroon, venous rather than arterial. Arterial blood was bright, urgent, pulsing with the heart's rhythm. Venous blood was slower, steadier, more patient. You could survive venous bleeding if someone applied pressure and kept applying it and didn't panic and didn't stop.

Tom did not panic. Tom did not stop.

He knelt on the glass floor of the bedroom corridor with his hands pressed against Juliet's left side, a folded towel beneath his palms serving as a compress. The blood had soaked through the first towel in ninety seconds. Elliot had brought a second. Tom replaced it without lifting the pressure—a technique he'd learned in his second year on the job, during a warehouse fire that had dropped a beam on a twenty-year-old apprentice electrician and opened his femoral artery like a zipper.

That man had lived. Tom had saved him.

Claire Davenport had not lived. Tom had not saved her.

He pushed the thought away. Filed it in the locked room in his mind where he kept the failures, the losses, the faces of the people he'd been too late for or too far from or too weak to reach. That room was crowded. It had been crowded for years. But the door still held, and as long as it held, he could function.

"Talk to me, Juliet," he said. His voice was steady—command voice, the tone that cut through chaos and panic and the animal noise of crisis. "Open your eyes. Stay with me."

Her eyelids fluttered. Her lips were pale—too pale, the color retreating, the body pulling blood inward to protect the vital organs. Compensatory shock. Stage one. He had minutes before stage two, when the blood pressure would drop and the heart rate would spike and the body's defenses would start to fail.

"Nora," Tom said without looking up. "The phone she was holding. Is there a signal?"

Nora picked up the cracked phone from the floor. The screen lit up—dimly, the display damaged—and showed a single bar of satellite signal, flickering in and out.

"Satellite phone," Nora said. "It's locked. Four-digit passcode."

"Try her birthday."

"I don't know her birthday."

"Try anything. We need to call out."

Iris appeared at the top of the staircase from the foundation level, slightly breathless. "The marine radio is on emergency channel sixteen. I've sent a mayday. Coast Guard Rockland is responding, but they can't launch a helicopter in this wind. They're estimating four to six hours before the storm drops enough for air evacuation."

"She doesn't have four to six hours," Tom said.

"She might. If the bleeding is controlled—"

"It's controlled. For now. But I can't assess internal damage. If the blade nicked her spleen, she could be bleeding internally. She could be compensating right now and crashing in an hour."

"What do you need?"

"A hospital. A surgeon. Things I can't get on this island." He adjusted his hands on the compress. The bleeding had slowed—a good sign, or a sign that there wasn't much blood left to bleed. He couldn't tell. "Barring that, I need a first aid kit with real supplies. Sutures. Hemostatic agents. Antibiotics. Something better than hotel towels."

"There's a medical kit in the utility room. Bram maintains it."

"Then get Bram."

A beat of silence. Tom looked up. Iris was standing at the top of the stairs, her bare feet on the cold glass, her face caught between the amber emergency lighting below and the darkness above. In that divided light, her expression was unreadable.

"Juliet said his name," Nora said. She was crouched beside Tom, her hand on Juliet's wrist, monitoring the pulse. "Before she passed out. She said 'Bram.' That's all. Just the name."

"That doesn't mean—" Iris began.

"It means she was telling us who stabbed her." Marcus's voice came from behind them. He was standing in the corridor with his back against the wall, his arms crossed, his face the rigid mask of a man holding himself together through sheer force of will. "She named him. Before witnesses. That's a dying declaration—admissible in court, no hearsay objection. She said Bram."

"She's not dying," Tom said sharply. "Nobody's dying. Get the medical kit. Get Bram. And get out of my way."

Iris descended the stairs. For thirty seconds, the corridor was silent except for Juliet's breathing—shallow, rapid, the breathing of a body under siege—and the storm's relentless percussion against the glass walls.

Then, from below, Iris's voice. Calling Bram's name. Once. Twice.

No answer.

A longer silence. Footsteps on stone. A door opening, then closing. Then Iris's voice again, louder this time, with an edge Tom hadn't heard before—the edge of someone whose carefully constructed plan has just veered into territory she hadn't mapped.

"He's gone."

Tom looked at Nora. Nora looked at Marcus. Marcus looked at the dark corridor stretching behind them, the glass walls streaming with rain, the house full of shadows and exits and hiding places.

"What do you mean, gone?" Tom called down.

Iris appeared at the base of the staircase. She was holding a green canvas bag—the medical kit—and her face, for the first time since they'd arrived, showed something other than composure.

Fear.

"His room in the utility area is empty. His coat is gone. His boots are gone." She climbed the stairs and handed the medical kit to Tom. Her hands were not quite steady. "He's left the house."

"In this storm? To go where?"

"I don't know."

"The boat—"

"The dock is destroyed. He can't launch."

"Then he's on the island somewhere. On foot. In a nor'easter." Tom unzipped the medical kit and inventoried its contents with rapid, practiced movements. Gauze pads, adhesive tape, antiseptic wipes, a foil packet of hemostatic powder—QuikClot, military grade—a suture kit still in its sterile wrapping, a vial of injectable lidocaine, a bottle of amoxicillin. Better than he'd hoped. "Nora, I need you to hold pressure while I prep the sutures. Can you do that?"

Nora moved into position. Her hands replaced his on the compress—smaller hands, but steady, and she applied pressure with a firmness that suggested she'd done this before or had an instinct for it.

Tom opened the suture kit. Sterile needle, sterile thread, forceps, scissors. His hands remembered the motions—he'd sutured in the field twice, once on a fellow firefighter whose arm had been torn open by a collapsing roof joist, once on himself, a gash on his thigh from a piece of shrapnel during a gas explosion. Neither time had been elegant. Both times, the person had survived.

"This is going to hurt her," Tom said. "Even with the lidocaine. If she wakes up during the stitching, I need someone to hold her still."

"I'll do it," Marcus said. He stepped forward, uncrossing his arms, rolling up his sleeves. His movements were precise, controlled—the movements of a man channeling his fear into utility. Whatever else Marcus Cole was—whatever he'd done, whatever he was hiding—he was not a man who fell apart in a crisis. He was a man who reorganized himself around the crisis and kept moving.

Tom loaded the syringe with lidocaine. "Nora, ease the compress back. Slowly. I need to see the wound."

Nora peeled the towel away. The wound was visible now—a slit, roughly two centimeters long, just below the left costal margin. Clean edges. Minimal tissue damage around the entry point. A thin blade, very sharp, inserted with precision.

Not a frenzied attack. Not a crime of passion. This was surgical. Deliberate. The work of someone who knew where to put a knife to cause maximum internal damage with minimum external evidence.

Tom injected the lidocaine around the wound perimeter. Juliet didn't react—she was deep in unconsciousness now, her body's defenses pulling her down into the dark where pain couldn't reach.

He applied the hemostatic powder to the wound bed. Waited thirty seconds for it to activate. Then, with the steadiness that thirty years of emergency work had burned into his hands, he began to suture.

The needle pierced skin. The thread pulled through. One stitch. Two. Three.

Behind him, Elliot stood at the rain-streaked window, looking out into the storm. Looking for Bram.

"He could be anywhere on the island," Elliot said. "The tree cover would hide him. There are outbuildings—I saw a shed near the dock path, some kind of storage structure on the north side."

"We're not going after him," Tom said. "Not in this weather. Not in the dark."

"He stabbed Juliet."

"Maybe. Or maybe Juliet saw him and assumed. Or maybe someone else did it and Bram ran because he knew he'd be blamed." Tom tied off the fourth stitch. "Right now, my priority is keeping her alive. Everything else comes later."

"And if he comes back? If he comes back with the knife?"

Tom looked up from the wound. Met Elliot's eyes. "Then we deal with it."

"How?"

"The way you deal with any threat. You see it coming, and you don't back down."

He turned back to Juliet. Stitch five. Stitch six. The wound was closing, the edges meeting neatly, the bleeding stopped. The hemostatic powder was doing its job. The sutures were holding.

But the internal damage—the damage he couldn't see, couldn't assess, couldn't reach—was the variable. If the blade had penetrated the peritoneum, if there was a laceration to the spleen or the kidney, Juliet could be bleeding into her abdominal cavity right now. Slowly. Quietly. The compress and sutures holding the outside closed while the inside filled with blood.

He'd know in the next few hours. If her vitals held—pulse, blood pressure, consciousness—she'd make it to the helicopter. If they didn't—

Tom tied off the final stitch. Seven sutures, evenly spaced, each one a small bridge between the edges of the wound. He applied a clean gauze dressing, taped it down, and sat back on his heels.

His hands were covered in blood. Juliet's blood. He looked at them in the amber light—the deep red staining the lines of his palms, the creases of his knuckles—and thought about Claire Davenport's twin sister, whose blood he'd never touched because he'd never reached her, because he'd chosen his daughter instead.

You chose, Juliet had said. And Claire Davenport died.

Yes. He had chosen. And now, here, on this island, in this storm, he was choosing again. Choosing to save the person in front of him instead of chasing the person who'd hurt her. Choosing the immediate over the important. Choosing life over justice.

He hoped it would be enough this time.

NORA — NOW

We arranged her on the mattress in the deepest alcove—the spot she'd claimed earlier, the farthest from the staircase, the most protected. Tom positioned himself beside her, checking her pulse every five minutes, his fingers on her wrist with the gentle, persistent attention of someone who had made a promise and intended to keep it.

The rest of us gathered in the main corridor. Four people and a ghost—because Iris, who had been so commanding, so certain, so relentlessly in control since the moment we'd arrived, had become something else. She sat on the floor with her back against the stone wall, her knees drawn up, her arms wrapped around herself, and stared at nothing.

The mask was slipping.

I recognized it because I wore masks myself—because my entire existence was a mask, and I understood the mechanics of performance the way an engineer understands the mechanics of a bridge. I could see the load-bearing points of Iris's composure, the structural elements that held the persona together: the authority, the clinical detachment, the therapeutic language, the constant, purposeful eye contact. These were the cables that kept the bridge upright. And right now, one by one, they were fraying.

Because this was not part of the plan.

The Mirror Exercise, the confessions, the slow accumulation of pressure—those were planned. The cracked window, maybe planned. The storm, not planned but accommodated. But Juliet bleeding on the glass floor of the bedroom corridor, Bram vanishing into the night, the group fracturing into suspicion and fear—these were deviations. These were the moments when the experiment escaped the laboratory and became something uncontrolled.

"We need to talk about Bram," I said.

Iris looked at me. Her dark eyes were glassy—not with tears, but with the unfocused quality of someone whose attention was divided between the present crisis and some interior landscape that was equally turbulent.

"Yes," she said. Her voice was hoarse. Stripped of its therapeutic warmth. "We do."

"Who is he?" Marcus asked. He was pacing the corridor—four steps one way, four steps back, the containment of a caged animal. "Not what he does. Not his job title. Who is he to you?"

Iris was quiet for a long moment. Then she spoke, and the voice that emerged was not the voice of Dr. Iris Vane. It was rawer, less polished, thickened by an accent she'd been suppressing—Eastern European. Faint, but there. The same accent Bram carried.

"His real name is Branislav Kolar. He's my brother-in-law."

The corridor went silent. Even Marcus stopped pacing.

"Maisie's father—my husband, Andrei—died four years ago. Cancer. Bram was his younger brother. After Andrei died, Bram became—" She paused, searching for the word. "A guardian. Not legally. Emotionally. He was there for me. For Maisie. He loved Maisie like his own child."

She pressed her palms flat against the stone floor. Grounding herself.

"When Maisie died on the ferry, Bram broke. Not the way I broke—I turned inward, I went cold, I became obsessed with finding the truth. Bram turned outward. He became angry. Violent. Not toward people—toward objects. Walls. Furniture. His own hands." She held up her own hands, palms out, as if demonstrating. "He punched a concrete wall so hard he shattered three metacarpals. He broke every piece of furniture in his apartment. He put his fist through the windshield of his truck."

"Comforting," Elliot murmured.

"Bram is not dangerous," Iris said. But the statement lacked conviction—it was a sentence she'd said before, many times, to herself and others, a mantra that had worked until tonight. "He came with me to the island because I needed help—with the house, with the logistics, with the physical aspects of the retreat. And because I couldn't leave him alone. His grief is too large for solitude."

"His grief may have stabbed Juliet Marsh," Marcus said.

"We don't know that."

"She named him."

"She said a name. In shock, in pain, in the dark. She may have been identifying the last person she saw, not the person who hurt her."

"Or she may have been identifying the person who put a knife in her." Marcus stopped pacing and stood directly in front of Iris. "You brought a violent, unstable man to an island with six strangers and didn't tell us. You disarmed us—took our phones, our connections, our ability to call for help. You isolated us. And now one of us is bleeding on a mattress and your brother-in-law has disappeared into a storm with, presumably, the weapon he used to put her there."

Iris met his gaze. The last remnants of the therapeutic mask crumbled, and what emerged beneath it was something harder and more honest—the face of a woman who had stared into an abyss for eighteen months and had not looked away.

"You're right," she said. "About some of it. I brought Bram because I trusted him. I took your phones because I needed you disconnected from the outside world so you couldn't coordinate your lies or contact anyone who might help you maintain them. I isolated you because isolation is the only condition under which truth becomes inevitable. And I didn't tell you about Bram's instability because—" She paused. "Because I didn't think he would act. I thought the investigation—the process—would reach its conclusion before anyone's grief overcame their patience."

"Investigation," Elliot said. "Not therapy. You're finally saying it."

"I've been saying it. You haven't been listening." Iris stood. The motion was stiff, effortful, the movement of a woman whose body was protesting the demands her mind was making. "This was never therapy. I am not Dr. Iris Vane. I used her name, her credentials, her methodology because they gave me access to you. Because none of you would have come to this island at the invitation of a stranger. But you would come for a renowned therapist. You would come for healing."

"Who are you?" Asha's voice, small and tight, from the far end of the corridor. She was sitting against the wall, her sketchpad clutched to her chest, her dark eyes enormous.

"My name is Lena Aldric. I was a forensic accountant. I am Maisie's mother. And I brought you here because one of you helped kill my daughter, and the rest of you know something—saw something, heard something, have some piece of the truth—that will tell me which one."

The stone walls absorbed her words and gave nothing back. No echo. No resonance. Just the flat, final weight of a confession that had been eighteen months in the making.

"You're insane," Marcus said. Not with contempt—with the flat, clinical assessment of a man who recognized strategic error. "You've locked yourself on an island with the person you believe murdered your child. You've created a situation where that person has every reason to feel cornered, every reason to act violently, and no consequences for doing so because there's no law enforcement, no witnesses beyond us, and no way off the island."

"There are witnesses," Lena said. "All of you. And there will be law enforcement—the Coast Guard is coming. When they arrive, they'll find either a confession or enough evidence to compel one."

"Or they'll find bodies," Marcus said.

The word dropped into the corridor like a stone into still water. Bodies. The word that had been circling the edges of every conversation since we'd arrived, the word that lived in the spaces between the careful therapeutic language and the polished performances—the word that named what we were all, on some level, afraid of becoming.

"Nobody else is going to get hurt," Lena said. But even she didn't sound certain.

"Juliet already got hurt," I said. "And Bram is out there. And whoever cracked that window did it before the stabbing, which means someone was planning to compromise the house's safety before Bram had any reason to attack Juliet. Which means—"

I stopped. The thought that had been forming—assembling itself from fragments, from observations, from the small, accumulating details that my mind had been cataloging since the moment I'd stepped onto Bram's boat—clicked into place.

"Which means Bram may not be the one who cracked the window," I said. "And if he didn't crack the window, then there are two threats in this house. Bram and someone else. Someone who's been here the whole time. Someone who came prepared."

The corridor was silent. Six people—seven, counting Juliet, unconscious in the alcove—and one of us was a killer and one of us was a fraud and all of us were trapped, underground, in a house of glass that was breaking, on an island in a storm, with no way off and no way out and the slow, terrible understanding that the truth we'd all been running from was the very thing that had been hunting us.

CHAPTER 29

NORA — NOW

I went upstairs while the others argued.

Not argued—fractured. The revelation of Lena's identity, the disappearance of Bram, the stabbing of Juliet—each one was a stress fracture in the group's cohesion, and now the fractures were connecting, spreading, the structure of trust and cooperation that had tentatively formed over the past thirty-six hours collapsing under the accumulated weight.

Marcus wanted to barricade the foundation level and wait for the Coast Guard. Elliot wanted to search the island for Bram. Tom wanted everyone to shut up and let him focus on keeping Juliet alive. Asha wanted to go home.
