Asha set down her pencil. She placed it on the sketchpad with the care of someone arranging flowers on a grave—parallel to the spine, perfectly centered. Then she closed the pad and held it against her chest.

"You think I stabbed Juliet," she said.

"Did you?" Elliot asked.

"No."

"Your tattoo—"

"I know what my tattoo looks like. I know what Juliet felt. I know what it sounds like." She looked up, and her eyes were not the wide, vulnerable eyes she'd been showing us. They were something else. Harder. Older. The eyes of a woman who had been underestimated her entire life and had learned to use that underestimation as a weapon. "But I didn't stab her. I was in the foundation level all night. I didn't go upstairs."

"Can anyone confirm that?" Tom asked.

"Can you? You were asleep. Nora was in the storage room with Elliot. Marcus was—I don't know where Marcus was. Lena was in her corner. No one was watching anyone. That's the point. No one saw anything, which means no one can confirm or deny anything, which means we're all suspects and none of us are suspects and this entire exercise is—"

She stopped. Drew a breath. When she continued, her voice was lower, more controlled—not the trembling voice of the fragile Asha, but the measured voice of someone who had decided to stop pretending.

"You want to know if I'm capable of it," she said. "That's the real question. Not whether I did it—you can't prove that either way—but whether I'm the kind of person who could stab a woman in the dark. Whether the sweet, broken, grieving twin sister you've all been so eager to protect is actually something else."

No one spoke.

"Yes," Asha said. "I am something else."

She opened her sketchpad. Not to the drawing she'd been making—to a page near the beginning, one she'd done on the first day. The view from the kitchen window: the cliff, the ocean, the sky. The fractured, abstracted landscape with hands reaching from the water and faces half-submerged.

"I told you this was how I process things," she said. "That I can't talk about the ferry, but I can draw it. That was true. But what I didn't tell you is what the drawings mean."

She held up the page. In the amber morning light, filtered through mist and damaged glass, the image was both beautiful and horrifying—the water rendered in jagged, violent strokes, the hands emerging from it like plants growing toward light, the faces caught between surface and depth, between air and water, between life and death.

"This is what I saw," Asha said. "This is my memory of the ferry. Not the version I've been telling you—the panicked twin, the screaming in the dark, the hand reaching for Priya and finding nothing. That happened. All of it happened. But there's more."

She turned the page. The next drawing was different. Not a landscape but a portrait. Two figures, standing close together in a narrow space—a corridor, rendered in tight, claustrophobic lines. One figure was smaller, darker—Priya. The other was taller, broader, with the solid build of an engineer.

Kian.

"I followed Priya down to the lower deck," Asha said. "She went to find Kian. To stop him—or so she told me. But when I got to the engine room corridor, Priya wasn't stopping Kian. She was helping him."

She turned another page. Another drawing: two pairs of hands working on a machine. The machine was rendered in precise, mechanical detail—a small rectangular box bolted to a larger structure, wires connecting them, a single dot of red on the box's face.

The device.

"They were adjusting it," Asha said. "Together. Priya and Kian. Their hands on the device, changing something—settings, dials, I don't know. I'm not an engineer. But I could see that what they were doing was deliberate. Focused. They knew exactly what they were changing and why."

"You're saying Priya modified the device herself," I said.

"I'm saying I saw her hands on it. I'm saying she and Kian were working on it together, in the engine room, in the minutes before the ferry began to sink. I'm saying that whatever the device was supposed to do—controlled failure, catastrophic rupture, whatever—Priya was involved in the final configuration."

"That contradicts what you told us," Elliot said. "You said Priya designed a controlled sabotage and someone else modified it."

"I lied."

The word fell like a stone into the silence.

"I lied because the truth is worse. The truth is that my sister—my twin, my mirror, the other half of myself—was in that engine room with her hands on the device that would kill thirty-one people, including a nine-year-old girl in a blue velvet dress. And she knew. She knew what it would do. The calculations, the simulations—those were real. Priya ran them. And the results told her exactly what would happen: the cooling system would rupture, the engine would explode, the hull would breach, and the ferry would sink in under fifteen minutes."

"And she did it anyway," Tom said.

"She did it anyway. Because Priya was brilliant and reckless and angry and greedy and certain—always certain—that she could control the outcome. She believed the life rafts would deploy in time. She believed the Coast Guard would arrive in time. "But people died," Tom said.

"Thirty-one people died. And Priya died with them. And I survived. And I've spent eighteen months trying to decide whether to tell the truth or to protect her, and I chose to protect her, because she's my sister and I loved her and the truth is unbearable."

She closed the sketchpad. Held it against her chest. Her eyes were dry—she had cried all her tears in the earlier confessions, the performed confessions, the ones designed to elicit sympathy and deflect suspicion. This confession had no tears. It was beyond tears. It was the dry, cracked, exhausted truth of a woman who had finally run out of lies.

"I didn't stab Juliet," she said again. "I burned the purchase order because it showed Priya's involvement. I lied about Priya being coerced because I couldn't bear for people to know what she really was. But I did not attack anyone. I am not a murderer."

She paused.

"My sister was."

CHAPTER 38

LENA — NOW

She had been wrong.

The realization moved through her like a fracture through ice—slowly at first, then all at once, splitting the surface of her certainty into pieces that drifted apart and could not be reassembled.

She had been wrong about the modification. Wrong about the unknown saboteur. Wrong about the premise that had sustained her for eighteen months—the belief that someone had taken Priya Patel's controlled disruption and weaponized it without her knowledge.

Priya had weaponized it herself.

Priya Patel—twenty-seven years old, bright, reckless, desperate—had sat in the engine room of the Blackwater Ferry with her boyfriend and deliberately configured a device to destroy the vessel. Not as an accident. Not as a miscalculation. As a choice.

And Maisie had died because of that choice.

Lena sat in the armchair and felt the grief—the grief she had locked away, the grief she had converted into purpose and fury and the relentless machinery of investigation—break free of its containment and flood through her like the water that had flooded the ferry. Black. Cold. Patient. Total.

Mommy, the water's cold.

She pressed her hands against her eyes. Her breath came in short, controlled bursts—the breathing technique she'd learned in the months after, when the panic attacks came like weather, sudden and uncontrollable, and the only defense was to ride them out.

"Lena." Nora's voice. Close. Careful. "Lena, are you with us?"

She lowered her hands. The room came back into focus—the glass walls, the gray light, the faces watching her. Tom, solid and watchful. Elliot, documenting. Asha, on the floor, her sketchpad clutched to her chest like a shield. And Nora—Elena—the woman who was nobody and everybody, the shapeshifter, the thief, standing two feet away with an expression that might have been concern or might have been calculation.

"I'm here," Lena said.

"We need to know what else you haven't told us," Nora said. "All of it. Every piece you've been holding back. Lena looked at her. Really looked—past the brown hair and the brown eyes and the careful composure, into the machinery beneath. Elena Carr. Identity thief. Professional impostor. A woman who had stolen a dead woman's life and was defending it with the tenacity of someone who had nothing else.

And yet—she was right. The time for strategy was over. The time for controlled revelations and therapeutic exercises and the patient, methodical extraction of confessions was over. The Glass House had cracked. The investigation had spiraled. The neat, rational process Lena had designed—six suspects, three days, systematic pressure, inevitable confession—had been obliterated by a stabbing and a storm and the raw, uncontrollable reality of human desperation.

"There's one more thing," Lena said. "The most important thing. The thing I haven't told anyone."

She stood. The movement was effortful—her body protesting, her muscles stiff from the night spent on the stone floor—but she stood, because what she was about to say needed to be said from a position of authority, or at least the memory of authority.

"On the ferry. In the final minutes. After the explosion, after the hull breached, after the ferry began to break apart. I was in the water, on a piece of debris, and I looked back at the wreck."

She paused. The room was absolutely still—the stillness of held breath, of suspended time, of the moment before a verdict.

"The stern section was the last to go under. It tilted vertical—straight up—and hung there for three seconds before sliding beneath the surface. And in those final seconds, while the stern was still above water, I saw a figure standing at the railing."

"The person with the phone," Nora said. "You told me about them on the Gangplank."

"I told you someone was there. I didn't tell you who."

"Who?"

Lena looked at Nora. Held her gaze. And spoke the name that had been living in her throat for eighteen months, the name she'd carried like a coal on her tongue, burning her every time she swallowed.

"Daniel Finch."

The name detonated.

Not loudly—there was no dramatic gasp, no shouted denial. The detonation was internal. I saw it move through the room like a shockwave, hitting each person differently: Tom's jaw tightening, Elliot's pen freezing mid-stroke, Asha's eyes widening. And Nora—

Nora went white.

Not the gradual paling of shock—the instantaneous, total loss of color that comes with a blow to the solar plexus, the body's response to an impact so fundamental that the cardiovascular system reroutes every drop of blood to the core in preparation for collapse.

"Daniel is dead," Nora said. Her voice was flat. Airless. The voice of a woman whose lungs had just been emptied.

"Daniel Finch was identified through dental records," Lena said. "The body recovered from the wreck site was consistent with his general physical description—height, weight, approximate age. But the face was—damaged. Beyond visual recognition. The dental records were the sole basis for identification."

"Dental records don't lie."

"Dental records can be switched. Altered. Falsified. If someone had access to the dental office where Daniel's records were kept—if someone could substitute another person's records for his—then the body on the ocean floor could be anyone. And Daniel Finch could be alive."

I watched Nora process this. Watched the information travel through her—not just through her mind but through her body, through the carefully constructed identity of Sarah Finch, through the eighteen months of performance and deception and the elaborate architecture of a stolen life.

Because if Daniel was alive, then everything changed.

If Daniel was alive, then the body identified as his was someone else. A stranger. A victim. Someone who had boarded the ferry and died and been claimed as Daniel Finch by a system that didn't look closely enough.

If Daniel was alive, then the life insurance payout—$750,000—had been collected under false pretenses. Not by Nora—she had collected it as Sarah, the grieving widow. But the premise of the collection—that Daniel was dead—was false. Which made the payout fraud. Which made Nora's impersonation of Sarah not just identity theft but wire fraud, insurance fraud, and potentially accessory after the fact to whatever Daniel had done.

If Daniel was alive, then Nora's entire existence was built on a lie inside a lie. She had stolen the identity of a woman whose husband had faked his own death. She was living in the house of a man who might, at any moment, walk back through the door.

And if Daniel was alive—if he had sabotaged the ferry, faked his death, and disappeared—then he was out there somewhere. Watching. Waiting. Possibly even—

I saw the thought hit Nora at the same moment it hit me.

Possibly even here.

"The boat," Nora said. Her voice had changed—no longer flat, no longer airless, but sharp with sudden, urgent clarity. "The boat I saw in the flashback chapter of the NTSB file. The escape boat. The one the figure swam toward after the ferry went down."

"The Meridian," Lena said.

"Meridian Consulting. Daniel's shell company. The boat was named after the company."

"Yes."

"Someone was waiting in that boat. Someone helped the figure aboard. Daniel didn't escape alone—he had an accomplice. Someone who was waiting outside the wreckage field with a boat, ready to pick him up."

"Yes."

"Who?"

Lena shook her head. "I don't know. The boat disappeared into the dark. I was hypothermic, barely conscious. I couldn't see who was driving it."

"But you've been looking. For eighteen months, you've been looking."

"I've been looking for a boat named Meridian registered in Maine or any neighboring state. I've searched maritime registries, Coast Guard databases, harbor master records. Nothing. The boat doesn't exist—or it's been renamed, or it was never registered in the first place."

Tom stepped forward. "Lena. If Daniel Finch is alive—if he escaped the ferry and is out there somewhere—then we have a bigger problem than we thought. Because Daniel was connected to everyone in this room. He was Marcus's intermediary. He was Priya and Kian's contact. He was the man whose shell company bought the device. And if he's alive, he has every reason to want the people who could identify him dead."

"Or silenced," Elliot said. "Or discredited. Or—" He looked at Nora. "Or married."

The room shifted.

Everyone looked at me.

"Nora," Elliot said carefully. "You told us you were Daniel's wife. But you're not—you assumed Sarah's identity after the ferry. So what was your actual relationship with Daniel?"

I stood in the ruined living room of the Glass House, in the gray morning light, with the eyes of five people on me and the weight of every lie I'd ever told pressing down on my shoulders like a hand pushing me toward the floor.

The time for performance was over. The mask—Nora, Sarah, Elena, whoever I was today—had been stripped away, layer by layer, confession by confession, until there was nothing left but the raw, unprotected truth.

"I was his mistress," I said. "For two years. He told me he was leaving Sarah. He told me he was going to start a new life. He told me to meet him on the ferry—that we'd take the crossing together and drive up the coast and disappear."

"But he didn't disappear with you," Lena said.

"No. He sat three rows away from me. He didn't speak to me. He went to the lower deck—I saw him go—and he didn't come back. And then the ferry sank, and Sarah died, and the body they called Daniel was pulled from the ocean, and I was alone."

"So you became Sarah."

"I became the only person who could claim the life he'd promised me. He said he'd take care of me. He said he'd make sure I was safe. And then he died—or I thought he died—and the only way to be safe was to become the woman he was supposed to leave."

"He used you," Lena said. Not with cruelty—with the flat, clinical recognition of someone who had also been used by Daniel Finch. "He put you on the ferry as his alibi. A witness to his death. Someone who would grieve publicly and convincingly and foreclose any investigation into whether he was really dead."

"I didn't know that."

"No. You didn't. Because Daniel was very good at using people without their knowledge. He used Marcus. He used Priya and Kian. He used Vivienne Marsh. He used me." She paused. "He used all of us. And then he disappeared. And left us to destroy each other."

The room absorbed this. The glass walls let in the gray light. The mist pressed against the damaged panels. The ocean, invisible but audible, continued its ancient, indifferent assault on the cliff.

"Then who stabbed Juliet?" Tom asked. The question was blunt, practical, the question of a man who needed to protect someone and couldn't protect anyone until he knew where the danger was coming from.

"I don't know," Lena said.

"Asha says she didn't do it. Bram says he didn't do it. Juliet says the attacker had a small hand and a wrist tattoo. That narrows it down."

"Or widens it," I said. "If Daniel is alive—if he's been watching this weekend—he could have someone on this island. An associate. An accomplice. Someone we don't know about."

"There are only seven of us on this island," Elliot said.

"That we know of."

The thought settled over the room like a cold fog. Seven people, yes—six guests and Bram. But the island was large enough to hide a person. The boathouse, the outbuildings, the dense spruce groves, the rocky northern shore—there were a hundred places where someone could shelter unseen, watching the Glass House, waiting.

Watching through the glass walls.

Seeing everything.

I thought of the cracked windows—both of them, the one on the northeast struck from inside, the one on the west cracked during the storm. What if the northeast window hadn't been cracked by one of us? What if someone from outside had used the Gangplank corridor—the section of the house that jutted out over the cliff, accessible from the exterior—to reach the glass and strike it? Creating a vulnerability. Preparing for the storm. Ensuring that the house would be compromised, that we would be driven underground, that the bedroom corridor would be unoccupied and dark.

Dark enough for someone to enter. Dark enough to find Juliet. Dark enough to put a knife in her side and disappear before anyone could see.

"We need to search the island," I said.

"In this weather?" Marcus had appeared at the top of the foundation staircase. He'd left Juliet resting—her vitals stable, Tom had confirmed, the sutures holding, the bleeding stopped. "The storm isn't over. The mist alone reduces visibility to fifty feet. And if there's someone out there—someone associated with Daniel Finch—walking into those trees is walking into an ambush."

"Staying in this house is walking into a fishbowl," I said. "Anyone outside can see everything we do. Every conversation, every movement, every alliance. The glass walls that were supposed to make us transparent to each other make us transparent to everyone."

"Then we cover the windows," Tom said.

"With what?"

"Sheets. Blankets. Anything opaque. We can't make the house solid, but we can block the sightlines. Make it harder for anyone outside to know where we are and what we're doing."

It was a practical solution. A firefighter's solution—work with what you have, address the immediate threat, buy time. I nodded.

"Do it. Tom, take Elliot. Cover every window you can reach. Start with the bedroom level—that's where we're most vulnerable. Marcus, go back to Juliet. Stay with her. Don't leave her alone."

"And you?" Marcus asked.

"I'm going to the boathouse. Bram should have the radio working by now. We need to contact the mainland. We need help."

"I'll go with you," Lena said.

I looked at her. This woman who had lied to me, manipulated me, studied me, deconstructed my identity with the clinical precision of a forensic accountant auditing a fraudulent return. This woman who had lost her daughter and built a crusade on the loss and brought six strangers to an island to break them open. This woman who was, in her own way, as much a fraud as I was—wearing a dead therapist's name, performing a dead therapist's methods, using transparency as a weapon while hiding more than any of us.

"Fine," I said. "Let's go."

"What about me?" Asha's voice, from the floor. Small again. The vulnerability creeping back, reflexive, automatic—the default setting of a woman who had learned that appearing weak was the most effective form of strength.

"Stay here," I said. "Help Tom with the windows."

"You don't trust me."

"I don't trust anyone. That's not personal. That's survival."

Asha held my gaze for a moment. And in her eyes—in the dark, complicated depths behind the performance—I saw something that surprised me.

Respect.

CHAPTER 39

NORA — NOW

The path to the boathouse was a ruin.

What had been a faint trail through the spruce grove was now a mudslide, the dark earth churned by the storm into a viscous slurry that sucked at our shoes and made each step a negotiation with gravity. The trees were battered—branches stripped, trunks scarred by the wind, the remaining needles wet and dripping, creating a steady percussion that sounded like applause or like rain or like the ticking of a clock counting down to something.

Lena walked beside me. She'd put on shoes—finally, after two days of bare feet, the therapist's affectation abandoned along with the therapist's identity—and a canvas jacket that was too large for her, probably Bram's. Her silver hair was pulled back, her face bare, her expression the focused, interior expression of a woman engaged in rapid, continuous calculation.

"You said Daniel was your lover," she said, as we picked our way through the mud. Not an accusation. A calibration.

"For two years."

"And you believed he was going to leave Sarah."

"Yes."

"Because he told you so."

"Because I wanted to believe it. Which, I now understand, is not the same thing."

She was quiet for a few steps. The spruce grove was dense here, the trees pressing close, their wet branches creating a tunnel that filtered the gray light into something dimmer and greener.

"I liked Daniel," Lena said. "When I met him. At the fundraiser. He was charming—genuinely charming, not performatively. He listened. He asked questions. He made you feel like the most interesting person in the room."

"That was his gift."

"That was his technique. Charm is a tool. It opens doors. It creates trust. It makes people tell you things they shouldn't tell you and do things they wouldn't normally do."

"I know."

"Do you?" She glanced at me. "Because from where I'm standing, it looks like Daniel used his charm to recruit you—not as a partner, not as a lover, but as a piece of his exit strategy. He needed someone on the ferry who would later identify his body. Someone who would grieve publicly. Someone who would make his death feel real."

"I was his mistress. Not his accomplice."

"The distinction may not matter. If Daniel is alive—if he planned the sabotage and faked his death—then everyone who helped him, knowingly or unknowingly, is part of the machinery. You. Marcus. Priya. Kian. Vivienne. Me. All of us, parts of a machine we didn't know we were building."

"You were a part too."

"I was the auditor. I found the problems. I brought them to Marcus. And Marcus—instead of going to the authorities—paid me for my silence and gave himself six months to fix things quietly. Six months he never got. Because Daniel—or someone—accelerated the timeline."

"Why? Why accelerate?"

"Because the controlled version—Priya's version, the engine stall, the stranded ferry, the scandal—wasn't enough. Someone wanted more. More destruction, more deaths, more chaos. The kind of catastrophe that doesn't just damage a company but obliterates it. And from that obliteration—"

"Money," I said. "Insurance. Lawsuits. The financial aftermath."

"Hundreds of millions. Hargrove Holdings carried liability insurance of $200 million. The class-action settlement was $340 million. The individual wrongful death suits added another $90 million. Total exposure: over $600 million."

"And someone positioned themselves to collect."

"Someone, or several someones. If you knew the disaster was coming—if you had advance knowledge of the exact date and time—you could take positions. Short the company's stock. Buy insurance on cargo that would be lost. File early claims. Position yourself as a victim—a survivor—and collect from the settlement pool."

"Is that what Daniel did?"

"I believe so. I've been tracing the financial trail for eighteen months. Through shell companies and offshore accounts and the labyrinth of corporate structures that men like Marcus build to hide money from scrutiny. And the trail leads, again and again, to Meridian Consulting. Daniel's company. A company that, in the six months before the ferry disaster, placed insurance claims on three separate shipments of goods that were supposedly aboard the ferry. Total value: $4.2 million."

"Were the goods actually aboard?"

"No. The goods didn't exist. The shipping manifests were forged. The insurance claims were filed the day after the disaster—before the wreckage had even been searched. Someone at Meridian knew the ferry was going to sink and had prepared the paperwork in advance."

"Daniel."

"Or someone using Daniel's company. But the claims were paid. $4.2 million, disbursed to accounts in the Cayman Islands that I've been unable to trace further. The money disappeared."

"Along with Daniel."

"Along with Daniel."

We emerged from the spruce grove into the clearing. The boathouse stood before us—squat, gray, industrial, its corrugated roof slick with rain. The rolling door was still open, and from inside came the faint glow of the battery lantern and the sound of Bram's voice.

But not Bram alone.

Another voice. Crackling, distorted, filtered through static and distance—the voice of a Coast Guard operator, coming through the repaired radio.

We entered the boathouse. Bram was at the workbench, hunched over the HF transceiver, the handset pressed to his ear. His face, in the lantern light, was drawn tight with concentration.

"—repeat, Calder Island, this is Coast Guard Sector Northern New England. We have your position. Be advised, a secondary weather system is developing offshore. Current forecast calls for sustained winds of forty-five to fifty-five knots beginning at approximately 1400 hours today, with conditions deteriorating through the evening."

Bram looked at us. His eyes were dark wells of controlled despair.

"What does that mean for evacuation?" he asked into the handset.

"Calder Island, be advised, helicopter operations are suspended until weather conditions permit safe flight. Current estimate for next available launch window is—" Static. A burst of noise. Then: "—minimum forty-eight hours. Repeat, minimum forty-eight hours."

Forty-eight hours.

Two more days. Two more nights. On this island, in this house, with a stabbed woman and a missing person and a conspiracy that grew more tangled with each revelation and a possible murderer who might be among us or might be watching from the trees.

"Calder Island, do you have medical emergencies to report?"

Bram looked at me. I nodded.

"Yes. One casualty. Stab wound to the left flank. Sutured on site. Patient is conscious and stable but requires hospital evaluation for internal injuries."

"Copy, Calder Island. We will prioritize your position for first available helicopter. In the meantime, please advise on number of persons on island and nature of the situation."

Bram hesitated. His eyes moved from me to Lena and back.

"Seven persons," he said. "One injured. The situation is—"

He paused. The radio crackled.

"The situation is under control," he said.

I wanted to laugh. Or scream. Or grab the handset and tell the Coast Guard operator the truth—that nothing was under control, that we were trapped in a glass house on a rock in the Atlantic with a web of lies and murder closing around us like a net, that we needed help not in forty-eight hours but now, right now, immediately.

But I didn't. Because the truth—the full truth, the complete and unedited truth of what was happening on Calder Island—was too complex for a radio transmission. It required not a mayday but a novel. A confession. A reckoning.

And it required time.

Forty-eight hours. That was what we had. Forty-eight hours to find the truth before the truth found us.

"Copy, Calder Island. Maintain radio contact on this frequency. Check in every six hours. Coast Guard Sector Northern New England out."

The radio went silent. The static lingered for a moment, then faded, leaving only the sound of the mist dripping from the boathouse roof and the distant crash of waves against the island's northern shore.

Bram set down the handset. He looked at Lena.

"Two more days," he said.

Lena's jaw tightened. I watched her process the information—the timeline extending, the variables multiplying, the plan she'd built with such meticulous precision continuing to unravel.

"Then we use them," she said. "Two more days. We finish this."

"Finish what?" Bram asked. "Lena, someone stabbed a woman in your house. Your investigation—your experiment—it's over. We wait for the Coast Guard. We keep everyone safe. We stop."

"We don't stop. We can't. Because whoever stabbed Juliet is still in that house. Or on this island. And forty-eight hours is a long time to be locked in a cage with a person who has already proven they're willing to use a knife."

"Then what do you propose?"

Lena looked at me. And in her eyes, I saw something I recognized—the look of a woman who had run out of plans and was improvising. The look of a woman who was, for the first time in eighteen months, operating without a script.

It was, oddly, the most honest I had ever seen her.

"I propose we stop lying," she said. "All of us. Every lie, every half-truth, every omission. We put it all on the table. Everything we know. Everything we saw. Everything we've been hiding. And we trust the group—this broken, dysfunctional, traumatized group of people—to do what I couldn't do alone."

"Find the killer," I said.

"Find the truth. The killer is in the truth. If we can get to the truth—all of it—the killer will be standing in it."

I thought about this. About trust—a concept I had spent my entire life avoiding because trust required vulnerability, and vulnerability required identity, and identity was the one thing I had never possessed. I had been Elena Carr, and then I had been a dozen other names, and then I had been Sarah Finch, and then Nora, and through all of those names and faces and performances, the one constant had been the absence of trust. I trusted no one. I relied on no one. I was alone because alone was safe.

But alone was not going to save me here.

"Okay," I said. "We go back. We gather everyone. And we tell the truth."

"All of it?"

"All of it."

CHAPTER 40

ELLIOT — NOW

They were covering the windows when Nora and Lena returned.

Tom had organized the operation with military efficiency—stripping sheets from the bedroom mattresses, cutting them into panels with a kitchen knife, taping them to the inside of the glass walls with the adhesive medical tape from the first aid kit. It was imperfect—the tape didn't hold well on cold glass, and the sheets were too thin to provide true opacity—but it changed the feel of the house. The fishbowl became something more enclosed. More defensible. More human.

Elliot worked alongside Tom, tearing sheets and taping panels, his journalist's hands performing manual labor with the awkward willingness of someone who spent most of his life at a desk. His mind, meanwhile, was racing—processing, connecting, building the narrative framework that would eventually become the story of the Glass House.

Because there would be a story. Elliot was certain of that. Whatever happened on this island—whatever truth emerged from the wreckage of lies and confessions and cracked glass—it would be a story. And he would tell it. Not because he was heartless, or mercenary, or indifferent to the suffering around him. But because telling stories was how he made sense of the world, and the world had never needed making sense of more than it did right now.

The story, as he understood it so far, had the following structure:

Act One: The Conspiracy. A network of people—Priya Patel, Kian Farrow, Daniel Finch, Vivienne Marsh, Marcus Cole, Lena Aldric—each with their own motive, each playing their role. The blackmail. The bribe. The device. The controlled sabotage that was supposed to damage a ferry company and enrich its architects.

Act Two: The Betrayal. Someone within the network modified the plan. Changed the device. Turned disruption into destruction. Thirty-one people died. The conspirators turned on each other—some dying on the ferry, some disappearing, some surviving to carry their secrets into the aftermath.

Act Three: The Reckoning. Lena Aldric, driven by the death of her daughter, assembled the survivors on an island to extract the truth. The Glass House as confessional. Transparency as weapon. But the truth, when it came, was more complex and more terrible than anyone had imagined.

And Act Four—the act that was still being written—was the question of Daniel Finch. Alive or dead. Architect or pawn. Present on this island or a ghost haunting it from a distance.

Elliot taped the last sheet to the living room window and stepped back. The room looked different now—smaller, dimmer, the glass walls hidden behind white cotton, the ocean invisible, the sky invisible, the transparent house transformed into something almost domestic. A room. Not a stage.

"It's better," Tom said. "Not great. But better."

"Any house is better when you can't see out," Asha said. She was standing in the corner of the living room with her arms crossed, watching them work. She hadn't helped—hadn't offered, hadn't been asked. The revelation of her sketchpad drawings and her revised confession had placed her in a category that was neither suspect nor ally but something in between. A witness. An unreliable narrator. A woman who had lied about the most important thing and told the truth about everything else—or who had told the truth about the most important thing and was still lying about everything else.

Elliot didn't know which. And the not-knowing was, he realized, the most dangerous part of all.

Nora and Lena came through the front door. They brought the cold with them—the mist and the salt air and the particular chill of an October morning on an island in the North Atlantic—and with it, news.

"The Coast Guard can't reach us," Nora said. "Second storm system. Forty-eight hours minimum."

The room absorbed this the way the room had been absorbing everything—with the tired, dull resignation of people who had passed beyond surprise into a state of chronic, low-grade dread.

"Juliet?" Nora asked.

"Stable," Tom said. "Sleeping. Her vitals are holding. If the internal bleeding has stopped—and I think it has—she'll make it to the helicopter."

"Good. Then we have forty-eight hours. And we're going to use them." Nora moved to the center of the room—the position Lena had occupied during the therapy sessions, the position of authority, the position that commanded attention. She stood there with the sheets-covered walls behind her and the mist-filtered light above her and the five remaining faces in front of her, and she spoke.

"Here's what we know. The Blackwater Ferry was sabotaged by Priya Patel and Kian Farrow, using a device purchased through Daniel Finch's shell company, Meridian Consulting. The sabotage was originally designed as a controlled engine failure—a disruption that would expose the ferry company's maintenance failures and generate lawsuits. But the device was modified—its specifications changed to cause a catastrophic rupture instead of a controlled stall. Priya may or may not have known about the modification. Asha says she saw Priya and Kian adjusting the device in the engine room before the disaster, which suggests they were aware. Thirty-one people died."

She paused. Let the summary settle.

"The financial architecture of the conspiracy involved Marcus Cole, who facilitated a bribery payment through Daniel Finch; Vivienne Marsh, who brokered the connection between Priya and Daniel; and Lena Aldric, who audited Hargrove Holdings' books and was paid sixty thousand dollars for her silence. Daniel Finch served as intermediary, bagman, and operator of the shell company through which the device was purchased and the insurance fraud was executed."

Another pause.

"Lena believes Daniel Finch is alive. She saw a face she identifies as his on the stern of the sinking ferry, moments before it went under. If she's correct, Daniel faked his death, escaped the ferry in a waiting boat, and has been in hiding for eighteen months—along with $4.2 million in fraudulent insurance claims."

She looked at each of them in turn. Tom. Elliot. Asha. Lena. Marcus, who had come upstairs from the foundation level and was standing in the doorway, his rolled sleeves now spotted with Juliet's blood, his face the color of cold ash.

"What we don't know," Nora said, "is who modified the device. Who turned Priya's sabotage into a massacre. Who killed thirty-one people. And who, last night, stabbed Juliet Marsh."

"Those could be the same person," Elliot said.

"They could be. Or they could be different people with different motives. We don't have enough information to distinguish." She took a breath. "So here's what I propose. We share everything. Not confessions—not the therapeutic version, not the performed version, not the version designed to make us look sympathetic or protect the people we love. The raw information. Every document, every observation, every piece of evidence, every secret. We put it all on the table and we build the picture together."

"You're asking us to trust each other," Marcus said from the doorway.

"I'm asking us to trust the process. The truth is a structure. It has load-bearing walls and connecting beams and a foundation. If we can identify enough structural elements, the rest will become visible. But only if we stop hiding pieces."

"Then the truth will identify them. That's the whole point. You can't hide inside the truth. You can only hide in the gaps between truths. We close the gaps, and whoever is hiding will be exposed."

Silence. The covered windows muffled the wind. The mist pressed against the glass beneath the sheets like a presence, like a breath, like someone standing just outside and listening.

"I'll go first," Marcus said. He stepped into the room. "I've told Nora most of this, but the rest of you need to hear it."

And he began to talk.

CHAPTER 41

THE GROUP — NOW

They talked for three hours.

Not in the structured, controlled format of Lena's therapeutic exercises. Not in the careful, one-at-a-time progression of the Mirror Exercise or the confession session. Not as Sarah. Not as Nora. Not as Elena. As myself—whoever that was, whatever remained when all the masks were removed and all the names were discarded and there was nothing left but a woman sitting in a glass house with five strangers who knew her secret and hadn't yet decided what to do about it.

"And he spent twelve years planning his revenge," Elliot said.

"He rebuilt himself. New firm—small, independent, under the radar. Meridian Consulting. He took clients no one else would take, built a network of contacts in the gray areas of finance, and waited. And when the opportunity came—when Marcus needed someone to deliver a bribe, when Priya needed a contact inside the system, when Vivienne needed a discreet financial intermediary—Daniel was there. Ready. Patient."

"The way the water was patient," I said.

Lena looked at me. And for the first time—the very first time since I'd met her—her eyes held not suspicion, not assessment, not the cold scrutiny of an investigator examining a suspect.

They held recognition.

"Yes," she said. "Exactly like that."

The mist outside the Glass House thickened. The second storm system was building—they could feel it in the air pressure, in the way the wind shifted and strengthened, in the darkening of the sky beyond the sheeted windows. The ocean's voice grew louder, the waves hitting the cliff with increasing force, the rhythm accelerating like a heartbeat under stress.

Forty-eight hours. Maybe more, if the second storm was worse than the first.

Forty-eight hours, sealed in a glass house on a rock in the Atlantic, with the truth finally—partially, imperfectly, painfully—on the table.

And somewhere—on this island or beyond it, alive or dead, present or absent—Daniel Finch.

The architect.

The ghost.

The patient water, still rising.

CHAPTER 42

NORA — NOW

The second storm announced itself with a sound I will never forget.

Not wind. Not rain. Not the groan of glass under pressure or the crash of waves against the cliff. It was a deeper sound—subsonic, felt in the bones before it registered in the ears. A vibration that moved through the Glass House the way a tremor moves through a building before an earthquake. The heated floors hummed. The steel frames of the window panels sang—a thin, metallic whine that rose in pitch as the pressure outside increased. And beneath everything, below the house, below the cliff, below the island itself, the ocean roared.

Not the patient, rhythmic assault of the first storm. This was different. This was fury.

The barometric pressure had been dropping since noon—Bram had reported the readings during his six-hour radio check with the Coast Guard. 985 millibars. 978. 971. Each number lower than the last, each one a rung on a ladder descending into conditions that the Coast Guard operator described, with the understated professionalism of someone accustomed to catastrophe, as "severe."

By two o'clock, the wind was back. Not from the northeast this time—from the northwest, hitting the Glass House on its most exposed face, the side where the Gangplank corridor jutted out over the cliff like an outstretched arm. The arm was shaking. I could see it from the living room—the Gangplank's steel frame vibrating visibly, the glass panels flexing in their mounts, the gap in the northeast wall where the first storm had knocked out a section of glass now admitting a horizontal stream of rain that hit the opposite wall with enough force to splash.

Tom had sealed the gap as best he could—a makeshift barrier of plywood scavenged from the boathouse, held in place with screws and optimism. It bowed inward with each gust, the plywood flexing, the screws groaning in their holes.

"That won't hold," Tom said. He was standing at the entrance to the Gangplank, assessing the barrier with the resigned expression of a man who knows the difference between a repair and a delay. "When the wind tops sixty knots—and it will—that plywood is coming in, and everything behind it is coming in with it."

"Then we seal the Gangplank," I said. "Close the interior door, block it, isolate that section. If it breaches, the damage stays contained."

"The interior door is glass."

"Everything is glass."

"Not the foundation level."

"No. Not the foundation level." I looked at the sheeted windows, the covered walls that had given us the illusion of enclosure for the past six hours. Beyond them, the mist had thickened into something denser—a fog that pressed against the glass like a living thing, reducing visibility to nothing. We couldn't see the ocean. Couldn't see the cliff. Couldn't see anything beyond the immediate perimeter of the house.

Which meant we couldn't see anyone approaching.

"We need to go down," I said. "All of us. Back to the foundation level. And we need to stay there until this passes."

"Agreed," Tom said. "I'll get Juliet."

"I'll get the supplies."

"I'll get the radio."

This last from Elliot, who had been standing at the living room window, his hand pressed against the sheeted glass, feeling the vibration of the storm through the cotton. He turned to me. "The boathouse radio. Bram's long-range transceiver. If we're going to be sealed underground for another night, we need a way to communicate with the outside world. That means someone has to go to the boathouse, disconnect the radio, and bring it back."

"In this weather?"

"The spruce grove provides some shelter. And the boathouse is only a quarter mile. I can make it in ten minutes."

"You'll need someone with you."

"I'll go," Tom said. He'd already moved to the staircase, his body shifting into operational mode—the mode that overrode fear and doubt and hesitation with the simple, powerful imperative of do something. "We'll get the radio and come straight back. Twenty minutes. Maybe less."

"And if the storm gets worse while you're out there?"

"Then we shelter in the boathouse until it passes and come back after."

I didn't like it. The splitting of the group went against every instinct I had—an instinct honed by years of watching for danger, of keeping potential threats in my line of sight, of never, ever letting someone I didn't trust out of my view.

But the radio was essential. Without it, we were not just isolated—we were silenced. And silence, on this island, was more dangerous than any storm.

"Go," I said. "Twenty minutes. If you're not back, I'm coming after you."

Tom nodded. He descended to the foundation level, checked Juliet's vitals one more time—pulse steady, breathing even, the sutures holding—and then returned. He and Elliot put on the heaviest coats available, zipped them to the chin, and went out through the front door into the fog and the wind and the roaring, invisible ocean.

The door slammed shut behind them.

The house shuddered.

And I stood in the sheeted, dimming living room of the Glass House with Marcus, Asha, and Lena—three people I didn't trust, one of whom might be a murderer—and listened to the storm close in around us like a fist.

CHAPTER 43

NORA — NOW

We moved to the foundation level.

The descent felt different this time. The first evacuation—the night before, when Tom had found the cracked window and Iris had still been Iris and the stabbing of Juliet had not yet happened—had been urgent but orderly. An inconvenience. A precaution. This time, it felt like retreat. Like surrender. Like the last move in a chess game where every other option has been eliminated and the only thing left is to protect the king.

The foundation level was as we'd left it—mattresses on the stone floor, emergency lanterns casting amber light, the generator humming its steady, mechanical prayer behind the utility room door. Juliet was in the alcove, sleeping, her color marginally better, her pulse marginally stronger. Marcus took his position beside her—the guardian, the watchman, the man who had discovered that he was capable of tenderness and was still adjusting to the revelation.

Asha settled on her mattress near the corridor wall. She sat cross-legged—always cross-legged, always grounded, always making herself small—and opened her sketchpad. But she didn't draw. She held the pencil above the paper and stared at the blank page as if waiting for it to tell her what to put on it.

Lena went to the storage room. I followed.

The storage room was unchanged—shelves of provisions, canned food, bottled water, the practical infrastructure of survival. But Lena wasn't looking at the shelves. She was looking at the floor.

Specifically, at a section of the concrete floor near the back wall where the surface was slightly different—smoother, newer, the color a shade lighter than the surrounding concrete. A patch. A repair. The kind of cosmetic fix that covers a hole or a seam or—

"A hatch," I said.

Lena looked at me. The look lasted three seconds. Then she knelt and pressed her palm flat against the lighter concrete.

"You knew this was here," I said.

"I built it."

"What's under it?"

She didn't answer immediately. She ran her fingers along the edge of the patch, finding the seam where the new concrete met the old. It was tight—professionally done—but there was a give to it, a slight flex, the kind that indicated the patch was not structural but cosmetic. A cover, not a seal.

She pressed harder. The patch shifted—a centimeter downward, then to the right. A sliding mechanism, hidden beneath the concrete surface. The patch moved aside, revealing a square opening in the floor—roughly two feet by two feet—and below it, darkness.

"A safe room," Lena said. "Built into the bedrock beneath the foundation. I had it constructed when the Glass House was being built. The real Dr. Vane's architectural plans included a root cellar—a traditional feature of island homes, for storing provisions through the winter. I modified the design. Made it deeper. More secure. More—" She paused. "More hidden."

"What's in it?"

"The evidence."

She reached into the opening and pulled a chain. A light clicked on below—a single bulb, bare, casting a stark white glow that illuminated a space roughly six feet by eight feet, carved from the limestone bedrock of Calder Island. The walls were rough, natural stone. The floor was concrete—poured over the bedrock, smooth and level. And against the far wall, on a steel shelf bolted to the rock, was a box.

A fireproof document box. Heavy. Gray. The kind used by banks and law offices to store records that cannot be lost.

Lena descended a short ladder—four rungs, bolted to the wall of the opening—and retrieved the box. She handed it up to me, then climbed back out. The box was heavy—twenty pounds, maybe more—and it was locked with a combination padlock.

Lena spun the dial. 0-9-1-6. The lock clicked open.

"Maisie's birthday," she said. "September sixteenth."

She opened the box.

Inside, in neat stacks secured with rubber bands and labeled with index cards in Lena's angular handwriting, was a collection of documents that made the conspiracy wall in her hidden office look like a rough draft.

Bank statements. Wire transfer records. Corporate filings. Insurance claims. Shipping manifests. Email printouts. Phone records. Surveillance photographs. And at the bottom of the box, in a sealed plastic bag, a USB drive.

"You said the evidence was lost," I said. "On the ferry. In your bag. You said it went down with the ship."

"I lied."

"You lied about the evidence being lost."

"I lied about the evidence being on the ferry. It was never on the ferry. I had copies—backups—stored in a safe deposit box in Portland. After the disaster, I retrieved them. And when I began planning this weekend, I brought them here. To the Glass House. To the safe room beneath the foundation."

"Why did you lie?"

"Because if people believed the evidence was lost, they would behave as if it was. They would believe themselves safe. They would be less careful, less guarded, more likely to reveal the things they were hiding." She paused. "Lies are tools, Nora. You know that better than anyone."

I did know that. I had built my life on that principle. But hearing it from Lena—from a woman who had spent the last two days lecturing us about the necessity of truth, about the architecture of honesty, about the Glass House as a mechanism for transparency—was jarring. It was like hearing a priest admit to atheism. The doctrine was intact. The believer was not.

"What's on the USB drive?"

"Everything. Every document, every photograph, every piece of financial evidence I've gathered in eighteen months. But also—" She reached into the box and extracted a smaller envelope, sealed, unmarked. "This."

She opened the envelope and pulled out a single sheet of paper. It was a printout of an email. Short—three lines. Sent from a ProtonMail account—encrypted, anonymous, untraceable by conventional means.

The email read:

The device has been modified. Original specifications replaced with enhanced parameters. Projected outcome: total structural failure within 12 minutes of activation. Casualties: maximum.

Confirm receipt.

— M

"'M,'" I said.

"One letter. One initial. One clue to the identity of the person who turned Priya's sabotage into a massacre."

"Where did you get this?"

"From Kian Farrow's email account. After the disaster, his accounts were frozen—standard procedure for deceased persons involved in ongoing investigations. But I had access to his credentials. Priya had shared them with Kian, and Kian had shared them with—" She glanced at me. "With a journalist who was investigating the ferry company. Who left them in a notebook that was recovered from the wreck site by NTSB divers."

"Elliot's notebook."

"Elliot's notebook. Waterlogged but legible. The NTSB catalogued its contents as part of the investigation file—the same file I obtained. And in that notebook, among Elliot's notes and interview transcripts and story outlines, were Kian Farrow's email credentials. Username and password. Written in Kian's handwriting, not Elliot's."

"Kian gave Elliot his email access."

"As a dead man's switch. In case something happened to him. In case the information needed to survive even if he didn't." Lena held up the email printout. "This email was sent to Kian's account three days before the ferry crossing. Three days before the device was installed. And the sender—'M'—gave Kian the modified specifications. The specifications that Asha says Priya was implementing in the engine room."

"So Priya received the modified specs from 'M,' through Kian's email."

"Yes."

"And 'M' is the person who designed the massacre."

"Yes."

"Who is 'M'?"

Lena set the email down on the document box. Her hands were steady—the hands of a woman who had been carrying this knowledge for months, who had turned it over and over in her mind, who had examined every possible answer and eliminated all but a few.

"I don't know," she said. "I've considered everyone. Marcus—his first name starts with M. Meridian—the name of Daniel's shell company. Marsh—Vivienne's surname. Every lead circles back to the same problem: the email was sent from an encrypted account that I can't trace. The sender's identity is protected by technology I can't break."

"But you have a theory."

"I have a theory." She looked at me. "The theory is that 'M' stands for a name none of us have considered. A name that isn't in the conspiracy. A name from outside the circle."

"Whose name?"

"I don't know. That's why I brought you all here. That's why I built this weekend. Because I believed that one of you—or several of you—had a piece of information that would identify 'M.' A conversation overheard. A name mentioned in passing. A detail that seemed insignificant at the time but that, in context, would point to the person who sent this email."

She paused. The generator hummed. The storm raged above us, muffled by stone and concrete but present, always present—the soundtrack of our imprisonment.

"But now I think I was wrong," she said. "I think the answer isn't in your memories. I think the answer is in this box. In the documents I've collected. In the financial records and the corporate filings and the phone logs. I think 'M' left a trail—everyone leaves a trail—and I think we can find it if we look."

"Then let's look," I said.

We spread the documents on the floor of the storage room. Stacks and stacks—hundreds of pages, eighteen months of investigation, the raw material of a case that had never been filed, a prosecution that had never been launched, a justice that had never been served.

And we began to read.

CHAPTER 44

ELLIOT — NOW

The spruce grove was a battlefield.

Trees down everywhere—uprooted, snapped, splintered by the wind. The path to the boathouse, already ruined by the first storm, was now a obstacle course of fallen trunks and torn branches and pools of standing water that reflected the gray sky like fragments of a broken mirror.

Elliot and Tom moved fast, bent against the wind, their coats whipping and snapping around them. The fog had lifted slightly—replaced by driving rain that came horizontally, stinging their faces, filling their eyes, turning the world into a blur of green and gray and the dark, wet brown of exposed earth.

Tom led. His stride was long and certain, his footing sure despite the treacherous ground—the body memory of a man who had spent decades navigating unstable terrain, burning buildings, collapsed structures. Elliot followed, his journalist's body protesting the pace, his lungs burning with the cold, wet air.

They didn't speak. The wind made conversation impossible—it tore words from their mouths and scattered them before they could be heard. So they moved in silence, each man alone with his thoughts and the storm.

Elliot's thoughts were about "M."

The email Lena had described—the one she'd found in Kian's account—was the key. He was certain of it. Every investigation he'd ever conducted had a key—a single piece of evidence that, once identified and correctly interpreted, unlocked everything else. The email from "M" was that key. Three lines. One initial. One confirmation request.

Confirm receipt.

The phrasing was businesslike. Transactional. Not the language of an ideologue or a zealot—the language of a professional. Someone accustomed to giving instructions and expecting compliance. Someone who operated within hierarchies, within systems, within the structured world of corporate communication.

Someone like Marcus.

Or someone like Daniel.

Or someone they hadn't considered at all.

The boathouse appeared through the rain—its corrugated roof streaming with water, the rolling door closed against the storm. Tom reached it first, hauled the door open, and they ducked inside.

The space was darker than before—the battery lantern had dimmed, its charge running low. The long-range radio sat on the workbench where Bram had left it, its components assembled, its antenna cable coiled neatly beside it. Bram himself was not there—he'd returned to the Glass House an hour ago, his task complete, his role in the weekend's drama reduced to that of a supporting player waiting in the wings.

Tom disconnected the radio from its power source—a marine battery, heavy, the size of a breadbox—and began packing it for transport. The transceiver itself was manageable—roughly the size of a shoebox—but the battery and the antenna components added bulk and weight.

"I'll take the battery," Tom said, lifting it with a grunt. "You take the radio and the antenna."

Elliot gathered the components. The radio was warm—residual heat from its operation, the circuits still cooling. He held it against his chest, the metal casing pressing against his sternum, and thought about the last transmission Bram had sent.

The situation is under control.

A lie, of course. But all communications were lies, to some degree—edited, compressed, shaped by the limitations of the medium and the assumptions of the audience. The Coast Guard operator heard "under control" and understood it to mean "not currently in crisis." What it actually meant was "we are in crisis, but we are managing it, and telling you the full truth would take longer than we have and accomplish less than we need."

Elliot understood this instinctively. It was the core principle of journalism—not that the truth should be told, but that it should be told in the way that serves its purpose. Context. Framing. Emphasis. The art of making people see what you need them to see.

He was halfway to the door when he noticed the boat.

Not Bram's boat—the fishing vessel that had brought them to the island, which was presumably still at the damaged dock on the south side. This was a different boat. Visible through the boathouse's seaward opening—the channel cut through the rock that led to the open ocean.

A boat sitting in the channel. Low in the water. Dark hull. No running lights.

Elliot set the radio down on the workbench. Moved to the channel opening. The wind hit him like a wall—cold, wet, violent—but he braced himself against the frame and looked.

The boat was thirty feet, maybe thirty-five. A cabin cruiser. Sleek, dark-hulled, built for speed rather than comfort. It was anchored in the channel—or wedged there, riding the surge, its hull scraping against the rock walls with each wave. The cabin windows were dark. No movement on deck. No sign of anyone aboard.

But on the stern, in letters that were barely visible through the rain and the spray, a name.

MERIDIAN.

Elliot's heart stopped.

"Tom." His voice was barely audible over the storm. "Tom, come here."

Tom set down the battery and joined him at the channel opening. He saw the boat. He saw the name.

"Christ," Tom said.

"It's here. The boat from the ferry night. The escape boat. It's here."

"How long has it been here?"

"I don't know. It could have arrived last night, during the storm. It could have been here since before we arrived."

"Is anyone aboard?"

They stared at the dark cabin. The rain hammered the deck, streaming off the cabin roof in sheets. The boat rocked in the surge, its hull groaning against the rock, its lines straining. No lights. No movement. No sound but the storm and the sea.

"We need to check," Elliot said.

"The hell we do. We need to get back to the house and tell the others."

"Tom—if Daniel Finch is on that boat—if he's been here, on this island, watching us through the glass walls of that house—"

"Then he's dangerous. And two unarmed men on a boat in a storm is not how you handle dangerous." Tom grabbed Elliot's arm. "We go back. We tell Nora. We tell everyone. And then we figure out what to do."

Elliot looked at the boat. The Meridian. Daniel Finch's ghost ship, materialized from the darkness, anchored in the hidden channel of an island where six people—seven, counting Bram—were trapped with the truth of what had happened on the Blackwater Ferry.

The truth that Daniel was alive.

The truth that he was here.

"Okay," Elliot said. "We go back."

They grabbed the radio, the battery, the antenna. They sealed the boathouse door. And they ran—not jogged, not walked, ran—through the battered spruce grove, through the mud and the fallen trees and the horizontal rain, back toward the Glass House.

Back toward the trap.

CHAPTER 45

NORA — NOW

I found it in the financial records.

Not immediately—the documents in Lena's box were dense, technical, the language of corporate finance and insurance law and maritime regulation. But I had been Daniel's lover for two years, and in those two years, I had absorbed his world the way a sponge absorbs water—not through study but through proximity. I had listened to his phone calls, glanced at his laptop screen, heard him talk about clients and deals and the intricate choreography of money as it moved through the systems he'd built.

I knew his vocabulary. I knew his patterns. I knew the way he structured a transaction—the shell companies, the intermediaries, the layered accounts that created distance between the source of money and its destination.

And I recognized, in the documents Lena had collected, a pattern I'd seen before.

The Meridian Consulting insurance claims—the fraudulent ones, the $4.2 million—had been filed through a brokerage firm called Pacific Rim Assurance. PRA. The firm was small, obscure, headquartered in Portland—the kind of brokerage that handled niche marine insurance policies for small shipping companies and fishing fleets. Not the kind of firm that would normally process $4.2 million in claims for a single client.

But PRA had a partner. A silent partner, whose name appeared on the firm's incorporation documents—filed in Delaware, naturally, because Delaware was where secrets went to live.

The partner's name was Martin Hale.

Martin. M.

I stared at the name. It meant nothing to me. I'd never heard it. Never encountered it in Daniel's world, in the fragments of his professional life that I'd absorbed through proximity and osmosis.

"Lena." I held up the incorporation document. "Martin Hale. Pacific Rim Assurance. Does this name mean anything to you?"

Lena took the document. Read it. Her eyes moved across the page with the speed and precision of a woman who had spent twelve years reading financial documents for the IRS, who could extract meaning from a balance sheet the way a radiologist extracts meaning from an X-ray.

"Martin Hale," she repeated. "No. I haven't encountered this name."

"He's a silent partner in the brokerage firm that processed Meridian's insurance claims. The fraudulent ones. The $4.2 million."

"A silent partner." Lena's eyes narrowed. "That means he had an ownership stake in PRA. He would have approved the claims. He would have known they were fraudulent."

"And his initial is M."

The silence between us was electric—charged with the static of a connection being made, a circuit being closed, the bright, dangerous spark of a clue becoming a conclusion.

"We need to find out who Martin Hale is," I said. "Everything about him. His background, his connections, his relationship to Daniel."

"We're on an island without internet access."

"The documents. Your files. Eighteen months of investigation—his name must appear somewhere else. Cross-reference it. Every bank statement, every corporate filing, every phone record."

We spread the documents wider. Lena took the financial records; I took the phone logs and corporate filings. We worked in silence, the only sounds the rustle of paper and the muffled fury of the storm above us.

It took forty minutes.

I found it in a phone log—a record of calls made from Daniel Finch's cell phone in the three months before the ferry disaster. The log was comprehensive—Lena had obtained it through means she didn't specify and I didn't ask about—and it showed every call Daniel had made or received, with numbers, durations, and, where available, names.

The number appeared fourteen times. Fourteen calls, spread over twelve weeks, averaging one every six days. Each call lasted between three and eight minutes. Short. Businesslike. The cadence of regular check-ins—progress reports, status updates, the kind of calls you make when you're managing a project and need to stay synchronized with a partner.

The number was registered to a prepaid phone—a burner—but the carrier records included a location ping for each call. The pings showed the phone was consistently located in one area: the waterfront district of Portland, Maine. Within a two-block radius of Pacific Rim Assurance's office.

"He called Martin Hale fourteen times," I said. "From his personal phone. Three months before the ferry."

"That's reckless," Lena said. "Using a personal phone for something like this."

"Daniel was reckless. He was brilliant and meticulous about financial structures, but careless about personal security. He used the same password for everything. He left documents on his laptop screen. He talked about clients in front of me. He—" I stopped. The memory of Daniel—his carelessness, his confidence, the way he moved through the world as if consequences were for other people—hit me with a force that was almost physical.

I had loved a man who was capable of killing thirty-one people. I had shared a bed with a mass murderer. I had let him inside me—inside my body, inside my life, inside the carefully constructed defenses that I had built against exactly this kind of vulnerability—and he had used that access to position me as his alibi, his cover, his posthumous shield.

The grief I felt was not for Daniel. It was for the version of Daniel I had believed in—the charming, careless, flawed man who loved me and was going to leave his wife and build a new life with me. That Daniel had never existed. He was a character, performed for my benefit, the way I performed Sarah for the benefit of the world.

We were the same, Daniel and I. Liars. Performers. People who built identities like houses and lived in them until they collapsed.

The difference was that his house had killed people. Mine had only killed me.

The front door banged open above us.

Footsteps—heavy, urgent, pounding across the glass floor of the living room. Then voices, distorted by distance and the stone stairwell but unmistakable.

Tom. Elliot. Shouting.

"Nora! Lena! Everyone—get up here! Now!"

I was on my feet before the last word landed. Lena followed. We climbed the spiral staircase—past the bedroom level, past the sheeted windows and the covered walls—and emerged into the living room.

Tom and Elliot were soaked. Drenched. Their coats streaming water, their hair plastered to their skulls, their faces flushed with cold and exertion and something else—something that burned in their eyes like a signal fire.

The radio was on the kitchen counter, its battery beside it, the antenna cable trailing across the floor. They had made it back. They had brought the equipment.

But they had brought something else too.

"There's a boat," Tom said. His chest was heaving, his breath coming in rapid bursts that fogged the cold air. "In the channel by the boathouse. A cabin cruiser. Dark hull. No lights."

"The Meridian," Elliot said.

The name rolled through the room like thunder.

Marcus appeared at the staircase behind us—drawn by the shouting, his face tight with alarm. Asha behind him, her sketchpad clutched to her chest. And behind Asha, moving slowly, one hand pressed against the wall for support—

Juliet.

She was upright. Pale, trembling, her left hand pressed against the dressing on her side, but upright. Conscious. Present.

"Someone want to tell me," she said, her voice thin but carrying the unmistakable edge of a woman who would not be left out of something important, "why everyone is shouting about a boat?"

CHAPTER 46

NORA — NOW

The sheets on the windows fluttered in the drafts that found their way through the damaged panels. The storm hammered the house from the northwest, each gust making the steel frames sing and the glass flex and the entire structure shudder like a living thing in pain.

Tom had the radio set up on the coffee table—the long-range transceiver, connected to the marine battery, the antenna cable running up through the stairwell and out through the gap in the northeast wall where the plywood barrier admitted a thin stream of wind and rain.

The question hung in the air. The storm answered it with a gust that made the Gangplank corridor groan—a deep, metallic sound, like a ship in distress.

"Tom's right," I said. "We call the Coast Guard first. We report everything. Then we secure the house and wait."

"The house isn't securable," Asha said quietly. Everyone looked at her. She was standing at the edge of the group, her sketchpad held against her chest like body armor. "The walls are glass.
